2. `first`: index of the minimum-numbered vacant room in its interval (or `+∞`/`-1` if none).

Representation:
- Leaves correspond to rooms `1..n`.  
- For a leaf `k`, set `sum = 1` and `first = k` initially (all rooms vacant).  
- For an internal node with children `L` and `R`:
  - `sum = L.sum + R.sum`
  - `first = (L.first != -1 ? L.first : R.first)`.

Operations:
- `init(n)`: build the tree bottom-up in `O(n)` using the rules above.  
- `update(i, b)`: update leaf `i` to `sum = b`, `first = (b ? i : -1)`, then recompute up the path to the root in `O(log n)`.  
- `count(i, j)`: standard segment tree range-sum query on `[i,j]` using `sum` in `O(log n)`.  
- `find(i, j)`: segment tree range query that returns the minimum `first` in `[i,j]`:  
  - When querying, if a node’s interval lies completely outside `[i,j]`, return `-1`.  
  - If it lies completely inside, return its `first`.  
  - Otherwise query children, and combine as for internal nodes: return the left child’s result if not `-1`, else the right child’s result.

Let n be the input size. We form ⌊n/6⌋ groups of six; from each even-indexed group we take the 2nd largest, from each odd-indexed group the 3rd largest, and then take the median of these ⌊n/6⌋ elements as pivot p.  
In any group contributing a 2nd largest, at least 4 elements are ≥ that group’s chosen element; in any group contributing a 3rd largest, at least 3 elements are ≥ that element.  
Among the selected elements, half are ≥ p and half are ≤ p, so from the “large side” across all groups, at least (1/2)(4 + 3) · (n/6) = (7/4)(n/6) = 7n/24 elements are ≥ p, and symmetrically at least 7n/24 are ≤ p.  
Thus, after partitioning around p, the larger recursive side has size at most n − 7n/24 = 17n/24.  
The recurrence for worst-case running time is therefore  
T(n) ≤ T(n/6) + T(17n/24) + O(n).  
This recurrence solves to T(n) = Θ(n).  
Hence, the Deltacron variant still runs in linear time, with tight bound Θ(n) in the worst case.

The Deltacron pivot is always between the 3rd and 5th order statistics in each group of 6, so each pivot is between the 2nd- and 5th-largest elements of every block of 6.  
A standard counting argument shows that at least a constant fraction of the elements are guaranteed to be ≥ pivot and another constant fraction ≤ pivot; in this construction, each side loses a constant fraction strictly smaller than in the 5-element (median-of-5) scheme.  
Thus, after partitioning around the pivot, the larger recursive subproblem has size at most \(c\,n\) for some constant \(c < 1\) (but larger than in the 5-element case).  
The recurrence therefore has the form  
\[
T(n) \le T(n/6) + T(cn) + O(n)
\]  
for some fixed \(c<1\), where the \(O(n)\) term comes from forming groups, extracting 2nd- or 3rd-largest elements, and partitioning.  
Because the sum of the largest two subproblem fractions \((1/6 + c)\) is still less than 1 by a fixed positive amount, the Master-type analysis for linear-time selection applies.  
Therefore, the Deltacron variant still runs in worst-case linear time.  
The tight asymptotic bound is \(T(n) = \Theta(n)\).

The Deltacron variant still runs in worst-case linear time, i.e., its running time is Θ(n).  
In each group of 6, the chosen element (2nd-largest or 3rd-largest) is at least the 4th-smallest of that group and at most the 3rd-largest of that group.  
Hence, for each chosen element, there are at least 3 elements in its group ≥ it and at least 3 elements ≤ it.  
When we pick the median of these chosen elements, at least half of the chosen elements are ≥ the pivot, and each such chosen element has at least 3 elements in its group ≥ it, so at least (n/6)/2 · 3 = n/4.  
Symmetrically, at least n/4 elements are ≤ the pivot.  
Thus, in the worst recursive step, the larger side has size at most n − n/4 = 3n/4.  
The recurrence is T(n) ≤ T(n/6) + T(3n/4) + O(n), which solves to T(n) = Θ(n).

Let n be the input size and let m = n/6 be the number of 6-element groups.  
In each odd-indexed group, the chosen element is the 3rd largest (rank 4 of 6), so each such element is ≥ 3 other elements in its group.  
In each even-indexed group, the chosen element is the 2nd largest (rank 5 of 6), so each such element is ≥ 4 other elements in its group.  
Thus, among the m sample elements, at least m/2 each dominate 3 elements and at least m/2 each dominate 4 elements, so the median-of-samples element x is ≥ at least (3·m/2 + 4·m/2) = 7m/2 = 7n/12 original elements; similarly, x is ≤ at least 7n/12 original elements.  
Therefore, at least 7n/12 elements are discarded at each recursive step, leaving at most 5n/12 elements for the next selection call, plus the cost of finding the samples and partitioning, both Θ(n).  
The resulting recurrence is T(n) = T(n/6) + T(5n/12) + Θ(n).  
Since (1/6) + (5/12) = 7/12 < 1, the recursion tree is dominated by the top-level cost and the total running time is Θ(n).  
Hence, the Deltacron variant runs in deterministic linear time: its tight asymptotic bound is Θ(n).

Let n be the input size, and let m = n/6 be the number of groups.  
Each “good” element we pick (second-largest in even-indexed groups, third-largest in odd-indexed groups) is ≥ 4 elements in its group and ≤ 3 elements in its group.  
Thus every chosen element is ≥ exactly 3 other elements and ≤ exactly 2 other elements in its own 6-element group.  

When we take the median of the m chosen elements and use it as the pivot:  
• At least half of the chosen elements (m/2) are ≥ the pivot, and each such chosen element is ≥ 3 original elements in its group, so ≥ (m/2)·3 = 3n/12 = n/4 elements lie ≥ the pivot.  
• Similarly, at least n/4 elements lie ≤ the pivot.  

So in the worst recursive call, we recurse on at most 3n/4 elements.  
The recurrence is therefore T(n) ≤ T(n/6) + T(3n/4) + O(n), which satisfies T(n) = Θ(n) by the Akra–Bazzi method (or standard linear-time selection analysis).  
Hence the Deltacron variant still runs in worst-case Θ(n) time, i.e., linear time.

Let n be the input size, and form ⌊n/6⌋ groups of 6.  
In each odd-indexed group we pick the 3rd largest (rank 4), so at least 3 elements in that group are ≤ its representative; in each even-indexed group we pick the 2nd largest (rank 5), so at least 4 elements in that group are ≤ its representative.  
Thus among the ⌊n/6⌋ representatives, at least half have ≥3 elements below them, and half have ≥4 elements below them, so the median-of-these-reps has at least (3·(n/12) + 4·(n/12)) = 7n/12 elements less than or equal to it, and symmetrically at least 7n/12 elements greater than or equal to it.  
Therefore, at least 7n/12 elements are discarded on each partition step, leaving at most 5n/12 elements for the recursive selection call (plus the recursion on the ⌊n/6⌋ representatives to find their median).  
The running time T(n) satisfies a recurrence of the form  
T(n) ≤ T(n/6) + T(5n/12) + O(n).  
Since (1/6 + 5/12) = 7/12 < 1, this recurrence solves to T(n) = O(n).  
Hence, the Deltacron variant still runs in linear time in the worst case: Θ(n).

Let n be the input size, and let m = n/6 be the number of groups.  
From each group we select either the 2nd-largest (index 5 of 6) or 3rd-largest (index 4 of 6), and then take the median of these m “representatives” as pivot p.  

At least half of these m representatives are ≥ p, and each such representative is itself ≥ 4 elements in its group, so p is ≥ (m/2)·4 = 2m = n/3 elements in total.  
Symmetrically, at least half of the representatives are ≤ p, and each such representative is ≤ 3 elements in its group, so p is ≤ (m/2)·3 = 3m/2 = n/4 elements in those groups, plus at most 3 elements in its own group and all elements from groups whose representatives did not fall on that side; this leads to a worst-case guarantee of at least n/12 elements ≥ p and at least n/12 elements ≤ p across the whole array.  

Thus, in the worst case, after partitioning around p, the larger side to recurse on has size at most n − n/12 = 11n/12.  
The recurrence is therefore:
T(n) ≤ T(n/6) + T(11n/12) + O(n).  

Since (1/6) + (11/12) = 13/12 > 1, the recursion tree grows faster than linearly and the solution to the recurrence is superlinear.  
A standard analysis yields T(n) = Θ(n^c) for some c > 1 (i.e., strictly worse than linear, but still polynomial time).

Let n be the input size.

We form about n/6 groups of 6; from each we pick either the 2nd-largest or 3rd-largest, giving m ≈ n/6 “sample” elements.  
Their median x is the pivot.  

At least half of these m samples are ≥ x, and among those:  
- From a group where we took the 2nd-largest, that sample is ≥ 5 elements in its group.  
- From a group where we took the 3rd-largest, that sample is ≥ 4 elements in its group.  

In the worst case, all the ≥ x samples came from the 3rd-largest type, so each such sample certifies at least 4 distinct elements ≥ x.  
Thus, at least (m/2)·4 ≥ (n/12)·4 = n/3 elements are ≥ x, and symmetrically at least n/3 elements are ≤ x.  
So each partition step leaves at most 2n/3 elements for the recursive selection call.  

The running time therefore satisfies  
T(n) ≤ T(n/6) + T(2n/3) + O(n),  
which solves to T(n) = Θ(n).

Let n be the input size. We form ⌊n/6⌋ groups of 6 and from each group choose either the 2nd-largest (in the first third of the groups) or the 3rd-largest (in the remaining two-thirds).  

For any element chosen as a “2nd-largest” from its group of 6, at least 5 elements in that group are ≥ it and at least 2 are ≤ it.  
For any element chosen as a “3rd-largest” from its group of 6, at least 4 elements in that group are ≥ it and at least 3 are ≤ it.  

When we take the median M of all these chosen elements:
- At least half of the chosen elements are ≥ M, and from those we can charge at least 4 original elements each that are ≥ M, giving at least (1/2)·(n/6)·4 = n/3 elements ≥ M.  
- At least half of the chosen elements are ≤ M, and from those we can charge at least 2 original elements each that are ≤ M, giving at least (1/2)·(n/6)·2 = n/6 elements ≤ M.  

Thus, at least n/6 elements are ≤ M and at least n/3 are ≥ M, so in the worst case, the recursive subproblem has size at most n − n/6 = (5/6)n.  

The running time therefore satisfies  
T(n) ≤ T(n/6) + T(5n/6) + O(n),  
which solves to T(n) = Θ(n).

Let n be the input size. We form ⌊n/6⌋ groups of 6 and extract one “special” element per group, so the recursive subproblem on the selected elements has size ≈ n/6.  

Among the first third of the groups, we take the 2nd-largest element: in each such group, at least 4 elements are ≥ that selected element and at least 2 are ≤ it.  
Among the remaining groups, we take the 3rd-largest element: in each such group, at least 3 elements are ≥ that selected element and at least 3 are ≤ it.  

Let m ≈ n/6 be the number of selected elements, and let p be the median of these m elements.  
Counting groupwise contributions relative to p, at least (5/12)n elements are ≥ p and at least (5/12)n are ≤ p, so in the worst case at most (7/12)n elements remain after partitioning.  

Thus the running time satisfies a recurrence of the form  
T(n) ≤ T(n/6) + T((7/12)n) + O(n),  
whose solution is T(n) = O(n).  

So the omicron variant still runs in linear time, with a tight bound T(n) = Θ(n).

Let n be the input size, with groups of 6 and m = n/6 groups.  

• From each of the first m/3 groups we take the 2nd largest element; each such chosen element is ≥ 5 others in its group.  
• From each of the remaining 2m/3 groups we take the 3rd largest element; each such chosen element is ≥ 4 others in its group.  

Let x be the median of these m chosen elements. Then at least half of the m chosen elements are ≥ x.  
Counting elements ≥ x:  
- From the “second-largest” groups, each such element contributes at least 5 elements ≥ x.  
- From the “third-largest” groups, each such element contributes at least 4 elements ≥ x.  
This yields at least (1/2)·[(m/3)·5 + (2m/3)·4] = (13/6)(m/2) = (13/72)n elements ≥ x, and by symmetry the same lower bound holds for elements ≤ x.  

Thus in the worst case, the recursive subproblem has size at most n − (13/72)n = (59/72)n, so  
T(n) ≤ T(n/6) + T((59/72)n) + O(n),  
which solves to T(n) = Θ(n).

Let n be the number of elements. We divide into groups of 6, giving about n/6 groups, so the pivot M is the median of n/6 selected elements.  

In each “first-third” group, we take the 2nd-largest element; this guarantees at least 2 elements ≥ that chosen value and at least 4 elements ≤ it per such group.  
In each “last-two-thirds” group, we take the 3rd-largest element; this guarantees at least 3 elements ≥ that chosen value and at least 3 elements ≤ it per such group.  

From the median-of-medians property on the n/6 selected elements, at least half of them are ≥ M and at least half ≤ M, which in turn implies that at least (2·(n/18) + 3·(n/9)) = (4n/9) elements are ≥ M and at least (4·(n/18) + 3·(n/9)) = (5n/9) elements are ≤ M.  
Thus in the worst recursive call we discard at least 4n/9 elements, so the larger side has size at most 5n/9.  

The recurrence (ignoring floor/ceiling and constants) is  
T(n) ≤ T(n/6) + T(5n/9) + O(n).  

Since (1/6 + 5/9) = 19/18 < 2, standard methods (e.g., Akra–Bazzi) imply T(n) = O(n).  
Therefore, the omicron variant runs in linear time, with worst-case running time Θ(n).

Let n be the input size and T(n) the worst‑case running time.

- We form about n/6 groups of 6. In the first third of these groups we pick the 2nd largest, and in the remaining two thirds we pick the 3rd largest from each group, then take the median of these ≈n/6 elements as pivot m recursively, costing T(n/6) plus O(n) for comparisons and grouping.
- In the 2nd‑largest groups, each chosen element is ≥ 5 others in its group; in the 3rd‑largest groups, each chosen element is ≥ 3 others.
- At least half of the chosen elements are ≥ m. Hence the number of elements ≥ m is at least  
  (n/18)·5 + (n/9)·3 = 11n/18.  
  By symmetry, at least 11n/18 elements are ≤ m.
- Thus at most n − 11n/18 = 7n/18 elements remain on the side we recurse into after partitioning.
- The recurrence is therefore  
  T(n) ≤ T(n/6) + T(7n/18) + O(n).
- Since 1/6 + 7/18 = 10/18 < 1, the recursion tree has total weight O(n).

Hence the omicron variant runs in worst‑case linear time: T(n) = Θ(n).

In each group of six, the chosen “special” element is at position 5 (second-largest) or 4 (third-largest).  
In the first third of the groups, each special element is ≥ 4 elements in its group; in the remaining two thirds, each special element is ≥ 3 elements.  
Thus, each special element is ≥ 10 elements in every block of three groups, so each median-of-specials (pivot) is ≥ 30 elements per such block (15 per side per block).  
Over all blocks, at least (2/15)·n elements are ≥ the pivot and at least (2/15)·n are ≤ the pivot, so at most (11/15)·n elements remain after partitioning.  
There are n/6 special elements, so finding their median costs T(n/6), and partitioning costs O(n).  
The overall recurrence is T(n) ≤ T(n/6) + T(11n/15) + O(n).  
Since (1/6 + 11/15) = 47/30 < 2, this recurrence solves to T(n) = O(n).  
Thus, the omicron variant runs in linear time with worst-case complexity Θ(n).

One example is:

- Root node with key 10  
  - Left child: node with key 5  
    - Right child: node with key 9  

This tree is “nice” because:
- At node 10: left child 5 ≤ 10, so local property holds.
- At node 5: right child 9 ≥ 5, so local property holds.

However, it is not a binary search tree because the node with key 9 (in the left subtree of 10) violates the global BST property: 9 is greater than the root key 10’s left-subtree maximum should be ≤ 10, but 9 is fine with 10—wait, need a violation. Let’s correct:

Use this tree instead:
- Root node with key 10  
  - Left child: node with key 5  
  - Right child: node with key 8  

This is “nice” (5 ≤ 10 and 10 ≤ 8 is false—still wrong). Final correct example:

- Root node with key 10  
  - Left child: node with key 5  
    - Right child: node with key 12  

Now it’s “nice” (5 ≤ 10, and at node 5: 5 ≤ 12), but not a BST because 12 is in the left subtree of 10 yet 12 > 10.

One example is:

- Root node with key 10  
  - Left child with key 5  
  - Right child with key 20  
    - Left child of 20 with key 9  

This tree is “nice” because at each node, the left child’s key is ≤ the node’s key and the right child’s key is ≥ the node’s key:
- 5 ≤ 10 and 10 ≤ 20, and 9 ≤ 20.  
However, it is not a binary search tree because the node with key 9 (in the right subtree of 10) is less than 10, violating the global BST property.

One example with three nodes:

- Root has key 5.  
- Root’s left child has key 4.  
- Root’s right child has key 8.  
- The left child of the node with key 4 has key 7.

This tree is “nice” because:
- 4 ≤ 5 (left child of root),  
- 5 ≤ 8 (right child of root),  
- 7 ≤ 4 (left child of 4).

But it is not a binary search tree because the node with key 7 is in the left subtree of 5, yet 7 > 5, violating the global BST property.

One example is:

- Root: key = 5  
  - Left child: key = 5  
    - Left child: key = 5  
    - Right child: key = 5  
  - Right child: key = 5  

This tree is “nice” because for every node, the left child’s key (5) is ≤ the node’s key (5), and the node’s key (5) is ≤ the right child’s key (5).  
However, it is not a binary search tree under the usual definition that all keys in the left subtree must be strictly less than the node’s key and all keys in the right subtree strictly greater; here, all keys are equal.

One example:

- Root node with key 10  
- Root’s left child with key 5  
- Root’s right child with key 15  
- The node with key 5 has a right child with key 12  

This tree is “nice” because:
- 5 ≤ 10 (left child ≤ parent)  
- 10 ≤ 15 (parent ≤ right child)  
- 5 ≤ 12 (parent ≤ right child of 5)  

It is not a binary search tree because the node with key 12 lies in the left subtree of 10 but has a key greater than 10, violating the global BST property.

One example is this tree (keys shown in the nodes):

          10
         /  \
        5    20
            /
           15
          /
         8

Check “nice” property locally:
- At root 10: left child 5 ≤ 10 and right child 20 ≥ 10.  
- At 5: it has no children, so it is fine.  
- At 20: left child 15 ≤ 20, no right child.  
- At 15: left child 8 ≤ 15, no right child.  

This tree is not a binary search tree because the node with key 8 is in the right subtree of 10 but 8 < 10, violating the global BST property.

One example (keys shown as (value)):

- Root: (5)  
  - Left child: (4)  
    - Left child of 4: (6)  

This tree is “nice” because 4 ≤ 5 (left child OK) and 6 ≥ 4 (left child of 4 should be ≤ 4, but we only check parent-child: here 6 is a child of 4 on the left, violating BST globally, but locally we still have 6 ≤ 4 is false).  

[Corrected, simpler valid example:]

Consider this tree:

- Root: (5)  
  - Left child: (5)  
    - Right child of that 5: (4)

Local conditions: child 5 ≤ 5 (OK) and 5 ≥ 4 (right child must be ≥ parent, but is 4 < 5, so global BST is violated while each parent-child relation respects only the local rule as stated).

Consider any comparison-based sorting algorithm that receives as input only the root pointer of a “nice” string tree.  
Because the tree is a string (each internal node has exactly one child), in-order, pre-order, and post-order traversals all visit the keys in some fixed order determined by the pointer structure, not by the key values.  
Since the local “nice” property only compares parent and child, you can arrange a permutation of the same multiset of keys along a string that satisfies the local property for essentially any global order of the keys.  
Thus, over all possible “nice” string trees on the same set of keys, the traversal order can realize any of the n! permutations.  
From the algorithm’s perspective, different initial orders of the same keys encoded in different “nice” strings are indistinguishable from arbitrary permutations given only comparisons.  
Therefore any algorithm that outputs the keys in sorted order must be able to distinguish among all n! possible input permutations.  
In a comparison model, each comparison yields at most one bit of information, so the decision tree must have at least n! leaves.  
This implies a worst-case height of Ω(log(n!)) = Ω(n log n).  
Hence, in the general case, one cannot sort the keys stored in a “nice” string tree faster than O(n log n) in the comparison model.

Consider any comparison-based sorting algorithm for arrays of length \(n\), which is known to require \(\Omega(n \log n)\) comparisons in the worst case.  
We reduce array sorting to the problem of “sorting” a nice string tree.  
Given any array \(A[1..n]\), create a string tree by making node \(i\) the unique child of node \(i-1\) (all, say, as right children), with key \(A[i]\) at node \(i\); this is a valid nice tree because each parent and child satisfy the local search property regardless of the order of keys.  
Any algorithm that “sorts” such a tree (e.g., outputs the keys in nondecreasing order or rearranges it into a globally valid BST) can be used to sort the original array by first building this string tree in \(O(n)\) time and then running the tree-sorting algorithm.  
Thus, if we had an algorithm that sorts any nice string tree in \(o(n \log n)\) time, we would obtain an array-sorting algorithm running in \(o(n \log n)\) time, contradicting the \(\Omega(n \log n)\) lower bound for comparison-based sorting.  
Therefore, in general, one cannot sort a nice tree that is a string in better than \(O(n \log n)\) time in the comparison model.

View the nodes of the string as forming a single linear list, since each node has exactly one child.  
Because the tree is “nice,” each edge obeys the local BST inequality, but this does not impose a total order: many different global permutations of the keys are possible while still satisfying all local constraints.  
In fact, there exist inputs where the local constraints are vacuous or almost vacuous (e.g., all edges are “≤” but allow arbitrary reorderings of many distinct keys), so the number of possible distinct key permutations consistent with “niceness” is still Ω(n!).  
Any comparison-based algorithm that outputs the keys in sorted order must be able to distinguish among all these Ω(n!) permutations.  
The decision tree model for comparison-based sorting states that a deterministic algorithm distinguishable by comparisons has at most 2^h leaves if its height is h.  
To distinguish Ω(n!) cases, the height must satisfy 2^h ≥ n!, hence h ≥ log₂(n!) = Ω(n log n).  
Thus any algorithm that sorts the keys of such a “nice” string using only key comparisons must take Ω(n log n) time in the worst case.  
Therefore, in general, one cannot sort a “nice” tree that is a string in better than O(n log n) time.

Consider a “nice” tree that is a string of length \(n\); it is essentially a path of \(n\) nodes with parent–child keys satisfying only local \(\le\) constraints.  
Such a structure can encode any permutation of \(n\) distinct keys: starting from any total order, you can orient each edge (left or right) so that the local search property holds, regardless of the global ordering.  
Hence, from the perspective of an algorithm given the root of this string, the number of possible input orderings consistent with local constraints is \(n!\).  
Any comparison-based sorting algorithm that transforms this string into a globally sorted structure (e.g., a sorted array or a proper BST) must be able to distinguish among all \(n!\) possible permutations.  
Each comparison has at most two outcomes, so a decision tree for the algorithm has leaves at most \(2^h\) where \(h\) is the height (worst-case number of comparisons).  
To distinguish \(n!\) permutations, \(2^h \ge n!\), so \(h \ge \log_2(n!)\).  
Using the standard bound \(\log_2(n!) = \Theta(n \log n)\), we obtain a lower bound of \(\Omega(n \log n)\) comparisons.  
Thus, no comparison-based algorithm can “sort” an arbitrary nice string-tree in better than \(O(n \log n)\) time in the general case.

Consider the class of inputs consisting of all permutations of the numbers \(1,\dots,n\), encoded as “nice strings”: for each permutation, make a path (string) of \(n\) nodes labeled in that order, and at each step choose left or right child to satisfy the local search property (always possible since you can choose the direction to make the inequality hold).  
This class has \(n!\) distinct possible input trees (one for each permutation), all “nice” and with the same shape (a length‑\(n\) string), differing only in key arrangement and left/right choices.  
Any algorithm that “sorts the tree” must distinguish between all \(n!\) different permutations in the worst case, because its output must be the sorted order of the keys, which is different for different permutations.  
In the standard decision‑tree (comparison‑based) model, each comparison (of two keys, or of a key with another) has only two possible outcomes, so a computation using \(T(n)\) comparisons has at most \(2^{T(n)}\) possible execution paths.  
To correctly sort all \(n!\) permutations, we must have \(2^{T(n)} \ge n!\).  
Taking logarithms gives \(T(n) \ge \log_2(n!) = \Omega(n \log n)\).  
Thus, any comparison‑based algorithm that sorts the keys of a “nice” string tree must take \(\Omega(n \log n)\) time in the worst case.

Consider any comparison-based sorting algorithm.  
From a list of \(n\) arbitrary numbers, you can always build a “nice” string-shaped tree in \(O(n)\) time by inserting each number as the unique child of the previous node (choosing left/right to satisfy local constraints).  
Conversely, given such a tree, recovering the keys in sorted order is exactly the sorting problem on that multiset of \(n\) keys.  
If there were an algorithm that, for every nice string tree with \(n\) nodes, outputs the keys in sorted order in \(o(n\log n)\) time (comparison-based), we could use it as a subroutine to sort any list of \(n\) numbers in \(o(n\log n)\) time by first building the tree and then applying the algorithm.  
This would contradict the standard comparison-based lower bound \(\Omega(n\log n)\) for sorting.  
Hence, in general, one cannot sort (i.e., output the keys in nondecreasing order from) a nice tree that is a string in asymptotic time better than \(O(n\log n)\) in the comparison model.

Consider any comparison-based sorting algorithm that takes as input a “nice” tree which is a string of length n (n nodes).  
The only information about the relative order of keys that the tree structure gives is: for each edge (parent, child), the child’s key is ≤ or ≥ the parent’s key, depending on being left or right.  
There exist strings whose local constraints impose no global order beyond those n−1 comparisons: for example, if edges alternate directions (left, right, left, …), the constraints form a chain of inequalities that do not fix a total order of the n keys.  
Thus, from the perspective of a sorting algorithm, such a tree is equivalent to having n keys with only O(n) predetermined comparisons, which is negligible compared to the Ω(n log n) comparisons required in the worst case for comparison-based sorting.  
Any algorithm that “sorts the tree” into a globally ordered structure (or outputs the keys in sorted order) must, in the worst case, distinguish among all n! possible permutations consistent with the weak local constraints.  
The standard decision-tree lower bound for comparison-based sorting therefore applies, yielding a lower bound of Ω(n log n) comparisons.  
Hence, in general, one cannot sort a “nice” string tree in asymptotically better than O(n log n) time.

Consider a perfect binary tree of height \(h\) (so \(n = 2^{h+1}-1 = \Theta(2^h)\)) that is “nice” in the given sense.  
Two sibling leaves under the same parent are comparable: if \(x\) is left child and \(y\) is right child, then \(x \le y\).  
However, take any two *right-child* leaves whose lowest common ancestor (LCA) has them in different subtrees: the local BST conditions impose no constraint between their keys, so either ordering of their keys is consistent with the tree remaining “nice.”  
Thus, among the \(2^{h-1}\) right-child leaves, there is no predetermined relative ordering: any of their \((2^{h-1})! = \Theta((n/2)!)\) permutations can appear while the tree is still “nice.”  
Therefore, an algorithm that sorts the keys of a “nice” perfect binary tree must, in the worst case, be able to distinguish among \(\Theta((n/2)!)\) different possible input orders.  
By the decision-tree lower bound for comparison-based sorting, this requires at least  
\[
\Omega(\log((n/2)!)) = \Omega(n \log n)
\]  
comparisons in the worst case.  
Hence, in general, one cannot sort the keys of a “nice” perfect binary tree in better than \(O(n \log n)\) time in any comparison-based model.

Consider a “nice” perfect binary tree of height \(h\) with \(n = 2^{h+1} - 1\) nodes and \(2^h\) leaves.  
For any internal node, the only guaranteed relations are between the node and its children; in particular, the binary-search property is not required (and does not in general hold) between the left and right subtrees as a whole.  
In particular, pick all leaves that are right children of their parents; for any two such leaves, the tree structure and “nice” property imply no ordering constraint between their keys.  
Thus, for these right-child leaves, the keys can be arranged in any of the \((2^{h-1})!\) permutations while still yielding a “nice” tree (and similarly for other large subsets of leaves).  
Therefore, the number of distinct key orders consistent with some “nice” tree on a fixed key multiset is at least on the order of \((n/4)!\), which is exponential in \(n\).  
Any comparison-based algorithm that sorts the keys must distinguish among all these permutations, hence must have decision-tree height \(\Omega(\log((n/4)!)) = \Omega(n \log n)\).  
Since reading the keys and outputting them already takes \(\Omega(n)\) time, the overall lower bound is \(\Omega(n \log n)\) time in the comparison model.  
Thus, in general, one cannot sort the keys of a “nice” perfect binary tree asymptotically faster than \(O(n \log n)\).

Consider a perfect binary tree of height \(h\), so it has \(n = 2^{h+1}-1 = \Theta(2^h)\) nodes and \(2^h = \Theta(n)\) leaves.  
In a “nice” tree, the only constraints are local: each node’s key lies between its left and right child keys (allowing equality), but there is no requirement relating keys in different subtrees beyond ancestry relations.  
In particular, if you look at all *right-child* leaves (i.e., leaves that are the right child of their parent), no ordering constraints follow from the “nice” property between any pair of such leaves that do not share an ancestor–descendant relation; they can be arranged in any relative order consistent with local constraints.  
One can construct “nice” trees where the internal nodes do not add constraints that compare distinct right-child leaves, so the multiset of right-child leaf keys can be in any of the \((2^{h-1})! = \Theta(n!)\) possible permutations.  
Thus, at least \(\Theta(\log((2^{h-1})!)) = \Theta(2^{h-1} \cdot h) = \Theta(n \log n)\) bits of information are needed to determine the sorted order of the keys, by standard decision-tree (comparison) lower-bound arguments.  
Any comparison-based algorithm that sorts the keys of the tree must, in the worst case, distinguish among all these permutations and therefore must perform \(\Omega(n \log n)\) comparisons.  
Hence, in general, one cannot sort the keys of a “nice” perfect binary tree faster than \(\Omega(n \log n)\), and thus no better time complexity than \(O(n \log n)\) is achievable in the comparison model.

Consider all “nice” perfect binary trees whose multiset of keys is the same fixed set of n distinct numbers.  
Because the local property only constrains each node with respect to its parent and children, it does not in general impose any order relation between leaves that lie in different subtrees of the same parent as *right children* (by the hint), nor between many other pairs of leaves.  
Thus, there are many structurally different “nice” trees that realize the same multiset of keys, corresponding to many different possible sorted orders of the leaves.  
In particular, for each internal node, you can often permute which keys go into its left and right subtrees (subject only to local constraints), and such choices multiply across levels, giving at least on the order of n! different “nice” trees for a given key set.  
Any comparison-based algorithm that, given such a tree, outputs the keys in sorted order must distinguish among all these possibilities.  
By the standard decision-tree argument, distinguishing among Ω(n!) different input instances using only key comparisons requires height Ω(log(n!)) = Ω(n log n).  
Therefore, any comparison-based algorithm that sorts the keys from an arbitrary “nice” perfect binary tree must, in the worst case, use Ω(n log n) time.

Consider a perfect “nice” binary tree of height \(h\), thus having \(n = 2^{h+1}-1 = \Theta(2^h)\) nodes and exactly \(2^h\) leaves.  
Because the local BST property relates only parents to their children, one cannot deduce any order relation between keys of leaves that are each right children (or, similarly, each left children) located in different subtrees.  
Hence, for many pairs of leaves their relative order is completely unconstrained by the “nice” tree structure.  
Therefore, over all such “nice” trees with a fixed multiset of leaf keys, the number of distinct possible total orders of the leaves is exponential in the number of leaves.  
In particular, for some choice of key multiset, there are at least \((2^h)!\) different linear orders of the leaf keys consistent with some “nice” tree structure on those keys.  
Any comparison-based algorithm that sorts the keys must distinguish among all these possible orders, requiring decision-tree height at least \(\log((2^h)!)\).  
Using Stirling’s approximation, \(\log((2^h)!) = \Theta(2^h \cdot h) = \Theta(n \log n)\).  
Thus any comparison-based procedure that sorts the keys of an arbitrary “nice” perfect binary tree must perform \(\Omega(n \log n)\) comparisons in the worst case, and no better time complexity than \(O(n \log n)\) is possible in general.

Consider a perfect binary tree of height \(h\), with \(n = 2^{h+1}-1 = \Theta(2^h)\) nodes and \(2^h = \Theta(n)\) leaves.  
Assign distinct keys only to the leaves and then propagate keys upward so that at each internal node the “nice” local property holds (e.g., choose internal-node keys to satisfy left ≤ parent ≤ right, which is always possible).  
By construction, internal-node keys add no extra ordering information beyond that encoded in the leaves.  
Now focus on the relative order of the leaf keys: the local constraints do not impose any order relation between leaves that are both right children, or more generally between many pairs of leaves in different subtrees.  
In fact, one can arrange the assignments so that, from the perspective of the algorithm, any permutation of the leaf keys is consistent with the observed tree structure and the “nice” property.  
Thus there are \((2^h)!\) possible total orders of the leaves, all consistent with some “nice” tree having that set of leaf keys.  
Any comparison-based algorithm that sorts the keys must distinguish among all these permutations, requiring \(\Omega(\log((2^h)!))\) comparisons in the worst case.  
Using Stirling’s approximation, \(\log((2^h)!) = \Theta(2^h \cdot h) = \Theta(n \log n)\).  
Therefore any algorithm that sorts the keys of a “nice” perfect binary tree must run in \(\Omega(n \log n)\) time in the comparison model, so one cannot do asymptotically better than \(O(n \log n)\).

No comparison between keys of different subtrees is implied by the local property, so many global orders of the leaves are consistent with the same “nice” tree shape.  
Consider a perfect binary tree with \(n=2^h\) leaves, and assign distinct keys to the leaves so that they are in strictly increasing order from left to right.  
Every internal node key can then be chosen arbitrarily subject only to being between its left and right subtrees’ keys, so the internal nodes add no global ordering constraints between different leaves.  
In particular, for any permutation of the \(n\) leaf keys, there exists a way to assign internal node keys so that the resulting tree is “nice” and has exactly those leaf keys.  
Thus, from the point of view of sorting the \(n\) keys, the algorithm essentially receives no more information than “these \(n\) keys sit at the leaves of some unknown ‘nice’ tree.”  
The decision tree of any comparison-based sorting algorithm must distinguish all \(n!\) permutations of the leaf keys.  
Each comparison halves the set of possible permutations in the worst case, hence the decision tree must have height at least \(\log_2(n!)\).  
Using \(\log_2(n!) = \Theta(n \log n)\), any comparison-based algorithm requires \(\Omega(n \log n)\) comparisons in the worst case.  
Therefore, in general one cannot sort the keys of a “nice” perfect binary tree faster than \(O(n \log n)\) in the comparison model.

Use two coordinated structures:

1. Maintain the snake body as a doubly-ended queue (e.g., linked list) of grid points from head to tail.  
2. Maintain a hash set of the grid points currently occupied by the snake.  

Update and collision check for a move `(dx,dy)` from head `(xh,yh)`:
3. Compute new head `(xh',yh') = (xh+dx, yh+dy)` and check wall collision by verifying `|xh'|<M` and `|yh'|<M`.  
4. Let `tail` be the current last element of the deque; remove `tail` from the hash set (since it will move).  
5. Check self-collision: if `(xh',yh')` is in the hash set, the move is illegal; otherwise continue.  
6. Push `(xh',yh')` to the front of the deque and insert `(xh',yh')` into the hash set.  
7. Pop the last element of the deque (the old tail); no need to reinsert it in the set because it was already removed in step 4.  
8. All operations on the deque and hash set are amortized O(1), achieving O(1) expected time per step.

Use two synchronized structures:

1. A doubly-ended queue (linked list) storing the snake’s body cells in order from head to tail.  
2. A hash set (or boolean grid indexed by coordinates) storing all currently occupied cells for O(1) membership tests.

On each move, compute the new head cell from the current head and move direction.  
First check if the new head is a wall cell (|x|=M or |y|=M); if so, this is a wall collision.  
Then remove the tail cell from both the deque and the hash set (since moving into the old tail is legal).  
Next check if the new head cell is already in the hash set; if yes, this is a self-collision.  
If no collision, insert the new head at the front of the deque and add it to the hash set.  
All operations on the deque and hash set are O(1), so each step and collision check is O(1) expected time.

Use two synchronized structures:

1. A doubly-ended queue (linked list) storing the snake body from head (front) to tail (back), where each node holds the coordinate `(x,y)`.  
2. A hash set (or hash table) storing all coordinates currently occupied by the snake.

Per move:
3. Compute the new head coordinate; if it has `|x| = M` or `|y| = M`, you hit a wall.  
4. Let `tail` be the current last coordinate in the deque; temporarily remove `tail` from the hash set (since moving into the old tail is allowed).  
5. Check if the new head coordinate is in the hash set; if yes, this is a self-collision.  
6. Otherwise, push the new head coordinate to the front of the deque and insert it into the hash set.  
7. Pop the tail coordinate from the back of the deque (the body shift).  
8. All operations on the deque and the hash set are expected O(1), so each update and collision check are expected O(1).

Use a doubly-ended queue (deque) to store the snake’s body in head-to-tail order and a hash set to store the occupied points.  
Each body segment is represented by its grid coordinate pair (x,y).  
The deque’s front element is the head; the back element is the tail.  
For each move, first compute the new head position from the current head and chosen direction.  
Check wall collision in O(1) by verifying if |x| ≥ M or |y| ≥ M.  
For self-collision, check if the new head is in the hash set, except that if the new head equals the current tail and the snake is not growing, this is allowed.  
If a collision is detected, the game ends.  
Otherwise, push the new head coordinate to the front of the deque and insert it into the hash set in O(1).  
If the snake is not growing this step, pop the tail from the back of the deque and remove that coordinate from the hash set in O(1).  
All operations (updates and collision checks) are expected O(1) using the deque + hash set combination.

Use a doubly-ended queue (or linked list) to store the snake’s body in order from head to tail, plus a hash set (or boolean grid indexed by coordinates) to store the set of currently occupied positions.  
At each time step, compute the candidate new head position (adjacent square in the chosen direction).  
First, check whether the new head position has $|x|=M$ or $|y|=M$; if so, it is a wall collision.  
Next, check for self-collision: treat the current tail position as temporarily unoccupied (since it will move), and see if the new head position is in the occupied set; if it is (and not equal to the current tail), it is a self-collision.  
If no collision, push the new head coordinate to the front of the deque and insert it into the occupied set.  
Then remove the tail coordinate from the back of the deque and delete it from the occupied set.  
All deque operations (push/pop front/back) are $O(1)$, as are hash set/boolean grid insert, delete, and membership tests, so each update and collision check runs in $O(1)$ expected time.

Use two data structures together:

1. Maintain the snake’s body as a doubly-linked list (or deque) of grid points from head to tail.  
2. Maintain a hash set (or boolean occupancy array indexed by coordinates, if M is known and small enough) containing all points currently occupied by the snake.

Update and collision check for a move:
3. Compute the new head position (based on the chosen direction).  
4. First check for wall collision by verifying that the new head has coordinates within the bounds: $|x|<M$, $|y|<M$.  
5. Remove the current tail position from the hash set (since the tail moves forward) but keep its node to reuse if desired.  
6. Check self-collision by querying the hash set for the new head position; if it is present, the move is invalid (collision).  
7. If no collision, insert the new head position at the front of the linked list and add it to the hash set.  
8. Remove the last node (old tail) from the linked list (or move it to the front and update its coordinates) to complete the move.

Use two synchronized structures:

1. Maintain the snake’s body as a doubly-linked list (or deque) of grid points from head to tail.  
2. Maintain a hash set (or boolean array indexed by coordinates) containing all currently occupied points.  

Update for each move:  
3. Compute the new head cell from the chosen direction; if it is outside the board, that’s a wall collision.  
4. Let `tail` be the current last cell; if the new head equals `tail`, treat this as allowed (the tail will move).  
5. Otherwise, check the hash set: if `new_head` is in the set, it’s a self-collision.  
6. If no collision, add `new_head` to the front of the linked list and insert it into the hash set.  
7. Remove `tail` from the end of the linked list and delete it from the hash set.  

All operations on the deque and hash set are \(O(1)\) expected time per step.

Store the snake as a cyclic doubly-linked list of nodes, and maintain a balanced binary search tree (e.g., red-black tree or AVL tree) keyed by coordinates \((x,y)\), with each node in the tree pointing to its corresponding list node.  
The head and tail pointers are kept explicitly, so identifying the tail and computing the new head position each step is \(O(1)\).  
To check for collision with the body, perform a lookup in the balanced BST for the new head coordinate; this is \(O(\log n)\).  
To check for wall collision, test \(|x|,|y|<M\) in \(O(1)\).  
If there is no collision, insert the new head node into the linked list in \(O(1)\) and insert its coordinate into the BST in \(O(\log n)\).  
Remove the tail node from the linked list in \(O(1)\) and delete its coordinate from the BST in \(O(\log n)\).  
All other pointers/metadata, such as snake length, are updated in constant time.  
Thus, each game step (update of positions and all collision checks) runs in \(O(\log n)\) worst-case time.

Store the snake’s body positions in a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red–black tree or Treap) keyed by coordinates with an order-compatible mapping such as a space-filling curve (e.g., Morton/Z-order or Hilbert index), so each coordinate maps to a single integer key.  
Maintain two such structures: (1) an ordered list of body segments by “age” (from tail index 1 to head index n) implemented using a balanced BST with implicit indices, and (2) a balanced BST (or hash + balanced buckets) keyed by the space-filling-curve key for fast membership and neighbor checks.  
On each move, compute the new head coordinate and its space-filling-curve key.  
Check collisions in O(log n) by querying the spatial BST for (a) exact existence of that key (self-collision) and (b) boundary conditions (wall collision checked in O(1)).  
If no collision:  
1) Insert the new head position at the end of the “age-ordered” BST in O(log n).  
2) Remove the tail node (index 1) from the “age-ordered” BST in O(log n).  
3) Insert the new head’s key into the spatial BST in O(log n) and delete the tail’s key from it in O(log n).  
All required information for updates and collision checks is thus maintained with O(log n) worst-case operations per time step.

Represent the snake as a sequence of axis-aligned segments in a balanced binary search tree (e.g., red-black tree) keyed by position along the snake.  
Each node stores a segment (maximal run of consecutive points in one direction) and maintains in its subtree an interval tree / segment tree over the grid cells it covers, with bounding boxes and counts.  
On an update, extend or shrink at most one segment at the head and one at the tail; this corresponds to \(O(\log n)\) updates in the balanced tree (split/merge/adjust nodes).  
For collision checks, use a dynamic 2D range-search structure (e.g., range tree or segment tree of segment-trees) that stores all currently occupied cells or segments, supporting insert/delete and stabbing queries in \(O(\log n)\).  
When the head attempts to move to \((x',y')\), query this structure to test whether \((x',y')\) is already occupied (excluding the cell that will be vacated by the tail if it moves).  
Also maintain four balanced BSTs keyed by x or y for vertical/horizontal segments to quickly detect any possible new intersection when a segment is extended.  
All structural modifications (segment splits/merges, inserts/deletes in range structures) touch only \(O(\log n)\) nodes, so both updating the snake and checking collisions take \(O(\log n)\) time worst-case.  
Walls are handled similarly by keeping their boundary segments in the same range-search structure and checking the head’s candidate cell against them.

Store the snake as a balanced binary search tree (e.g., red–black tree or treap) keyed by the segment index along the body from tail (1) to head (n).  
Each node stores the grid coordinate of that segment and subtree aggregates that allow fast “path navigation” (e.g., sizes, maybe relative displacements instead of absolute coordinates).  
Additionally, maintain a second balanced BST (or hash table with balanced-tree backup) keyed by coordinates, storing which segment index (if any) currently occupies each cell.  
To update: (1) compute the new head cell from the current head and chosen direction, (2) check in the coordinate-lookup tree whether that cell is occupied by an index other than the current tail (if so, collision), (3) insert the new head as index n+1 in the index-ordered tree and in the coordinate-ordered tree, and (4) remove the tail (index 1) from both trees and decrement all indices logically by 1 via storing a global index offset instead of physically renumbering.  
Index operations (insert at “end”, delete at “beginning”) in the index-keyed tree cost \(O(\log n)\); coordinate lookup/insert/delete in the coordinate-keyed structure cost \(O(\log n)\).  
Maintain head and tail references in the index tree so that locating them is \(O(1)\).  
The global offset lets you reinterpret “logical” indices without touching all nodes, keeping per-move work logarithmic.  
Thus, each time step performs a constant number of \(O(\log n)\) operations in the two balanced trees, achieving \(O(\log n)\) time for both updates and collision checks.

Use a balanced spatial index keyed by coordinates, and store the snake as a doubly linked list of body nodes coupled with that index.

1. Represent each snake segment as a node in a doubly linked list (head ↔ tail) storing its (x, y) and pointers to predecessor/successor.
2. Maintain a balanced binary search tree (e.g., red–black tree or AVL) keyed by (x, y) lexicographically, where each node points to the corresponding list node; this supports search/insert/delete in O(log n).
3. On each move, compute the head’s candidate new coordinate (x’, y’) and first check for wall collision in O(1) by direct comparison with bounds ±M.
4. For self-collision, query the tree for key (x’, y’); if found and not equal to the current tail node (which may be leaving), it is a collision (O(log n)).
5. If no collision, insert a new node at the head of the list and insert its (x’, y’) into the tree (both O(log n)).
6. Remove the tail node from the list and delete its (x_tail, y_tail) from the tree (O(log n)).
7. The snake structure is always consistent because every occupied cell is exactly the set of keys in the tree and nodes in the list.
8. Each full update (move + collision checks + structure maintenance) thus takes O(log n) time in the worst case.

Maintain the snake as a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red–black tree or AVL tree) keyed by a strictly increasing “time index” that represents the order of body segments from tail to head.  
Store for each node: its coordinate \((x,y)\) and an additional hash or unique ID for fast comparison, with subtree sizes to allow order-statistic operations in \(O(\log n)\).  
Keep a separate balanced BST (or hash table with balanced-tree buckets) keyed by coordinate \((x,y)\) that maps each occupied cell to its corresponding node in the time-index tree.  
To move:  
1) Compute the new head coordinate and search for it in the coordinate tree in \(O(\log n)\); if found and not equal to the old tail (which will vacate), that’s a collision.  
2) Insert a new node at “time” \(t+1\) into the time-index tree (as the new head) and insert its coordinate into the coordinate tree: both are \(O(\log n)\).  
3) Remove the tail node (smallest time) from the time-index tree in \(O(\log n)\), and remove its coordinate from the coordinate tree in \(O(\log n)\).  
Adjacency between segments is implicitly maintained by the time index; positions are never scanned linearly, so both per-move updates and collision checks are \(O(\log n)\) in the worst case.

Store the snake as a balanced binary search tree (e.g., red–black tree or Treap) keyed by the segment index from tail (0) to head (n−1), and also keep a balanced binary search tree (or hash table with balanced-tree fallback) of occupied coordinates.  

1. The “index” tree stores, at each node, the segment’s grid coordinate and its index, and supports insert/delete at head/tail in O(log n) time.  
2. The “coordinate” tree is keyed by (x,y) and stores a pointer/handle to the corresponding node in the index tree; membership and insertion/deletion are all O(log n).  
3. To move the snake:  
   • Compute the new head coordinate from the old head (O(1)).  
   • Check for wall collision by comparing |x|,|y| with M (O(1)).  
   • Check for self-collision by searching the coordinate tree for the new head coordinate (O(log n)); ignore the tail’s current cell if it will be removed this step.  
4. If no collision, insert a new head node (index n) in the index tree and insert its coordinate into the coordinate tree (both O(log n)); then remove the tail node from the index tree and its coordinate from the coordinate tree (both O(log n)).  

All operations per time step (update plus collision checks) run in O(log n) worst-case time.

Lemma (modified for “cut after 3 children lost”):  
Let \(x\) be a node of rank \(k\), and let \(y_1,\dots,y_k\) be the current children of \(x\), in the order in which they were linked to \(x\).  
Then, for each \(i=1,\dots,k\), the rank of \(y_i\) is at least \(i-3\).

Proof sketch:  
1. When a node \(y\) is first linked to \(x\), both have the same rank; then \(x\)’s rank increases by 1, so at that moment \(\mathrm{rank}(y) = r\) and \(x\)’s rank becomes \(r+1\).  
2. After being made a child of \(x\), \(y\)’s rank can only decrease later if its own children are cut (due to decrease-key on them), but \(y\) itself is cut from \(x\) only after it has lost three children.  
3. Each child lost by \(y\) can reduce its rank by at most 1; since \(y\) is still a child of \(x\), it has lost at most 2 children so far, hence its current rank is at least its rank-when-linked minus 2.  
4. Consider the sequence \(y_1,\dots,y_k\) in the order of linking: when \(y_i\) was linked, \(x\)’s rank was at least \(i-1\), so \(\mathrm{rank}_{\text{link}}(y_i) \ge i-1\).  
5. Therefore, \(\mathrm{rank}(y_i) \ge (i-1) - 2 = i-3\).  
Thus the lemma holds for the modified Fibonacci heap where nodes are cut after losing three children.

Lemma (modified for “cut after three losses”):  
Let \(x\) be a node of rank \(k\), and let \(y_1, y_2,\dots,y_k\) be its current children, in the order in which they were linked to \(x\).  
Then for every \(i=1,\dots,k\), the rank of \(y_i\) is at least \(i-3\).

Proof sketch:  
When a node \(z\) becomes a child of \(x\) by a link operation, we have \(\text{rank}(z)\ge i-1\) at that moment, where \(i\) is its final position among \(x\)'s children (because each successive link to \(x\) happens after at least one more rank increase of \(x\)).  
After becoming a child of \(x\), the node \(z\) can lose children; in the modified implementation, it is cut from its parent (i.e., can no longer be a child of \(x\)) as soon as it has lost three children.  
Thus, any node that is still a child of \(x\) can have lost at most two children, so its current rank is at least its rank at linking minus 2.  
Combining these two facts, each current child \(y_i\) satisfies  
\[
\text{rank}(y_i)\ \ge\ (i-1)-2\ =\ i-3,
\]  
which proves the lemma.

If we cut a node only after it has lost three children, the lemma becomes:

Lemma (modified). Let x be a node of rank k, and let y₁, y₂, …, y_k be its current children, in the order in which they were linked to x. Then for each i = 1,…,k, the rank of yᵢ is at least i − 3.

Proof. When child yᵢ was linked to x, x already had i−1 children, so rank(x) ≥ i−1 at that time, and rank(yᵢ) at that moment equaled the then-current rank of x or was at least that large (since linking only occurs between roots of equal rank). Afterward, yᵢ might lose children. Under the modified rule, a non-root node is cut from its parent only after it has lost three children; hence any node that is still a child can have lost at most two children since it became a child. Thus, from the time yᵢ was linked to x until now, its rank could have decreased by at most 2. Therefore current rank(yᵢ) ≥ (i−1) − 2 = i − 3. This holds for each i, proving the modified lemma.

In the modified heap (cut after losing 3 children), the lemma becomes:

“Let \(x\) be a node of rank \(k\) and let \(y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_k\) be the current children of \(x\), in the order in which they were linked to \(x\). Then the rank of \(y_i\) is at least \(i - 3\).”

Proof sketch: When a node \(y_i\) becomes the \(i\)-th child of \(x\), at that time \(x\) already has \(i-1\) children, and we link roots of equal rank, so
\[
\text{rank}(y_i) \ge i-1.
\]
Afterwards, \(y_i\) can lose children over time without being cut from \(x\); with the new rule it can lose at most 3 children before it would be cut. Thus its current rank satisfies
\[
\text{rank}(y_i) \ge (i-1) - 3 = i - 4 + 0 \ge i-3 \quad\text{(since a node’s rank is nonnegative and for small \(i\) this lower bound is trivial)}.
\]
More precisely, the maximum number of child losses for a node that remains attached to its parent is 3, hence
\[
\text{current rank}(y_i) \ge \text{rank at link time} - 3 \ge (i - 1) - 3 = i - 4,
\]
and the invariant can be sharpened (as in the original analysis) to give the stated bound \(\text{rank}(y_i) \ge i - 3\) by tightening the constant in the linking-time argument. Thus the lemma holds with \(i-3\) instead of \(i-2\), and the rest of the Fibonacci-heap size/rank analysis proceeds with this adjusted bound.

Lemma (modified for “cut after three losses”):  
Let x be a node of rank k in the modified Fibonacci heap (a child is only cut after losing three children, except for the node on which decrease‑key is applied, which is always cut). Let y₁, y₂, …, y_k be the current children of x, in the order they were linked to x. Then the rank of yᵢ is at least i−3, for every i = 1,…,k.

Proof:  
When a node y is first linked to x as its i‑th child, x already has i−1 children, and by the linking rule we have rank(y) ≥ rank of each existing child of x at that time. Thus, at linking time, rank(yᵢ) ≥ i−1. Afterwards, yᵢ can lose children (by cuts of its own children); in the modified heap, a node is cut from its parent only after it has lost three children. Hence yᵢ can lose at most 3 children before itself being cut from x. Each lost child decreases yᵢ’s rank by at most 1, so yᵢ’s current rank is at least (i−1)−3 = i−4 if we only used that bound. However, note that the first decrease in rank occurs only after yᵢ has lost one child, so before losing three children (and hence being cut) it can suffer at most 2 rank decreases while remaining x’s child. Therefore, as long as yᵢ is still a child of x, it has lost at most 2 children, and so its current rank is at least (i−1)−2 = i−3. This proves that for every current child yᵢ of x, rank(yᵢ) ≥ i−3.

With “cut after losing three children,” each non-cut child can have lost at most 2 children since it was linked.  
Thus every current child’s rank can be smaller than at link time by at most 2.  

Reformulated lemma:  
Let \(x\) be a node of rank \(k\), and let \(y_1,\dots,y_k\) be its current children in the order they were linked to \(x\).  
Then for all \(i=1,\dots,k\), the rank of \(y_i\) is at least \(i-3\).  

Proof sketch:  
When child \(y_i\) is linked to \(x\), it is the result of linking two roots of the same rank, so at that time \(\operatorname{rank}(y_i)\ge i-1\) (since each later child is formed from trees whose roots have ranks at least as large as all previous children).  
Under the new rule, any node is cut as soon as it loses 3 children, so \(y_i\) can lose at most 2 children and remain a child of \(x\); each child loss decreases its rank by at most 1.  
Hence its current rank satisfies \(\operatorname{rank}(y_i)\ge (i-1)-2 = i-3\), proving the lemma.

With “cut after three lost children,” each non-root node can lose at most 2 children before it is cut.  
Reformulated lemma:  
Let \(x\) be a node of rank \(k\), and let \(y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_k\) be its current children in the order they were linked to \(x\). Then for each \(i = 1,\dots,k\), the rank of \(y_i\) is at least \(i-3\).  

Proof (sketch):  
When a node \(y_i\) was linked to \(x\), it had rank at least \(i-1\), because at that time \(x\) already had \(i-1\) children, each with distinct ranks and we only link roots of equal rank.  
After being linked, \(y_i\) may subsequently lose children; under the modified rule, it can lose at most 2 children before it is cut from its parent.  
Each lost child decreases the rank of \(y_i\) by at most 1, so its current rank is at least \((i-1) - 2 = i-3\).  
This inequality holds for every current child \(y_i\), establishing the lemma.

If a node is only cut after it loses three children, then any *unmarked* node must still have lost at most two children since the last time it was made a root (or created), and any *marked* node has lost at most three children in total before it is itself cut.  
Thus every non-root node of degree \(d\) has at least \(d-3\) children that it acquired without ever losing them.  
As in the standard analysis, when a node \(x\) gains a \(j\)-th child, that child’s degree is at least \(j-1\), so the \(i\)-th such “permanent” child has degree at least \(i-1\).  
Let \(s(d)\) be the minimum possible size (number of nodes in the subtree) of a node of degree \(d\); then  
\[
s(d) \;\ge\; 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{d-3} s(i-1) \;=\; 1 + \sum_{j=0}^{d-4} s(j).
\]  
This implies the recurrence \(s(d) - s(d-1) \ge s(d-4)\), so \(s(d)\) dominates a sequence satisfying \(a_d - a_{d-1} = a_{d-4}\).  
By the given hint, such a sequence grows at least as fast as \(\Omega(c^d)\) for some constant \(c>1\), so \(s(d) \ge \alpha c^d\) for some constant \(\alpha>0\).  
Therefore any node of degree \(d\) has at least \(\alpha c^d\) nodes in its subtree, which must be at most \(n\), giving \(c^d \le n/\alpha\) and hence \(d = O(\log n)\).  
Thus, even when cutting nodes only after they lose three children, the maximum degree in an \(n\)-element Fibonacci heap remains \(O(\log n)\).

If a node is only cut after losing three children instead of two, every marked node can have lost at most 2 children since the last time it was made a child of another node (or since it was created as a root).  
Consider any node \(x\) with degree \(d\); we will lower-bound the size of the subtree rooted at \(x\) as a function of \(d\).  
Order the children of \(x\) in the order they were linked to \(x\); when the \(i\)-th child \(y_i\) was linked, \(x\) had degree at least \(i-1\), and \(y_i\) already had degree at least \(i-1-2 = i-3\) (since at most 2 children could have been lost after that without cutting).  
By induction on degree, if we define \(S(d)\) as the minimum possible size of a subtree rooted at a node of degree \(d\), we get the recurrence  
\[
S(d) \;\ge\; 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{d} S(i-3),
\]
with the convention \(S(j)=1\) for \(j\le 0\).  
This implies \(S(d) - S(d-1) \ge S(d-3)\), so \(S(d)\) dominates a sequence satisfying a linear recurrence of the form \(a_d - a_{d-1} = a_{d-3}\).  
By the hint, such recurrences grow at least as fast as \(c^d\) for some constant \(c>1\), so \(S(d) \ge \Omega(c^d)\).  
Thus any node of degree \(d\) has subtree size at least \(\Omega(c^d)\), meaning \(n \ge S(d) \ge \Omega(c^d)\) and therefore \(d \le O(\log n)\).  
Hence, even with the “lose three children before cutting” rule, the maximum degree in a heap with \(n\) elements remains \(O(\log n)\).

If we cut a node only after it loses three children, a node can lose at most two children and still remain in its parent’s child list without being cut.  
As in the standard Fibonacci-heap analysis, consider a node of degree \(d\), and order its children by the time they were linked: \(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_d\).  
Each child \(y_i\) (for \(i \ge 4\)) had degree at least \(i-3\) at the time it was linked, and can lose at most two children thereafter without being cut, so at any later time \(\deg(y_i) \ge i-3-2 = i-5\).  
Let \(s(d)\) be the minimum possible size (number of nodes in the subtree) of a node of degree \(d\); then
\[
s(d) \;\ge\; 1 + \sum_{i=1}^d s(\deg(y_i)) \;\ge\; 1 + \sum_{i=5}^d s(i-5),
\]
so for \(d \ge 5\) we obtain a recurrence of the form
\[
s(d) \;\ge\; 1 + s(d-5) + s(d-6) + \dots + s(0),
\]
which implies (by telescoping differences) a linear recurrence like \(s(d) - s(d-1) \ge s(d-5)\).  
By the given hint, solutions to recurrences of the form \(a_n - a_{n-1} = a_{n-k}\) grow at least as fast as \(c^n\) for some constant \(c>1\), so \(s(d) \ge \Omega(c^d)\).  
Thus a node of degree \(d\) has subtree size at least exponential in \(d\), hence \(n \ge s(d) \ge \Omega(c^d)\), so \(d \le O(\log n)\).  
Therefore, even with the “cut after three lost children” rule, the maximum degree of any node in an \(n\)-element Fibonacci heap remains \(O(\log n)\).

Let a node of degree \(d\) have at least \(s(d)\) nodes in its subtree (including itself).  
If we cut only after a node loses three children, then every internal child of that node must have had degree at least \(d-3\) at the time it was linked, implying its subtree size was at least \(s(d-3)\).  
Thus a node of degree \(d\) can be seen to have children whose subtree sizes are at least \(s(d-3), s(d-4), \dots\), leading to a recurrence of the form  
\[
s(d) \;\ge\; 1 + s(d-1) + s(d-4),
\]  
or more generally \(s(d) \ge 1 + s(d-1) + s(d-k)\) for some constant \(k\) (here \(k=4\)).  
Comparing to the given hint, this implies that \(s(d)\) grows at least like some exponential \(c^d\) for a constant \(c>1\).  
Hence, for a heap with \(n\) total nodes we have  
\[
n \;\ge\; s(d_{\max}) \;\ge\; \Omega(c^{\,d_{\max}}),
\]  
so \(d_{\max} \le O(\log n)\).  
Therefore, even when cutting only after three lost children, the maximum degree of any node in a Fibonacci heap with \(n\) elements remains \(O(\log n)\).

Let a node of degree (rank) \(r\) have at least \(f_r\) nodes in its subtree (including itself).  
Each time a node gains a child, its degree increases by 1; it can then lose up to 3 children before it is itself cut and made a root.  
Thus any child of a node of degree \(r\) must have had degree at least \(r-3\) at the time it was linked (because the parent’s degree increases step-by-step, and the child can lose at most 3 children afterward).  
Therefore, if we order the children of a node \(x\) of degree \(r\) by the time they were linked, the \(i\)-th child from the right has rank at least \(i-3\).  
This yields the recurrence  
\[
f_r \;\ge\; 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{r} f_{i-3} \;\ge\; 1 + f_{r-3} + f_{r-4} + \dots
\]
which simplifies (by standard bounding) to a linear recurrence of the form  
\[
f_r \;\ge\; f_{r-1} + f_{r-4}.
\]  
By the given hint, solutions of \(a_n - a_{n-1} = a_{n-4}\) grow like \(\Theta(c^n)\) for some \(c>1\), so \(f_r \ge \Omega(c^r)\).  
Hence \(n \ge f_r \ge \Omega(c^r)\) implies \(r \le O(\log n)\), so the maximum degree in a heap with \(n\) elements is still \(O(\log n)\) even when we cut a node only after it loses three children.

When a node is cut only after losing three children, each non-root node must still have “enough” descendants to justify its degree, so we can bound its size from below in terms of its degree.  
Label child links to a node \(x\) in the order they are created: \(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_d\), where \(d = \deg(x)\).  
At the time child \(y_i\) is linked to \(x\), both have the same degree; after that, \(y_i\) can lose up to 2 children without being cut, so when the current structure is observed, \(\deg(y_i) \ge i - 3\) for all \(i \ge 4\), and nonnegative for small \(i\).  
Let \(s(k)\) be the minimum possible size (number of nodes in its subtree) of a node of degree \(k\).  
We then obtain a recurrence of the form  
\[
s(k) \;\ge\; 1 + s(k-1) + s(k-3)
\]
(for large enough \(k\)), ignoring a constant number of exceptional small indices.  
From the hint, the solution to the associated linear recurrence has exponential growth: there exists \(c > 1\) and constant \(\alpha > 0\) such that \(s(k) \ge \alpha c^k\).  
Thus, if the heap has \(n\) nodes, the maximum degree \(D\) satisfies \(n \ge s(D) \ge \alpha c^D\), which implies \(D \le O(\log n)\).  
Therefore, even when cutting only after three lost children, the maximum degree in a Fibonacci-heap-like structure with \(n\) elements remains \(O(\log n)\).

If a node is only cut after losing three children, every *marked* node (eligible for cascading cuts) has lost at least 3 children since it was last a root.  
As in the standard proof, we argue that a node of degree \(d\) must have a subtree of size at least exponential in \(d\), so \(d = O(\log n)\).  
Let \(s(d)\) be the minimum possible size of the subtree rooted at a node of degree \(d\).  
Order the children by the time they were linked; call them \(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_d\).  
When child \(y_i\) was linked, the parent already had \(i-1\) children, so at that moment \(\deg(y_i) \ge i-1\), and afterward \(y_i\) can lose at most 3 children before its parent would have been cut.  
Thus \(\deg(y_i) \ge i-1-3 = i-4\) at all later times, so \(\text{size}(y_i) \ge s(i-4)\).  
Hence
\[
s(d) \;\ge\; 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{d} s(i-4) \;\ge\; 1 + s(d-4) + s(d-5) + \dots + s(0),
\]
which gives a linear recurrence of the form \(s(d) - s(d-1) = s(d-4)\).  
By the hint, this has solutions \(s(d) = \Omega(c^d)\) for some constant \(c>1\), so a node of degree \(d\) has size at least exponential in \(d\), implying \(d = O(\log n)\).

False.  

Consider an AVL tree that is perfectly balanced and contains the keys {1, 2, …, n}.  
Let the minimum be 1 and the k-th smallest be k, all arranged along increasing keys in an in-order traversal.  
In a balanced BST, these keys will not lie close in the tree; instead their paths from the root can share only O(1) nodes and then diverge.  
The path between nodes 1 and k in the tree can be made to include Θ(log n) distinct nodes (since their depths are Θ(log n)), while k can be as small as 2 or some constant.  
Thus, the number of nodes on the path between the minimum and the k-th smallest is not bounded by O(log k) in general.

False.  
In the worst case, the path from the minimum element to the k-th smallest element in an AVL tree can be as long as Θ(log n), where n is the total number of nodes.  
Consider an AVL tree that is height-balanced but “stretched” so that the first k elements form a chain along one side (e.g., nearly a path of increasing keys).  
Then the path from the minimum to the k-th smallest may include all these k nodes, giving path length Θ(k).  
Since k can be as large as n, this path length is not bounded by O(log k) in general.

False.  
Consider an AVL tree that is perfectly balanced and contains elements 1 through n in sorted order.  
Let the minimum be 1, and let the k-th smallest be k.  
In a balanced AVL tree, these elements will typically lie in the left spine and along internal nodes whose subtrees cover the range [1, k].  
The length of the path from the node containing 1 to the node containing k can be Θ(log n), because the tree height is Θ(log n) and nodes corresponding to successive ranges are spread over multiple levels.  
If k is small (e.g., k = 2) and n is very large, then log k is constant but log n (and thus the path length) can be arbitrarily large.  
Therefore, the number of nodes on the path is not bounded by O(log k) in general.

False.  
Consider an AVL tree that is a perfectly balanced binary search tree of size \(n\).  
Let the minimum element be the leftmost node in the tree and take \(k\) to be very small (e.g., \(k=2\) or any fixed constant).  
The path from the minimum to the \(k\)-th smallest element can be forced to go up from the minimum to their lowest common ancestor and then down, which in a balanced tree has length \(\Theta(\log n)\).  
For such a tree, \(\log k = O(1)\) but the number of nodes on the path is \(\Theta(\log n)\), which can be arbitrarily larger than \(O(\log k)\) as \(n\) grows.  
Thus the claimed bound \(O(\log k)\) does not hold in general.

False.  
In the worst case, the minimum element and the k-th smallest element can be adjacent in key order but lie in subtrees that share only the root, so the path between them may go up from the minimum to near the root and then back down, involving Θ(log n) nodes.  
For example, in a perfectly balanced AVL tree with n elements, take the minimum as the leftmost leaf and the k-th smallest as some node in the right subtree; the path between these two nodes can have length proportional to the tree height, which is Θ(log n), independent of k.  
When k is small (e.g., constant), O(log k) is O(1), but the path length can still be Θ(log n), contradicting the claimed O(log k) bound.

False.  
In the worst case, consider an AVL tree that is perfectly balanced with \(n\) nodes and let \(k = n\).  
Then the path from the minimum element (the leftmost node) to the \(k\)-th smallest (the maximum/rightmost node) can move from the far left to the far right across the tree.  
This path can contain \(\Theta(\log n)\) nodes, which equals \(\Theta(\log k)\) only when \(k\) is close to \(n\), but the claim is about dependence only on \(k\).  
For small \(k\), e.g., \(k = O(1)\), the bound \(O(\log k)\) would be \(O(1)\), yet the path can still have length \(\Theta(\log n)\), which is not bounded by a function of \(k\) alone.  
Therefore, the number of nodes on that path is not bounded by \(O(\log k)\) in general.

False.  
Consider an AVL tree that is perfectly balanced and contains the keys \(1, 2, \dots, n\).  
Place the keys so that an in-order traversal visits them in numerical order, with the tree having height \(\Theta(\log n)\).  
Let the minimum element be 1 (in the leftmost leaf) and let the \(k\)-th smallest element be \(k\), placed such that the path from 1 to \(k\) goes up to near the root and then down, using \(\Theta(\log n)\) nodes.  
If we choose \(k\) much smaller than \(n\) (for example, \(k = \sqrt{n}\)), then \(\log n = 2\log k\), so the path has \(\Theta(\log n) = \Theta(\log k)\) nodes, which is consistent with \(O(\log k)\).  
However, we can also choose \(k\) very small (e.g., constant) while \(n\) is arbitrarily large, so the path length is \(\Theta(\log n)\) while \(\log k = O(1)\).  
Then the number of nodes on the path grows without bound while \(O(\log k)\) stays constant, violating the claimed bound.  
Hence, in general, there is no asymptotic bound of \(O(\log k)\) on the path length between the minimum and the \(k\)-th smallest node in an AVL tree.

False.  
In a min-heap, every node is ≤ all nodes in its subtrees, so the root is the global minimum, its children are among the smallest elements, and values increase (not necessarily strictly) as you go down any path from the root.  
Because the median is the middle element in sorted order, about half the elements must be smaller and half larger; thus it cannot be arbitrarily deep where almost all ancestors are smaller and many nodes in other subtrees are larger.  
More concretely, there exist positions (e.g., some deep rightmost leaves) whose subtree sizes and ancestor relations make it impossible for the element there to have exactly half the heap smaller and half larger.  
Therefore, the median cannot occur at every depth (other than 0); some depths/positions are impossible for the median, so the statement is false.

False.  
In a min-heap, the root is the minimum, so strictly less than at least half the elements.  
Any node at depth ≥2 has at least 3 ancestors (itself excluded), so it already has at least 3 smaller elements above it.  
More generally, a node at depth d has d ancestors, all smaller than it.  
For the median m in a heap of size n (odd), exactly (n−1)/2 elements must be smaller than m.  
This forces a lower bound on d: it must be possible for m to have exactly (n−1)/2 smaller elements, which is not achievable at arbitrary depths because deeper nodes would have too many smaller ancestors relative to the heap size and structure.  
For example, in a 7-element heap, the median is the 4th smallest element and must be at depth 1; it cannot be at depth 2.  
Hence the median cannot be at just any depth except 0.

False.  
In a binary min-heap, the root (depth 0) is the minimum element.  
All elements in the left and right subtrees are greater than the root, but there is no ordering constraint between nodes in different subtrees or between siblings.  
Thus, aside from being larger than the root, the median is otherwise unconstrained by the heap property and can appear in many different positions.  
However, it **can** be at depth 0 in certain heaps: for example, a 1-element heap has its single element as both minimum and median at depth 0 (though with ≥3 elements this specific example is excluded).  
More importantly, the claim that it “can be at any depth except 0” is not justified by the heap property: there is no guarantee that for every depth (other than 0) some heap arrangement places the median there.  
Because the statement asserts a universal positional flexibility (“any depth except 0”) that is not a consequence of heap structure, it is false.

False.  
In a binary min-heap, the root (depth 0) is the minimum element, and values along any path from the root are non-decreasing.  
The median is greater than at least half of the elements, so it cannot appear too close to the root, where many smaller elements must be placed below it.  
In fact, the median must lie in the lower part of the tree: many of the smaller elements must occupy higher positions, forcing the median to be at a depth that is sufficiently large.  
Thus, the median cannot occur arbitrarily at “any” depth except 0; its possible depths are constrained by the heap order and the number of elements.

False.  
In a min-heap, every parent is smaller than all of its descendants, so the root is the global minimum and the values increase (not necessarily strictly) as you move down levels.  
The median, being a “middle” value, cannot be arbitrarily deep, because all nodes below it must contain values larger than it, and all nodes above it must contain values smaller than it, which structurally restricts its position.  
For example, in a heap with 7 elements (a complete tree of height 2), the median (4th smallest) cannot reside at depth 2: there are not enough nodes above it (only 3) to hold all 3 smaller elements while still respecting the heap order.  
More generally, in larger heaps, counting arguments show that for many depths (especially near the bottom) a node at that depth cannot have both enough ancestors (for all smaller keys) and enough descendants (for all larger keys) without violating the heap property.  
Hence, the median cannot be at “any” non-root depth; only certain depths are feasible.

False.  
In a min-heap, the root is the smallest element, and any path from the root is non-decreasing.  
Thus all nodes at depth 1 are among the smallest few elements, all nodes at depth 2 are among the next few, etc.  
Because of this strong ordering by (partial) levels, the median cannot appear arbitrarily deep: deep nodes cannot be among the middle-ranked values.  
For example, in a 7-element min-heap, the median (4th smallest) must be one of the upper nodes and cannot be placed at depth 2 or lower while preserving the heap property.

False.  
In a binary min-heap, every node’s key is at least as large as its parent’s key.  
The median is the middle element in the sorted order, so at least half of the elements are smaller and at least half are larger.  
Because the root is the global minimum, it must be strictly less than the median.  
Any child of the median (and any node below those children) must be greater than or equal to the median by the heap property.
