– On each delete-min-approx, compare the roots of \(H\) and \(B\) and delete the smaller; this costs \(O(1)\) if both are present.  
– To maintain the invariant that both heaps (when nonempty) contain one of the two global minima, after each \(O(1)\) operation you move at most a constant number of elements between \(H\) and \(B\) using extract-min/insert operations, whose costs are paid for by the periodic rebuild and amortization over many updates.  
Because each element participates in only a constant number of heap operations (inserting once into \(B\), possibly once into \(H\), and at most a constant number of rebalancing moves), its total handling cost is \(O(1)\) amortized.  
The approximate-min guarantee holds since at all times the two roots are maintained as the two smallest elements, and we always return and delete one of them.  
Hence, for \(k=2\), a sloppy heap with \(O(1)\) amortized time per operation is achievable.

The claim is false: even for \(k = 2\), such a sloppy heap cannot support all operations in \(O(1)\) amortized time in the standard comparison-based model.  
Suppose we had a data structure \(D\) supporting Insert, Delete-2nd-Min (returning and deleting one of the two smallest elements), and Find-2nd-Min (returning one of the two smallest elements) in \(O(1)\) amortized time.  
We can use \(D\) to implement a standard priority queue with \(O(1)\)-amortized-time Extract-Min as follows: always keep at most one item “in reserve” outside \(D\), and whenever we need the true minimum we sometimes call the sloppy extract twice and keep the smaller of the two as the true minimum, reinserting the larger if needed.  
More concretely, by maintaining 2–3 candidates and using constant-time comparisons plus at most a constant number of sloppy-heap operations per real Extract-Min, we can always determine and remove the true minimum from the global set using only \(O(1)\) calls to \(D\).  
This would yield a comparison-based priority queue with insert and extract-min in \(O(1)\) amortized time.  
However, it is known from standard lower bounds (via reductions to sorting) that any comparison-based priority queue which supports Extract-Min and Insert in \(O(1)\) amortized time would allow sorting \(n\) elements in \(O(n)\) time, contradicting the \(\Omega(n \log n)\) lower bound.  
Therefore, the assumed sloppy heap with \(k=2\) and \(O(1)\)-amortized-time operations cannot exist in the comparison-based model.

The claim is true: for \(k=2\) we can achieve \(O(1)\) amortized time per operation.  
Maintain two components:  
1. A constant-sized structure holding the current two smallest elements (call it the “front buffer”).  
2. A standard binary min-heap \(H\) containing all remaining elements.  

The buffer is always kept sorted so that it holds the two smallest keys overall; insertions place the new element either directly into the buffer (possibly evicting its larger element into \(H\)) or into \(H\).  
A delete-min-approx operation returns and deletes the smallest element in the buffer, then refills the buffer (if needed) by extracting the minimum from \(H\) and inserting into the buffer so that it again holds the two smallest items overall.  
Each buffer update is \(O(1)\) time.  
Each element can cause at most one extract-min on the heap and one insertion to/from the buffer, so heap operations are \(O(\log n)\) each but are charged over \(\Theta(\log n)\) ordinary operations, giving an amortized \(O(1)\) per high-level operation.  
Thus, with this buffering scheme for the two smallest elements, a sloppy heap for \(k=2\) with \(O(1)\) amortized time per operation is possible.

The claim is false: even for \(k=2\), you cannot support all operations in \(O(1)\) amortized time in the standard comparison model.  
Suppose you could do so; then you would have a priority queue that always returns one of the two smallest elements, with insert, delete-min (sloppy), and find-min (sloppy) all in \(O(1)\) amortized time.  
Now use this data structure to sort \(n\) distinct keys: insert all \(n\) keys, then repeatedly perform the sloppy delete-min operation until the structure is empty, recording the returned elements.  
Because each returned element is one of the two smallest remaining, at each step you can determine the true next smallest element using only a constant number of extra comparisons (keep track of a candidate and compare with the newly returned element).  
Thus you can stably sort in \(O(n)\) amortized time (since there are \(n\) inserts and \(n\) deletes, each \(O(1)\) amortized).  
However, in the comparison model, any algorithm that sorts \(n\) arbitrary distinct keys requires \(\Omega(n \log n)\) comparisons in the worst case.  
This contradiction shows that such an \(O(1)\)-amortized sloppy heap for \(k=2\) cannot exist.

The claim is true: such a sloppy heap with \(k=2\) and \(O(1)\) amortized time operations can be implemented.  
Maintain two ordinary heaps: \(H_1\) of size at most 2 that always stores the two smallest elements overall, and \(H_2\) that stores all remaining elements arbitrarily large.  
Insert works in \(O(1)\) amortized time by inserting into \(H_2\) and occasionally rebalancing: if a newly inserted element is smaller than one in \(H_1\), swap it in and push the displaced element to \(H_2\); the number of such promotions per element is bounded.  
To support `find-min` (the sloppy minimum), return either of the two elements in \(H_1\) in \(O(1)\) time.  
For delete of the returned element, delete it directly from \(H_1\) in \(O(1)\), and if \(H_1\) drops below size 2, move the minimum element from \(H_2\) into \(H_1\); such transfers are infrequent enough to be charged to previous insertions.  
Formally, by choosing appropriate potential function (e.g., proportional to |\(H_2\)| or number of elements needing rebalancing), the cost of heap operations in \(H_2\) is amortized over many constant-time operations.  
Because each element can cause only a constant number of expensive moves between heaps, total cost over \(n\) operations is \(O(n)\), giving \(O(1)\) amortized time per operation.

Use three n×n arrays (or matrices):

1. **Board[n][n]** – stores the value at each cell, or 0/NULL if empty.  
2. **RowHas[n][n+1]** – RowHas[i][x] = true iff row i already contains number x.  
3. **ColHas[n][n+1]** – ColHas[j][x] = true iff column j already contains number x.

Initialization: set all Board entries to 0 and all RowHas/ColHas entries to false in O(n²) time and space.

Operation **Write(i,j,number)**:  
• If RowHas[i][number] or ColHas[j][number] is true, report illegal move (do nothing).  
• Otherwise set Board[i][j] = number; RowHas[i][number] = true; ColHas[j][number] = true. (All O(1).)

Operation **Contains(i,j)**:  
• Return Board[i][j] (0/NULL means empty). (O(1).)

Operation **Erase(i,j)**:  
• Let x = Board[i][j]; set Board[i][j] = 0; RowHas[i][x] = false; ColHas[j][x] = false. (O(1).)

Use three n×n arrays:

1. `board[i][j]` – stores the number at cell (i, j), or 0/NULL if empty.  
2. `rowHas[i][k]` – boolean: whether number k is present in row i.  
3. `colHas[j][k]` – boolean: whether number k is present in column j.

Initialization: allocate all three arrays and set all entries to 0; time and space are Θ(n²).

Operations:  
• `Write(i, j, x)`: if `rowHas[i][x]` or `colHas[j][x]` is true, report illegal; else set `board[i][j] = x`, `rowHas[i][x] = true`, `colHas[j][x] = true`.  
• `Contains(i, j)`: return `board[i][j]` (0/NULL means empty).  
• `Erase(i, j)`: let `x = board[i][j]`; set `board[i][j] = 0`, `rowHas[i][x] = false`, `colHas[j][x] = false`.

All lookups and updates are direct array accesses, so every operation runs in worst-case O(1) time.

Use three \(n \times n\) arrays (or equivalent 2D structures):

1. `Board[1..n][1..n]`: stores the number at each cell, or 0/NULL if empty.  
2. `RowUsed[1..n][1..n]`: boolean, where `RowUsed[i][k]` is true iff number `k` is already used in row `i`.  
3. `ColUsed[1..n][1..n]`: boolean, where `ColUsed[j][k]` is true iff number `k` is already used in column `j`.  

Initialization: set all entries of `Board` to 0 and all entries of `RowUsed` and `ColUsed` to false in \(O(n^2)\) time and space.  
`Write(i,j,number)`: if `RowUsed[i][number]` or `ColUsed[j][number]` is true, report illegal; otherwise set `Board[i][j]=number`, `RowUsed[i][number]=true`, `ColUsed[j][number]=true`.  
`Contains(i,j)`: return `Board[i][j]` (0/NULL means no element).  
`Erase(i,j)`: let `x = Board[i][j]`; then set `Board[i][j]=0`, `RowUsed[i][x]=false`, and `ColUsed[j][x]=false`.  
All operations use only direct array accesses, so they run in worst-case \(O(1)\) time.

Store three n×n integer arrays:

1. `board[i][j]`: the number at (i, j), or 0 if empty.  
2. `rowUsed[i][num]`: 1 if number `num` already appears in row i, else 0.  
3. `colUsed[j][num]`: 1 if number `num` already appears in column j, else 0.  

Initialization: allocate all three arrays of size n×n and fill with 0 (O(n²) time and space).  

`Write(i, j, num)`:  
- If `rowUsed[i][num] == 1` or `colUsed[j][num] == 1`, report illegal move.  
- Else set `board[i][j] = num`, `rowUsed[i][num] = 1`, `colUsed[j][num] = 1`.  

`Contains(i, j)`: return `board[i][j]` (0 means empty).  

`Erase(i, j)`: let `num = board[i][j]`, set `board[i][j] = 0`, `rowUsed[i][num] = 0`, `colUsed[j][num] = 0`.

Use three n×n arrays:

1. `board[i][j]` stores the number at cell (i, j), or 0 if empty.  
2. `rowHas[i][k]` is Boolean: true if number k appears in row i.  
3. `colHas[j][k]` is Boolean: true if number k appears in column j.

Initialization: allocate all three arrays and set all entries to 0/false in O(n²) time and space.  

`Write(i, j, number)` (assume 1-based indices and number ∈ [1..n]):  
- If `rowHas[i][number]` or `colHas[j][number]` is true, report “illegal move” and do nothing.  
- Else set `board[i][j] = number`, `rowHas[i][number] = true`, `colHas[j][number] = true`.  

`Contains(i, j)`:  
- Return `board[i][j]` (0 means empty).  

`Erase(i, j)`:  
- Let `x = board[i][j]`; set `board[i][j] = 0`, `rowHas[i][x] = false`, `colHas[j][x] = false`.  

All operations are O(1) worst-case, with O(n²) space and initialization time.

Use three n×n arrays:

1. `board[1..n][1..n]` – stores the number at each cell, or 0/NULL if empty.  
2. `rowUsed[1..n][1..n]` – boolean; `rowUsed[i][x] = true` iff number x appears in row i.  
3. `colUsed[1..n][1..n]` – boolean; `colUsed[j][x] = true` iff number x appears in column j.

All are initialized in O(n²) time and use O(n²) space.

Operations:

- `Write(i, j, x)`: if `rowUsed[i][x]` or `colUsed[j][x]` is true, report “illegal”; otherwise set `board[i][j] = x`, `rowUsed[i][x] = true`, `colUsed[j][x] = true`.  
- `Contains(i, j)`: return `board[i][j]` (or “empty” if it is 0/NULL).  
- `Erase(i, j)`: let `x = board[i][j]`; set `board[i][j] = 0/NULL`, `rowUsed[i][x] = false`, `colUsed[j][x] = false`.

All operations access or update a constant number of array entries, so they run in worst-case O(1) time.

Use three n×n arrays:

1. `board[i][j]` – stores the value at cell (i, j) or 0/NULL if empty.  
2. `rowHas[i][num]` – boolean: true if number `num` is already used in row i.  
3. `colHas[j][num]` – boolean: true if number `num` is already used in column j.

Initialization: allocate and zero all three arrays (time and space Θ(n²)).  

`Write(i, j, number)`:
- If `rowHas[i][number]` or `colHas[j][number]` is true, report illegal move.
- Else set `board[i][j] = number`, `rowHas[i][number] = true`, `colHas[j][number] = true`.  

`Contains(i, j)`:
- Return `board[i][j]` (0/NULL indicates empty).  

`Erase(i, j)`:
- Let `x = board[i][j]`.
- Set `board[i][j] = 0/NULL`, `rowHas[i][x] = false`, `colHas[j][x] = false`.

Represent the vector as a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red-black or AVL tree) where each node stores:
1) One element of the vector.
2) The size of its subtree (number of nodes in its left and right subtrees plus one).

The position \(i\) is interpreted as an order statistic (rank) in this tree.  
To read position \(i\), descend the tree using subtree sizes: at each node, compare \(i\) with the size of the left subtree to decide whether to go left, return the current node, or go right with adjusted rank; this takes \(O(\log m)\).  
To insert at position \(i\), perform an order-statistic search to find the node currently at position \(i\) (or where it should go), insert a new node there as in a standard balanced BST, and update subtree sizes on the path up; this is \(O(\log m)\).  
To delete at position \(i\), locate the node of rank \(i\), remove it using standard balanced BST deletion, and update subtree sizes; this is \(O(\log m)\).  
Initialization is \(O(1)\) (empty tree), and at any moment the tree stores exactly one node per vector element, so space is \(O(m)\).

Represent the vector as a balanced binary search tree (e.g., red-black or AVL tree) where each node stores:
1) The element value.
2) The size of its subtree (number of nodes in the subtree rooted at that node).

The subtree-size field allows you to treat the tree as an “order-statistics tree” indexed by position:  
- To read position i, start from the root, use subtree sizes to decide whether to go left, return the current node, or go right, taking O(log m).  
- To insert at position i, search for the (i)-th position using subtree sizes, perform a standard balanced-tree insertion there (as a new node between neighbors), then update subtree sizes on the path and rebalance, all in O(log m).  
- To delete at position i, find the (i)-th node using subtree sizes, remove it with the standard balanced-tree delete operation, update subtree sizes, and rebalance in O(log m).

The tree stores exactly one node per element, so space is O(m).

Represent the vector as a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a weight-balanced tree, AVL tree, or red-black tree), where each node stores:
1) One element of the vector.  
2) The size of its subtree (number of nodes in that subtree).

Define the implicit index of a node by the sizes of left subtrees along the path from the root, so the element at position \(i\) is the one whose in-order rank is \(i\).  
To read from position \(i\), traverse from the root using the stored subtree sizes to decide whether to go left, right, or stop when the left subtree size equals \(i\).  
To insert at position \(i\), find the node that will occupy position \(i\) (again via subtree sizes), then insert a new node into the tree so that the in-order sequence reflects the new element at position \(i\), updating subtree sizes and rebalancing as needed.  
To delete from position \(i\), locate the node of rank \(i\), delete it using standard balanced-BST deletion, update subtree sizes on the path up, and perform any required rebalancing.  
Each operation follows a root-to-leaf path in a balanced tree of height \(O(\log m)\), so read/insert/delete take \(O(\log m)\) worst-case time.  
The tree stores one node per element plus \(O(1)\) extra data per node, so space is \(O(m)\), and initialization with an empty tree is \(O(1)\) time and space.

Represent the vector as a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red-black tree or AVL tree) where each node stores one element and an extra field: the size of its subtree.  
The inorder traversal of the tree corresponds to the logical order of the vector elements.  
For insertion at position \(i\), use the subtree sizes to perform an order-statistic search to find the node that should become the \(i\)-th element, and insert a new node there while rebalancing the tree.  
For reading from position \(i\), again use subtree sizes to navigate from the root to the node whose inorder rank is \(i\) in \(O(\log m)\) time.  
For deletion at position \(i\), find the node of rank \(i\) using subtree sizes, remove it using standard balanced-BST deletion, and update subtree sizes up the tree.  
Each operation touches only \(O(\log m)\) nodes because the tree height is \(O(\log m)\), and each node stores constant extra information (the subtree size), so space is \(O(m)\).  
Initialization is \(O(1)\) by starting with an empty tree and creating nodes only as elements are inserted.

Represent the vector as a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red-black or AVL tree) where each node stores:  
1) one element of the vector, and  
2) the size of its subtree (number of nodes in that subtree).  

Use implicit indexing: positions are determined by subtree sizes rather than explicit keys.  
To read position \(i\), descend from the root using subtree sizes (go left or right depending on the size of the left subtree) in \(O(\log m)\).  
To insert at position \(i\), perform an order-statistic search to find the node currently at position \(i\) (or the successor/predecessor as appropriate) and insert a new node there while maintaining balance and updating subtree sizes, taking \(O(\log m)\).  
To delete at position \(i\), again find the node at position \(i\) via subtree sizes, delete it as in a normal balanced BST, and update subtree sizes on the path up in \(O(\log m)\).  
Initialization is \(O(1)\) by starting with an empty tree, and the space used is \(\Theta(m)\) pointers and elements for \(m\) items.

Represent the vector as a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red-black or AVL tree) where each node stores one element and an extra field: the size of its subtree.  
Each node’s subtree-size field equals 1 plus the sizes of its left and right children.  
To read position \(i\), start at the root and use subtree sizes: if left-subtree size is \(k\), then the element at position \(i\) is in the left child if \(i \le k\), in the right child if \(i > k+1\), and at the current node if \(i = k+1\); following this logic takes \(O(\log m)\) time.  
To insert at position \(i\), perform a tree insertion that finds the node that will become the \(i\)-th element (again using subtree sizes to navigate) and insert a new node there while rebalancing as in standard balanced BSTs.  
During insertion, update subtree sizes along the search path and any nodes affected by rotations; this keeps the structure balanced and maintains correct ranks, all in \(O(\log m)\) time.  
To delete at position \(i\), find the node of rank \(i\) using subtree sizes, remove it as in a normal balanced BST deletion, rebalance if needed, and update subtree sizes on the affected path.  
All operations touch only \(O(\log m)\) nodes in a height-balanced tree, so insertion, deletion, and access by index each take \(O(\log m)\) worst-case time.  
The space used is linear in the number of elements (one node per element plus constant extra fields), and initialization is \(O(1)\) by starting with an empty tree.

Use a balanced binary search tree whose in-order traversal corresponds to the vector order.  
Each node stores: the element value, pointers to left/right children, and the size of its subtree (number of elements in that subtree).  
To read position \(i\), start at the root, let \(L\) be the size of the left subtree; if \(i = L+1\) return this node, if \(i \le L\) go left, else go right with \(i := i - (L+1)\); this takes \(O(\log m)\).  
To insert at position \(i\), perform an order-statistics search as above to find where the new element should go, insert it as in a standard balanced BST, and update subtree sizes on the path up; rebalancing costs \(O(\log m)\).  
To delete at position \(i\), locate the \(i\)-th node using the same order-statistics search, remove it using standard balanced BST deletion, and update subtree sizes and perform rebalancing; this also costs \(O(\log m)\).  
Any self-balancing BST (e.g., red-black tree, AVL tree, treap) augmented with subtree sizes works.  
Initialization is \(O(1)\) by starting with an empty tree.  
Space at any time is \(O(m)\) because each of the \(m\) elements is stored in exactly one node plus \(O(1)\) metadata per node.

Use a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red–black tree or AVL tree) whose keys are the matrix positions and whose values are the stored entries.  
Encode a position \((i,j)\) as a single key, for example \(k = i \cdot m + j\), so that the tree is ordered lexicographically by row and then column.  
Each node in the tree stores: the key \(k\), the value \(A[i][j]\), and the usual tree-balancing metadata (like color or height).  
To insert at \((i,j)\), compute \(k\), perform a standard tree insertion with key \(k\) and the value, which costs \(O(\log m^2) = O(\log m)\).  
To read from \((i,j)\), compute \(k\) and search for key \(k\) in the tree; if found, return its value; otherwise return 0 (or “empty”), also in \(O(\log m)\) time.  
To delete at \((i,j)\), compute \(k\) and perform the standard tree deletion for key \(k\), costing \(O(\log m)\).  
Initialization sets the root pointer to null, which is \(O(1)\) time and space.  
At any time, the tree contains exactly one node per nonempty matrix entry, so the total space is \(O(\text{# of stored entries})\).

Use a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red–black tree or AVL tree) whose keys are the occupied positions of the matrix.  
Encode each position \((i,j)\) as a single key, for example \(k = i \cdot m + j\), so that keys are totally ordered by their integer value.  
Store in each node the key \(k\) and the corresponding matrix value, plus the usual tree pointers and balance information.  
To insert at \((i,j)\), compute \(k\), search for \(k\); if not found, insert a new node with key \(k\) and value, all in \(O(\log m^2) = O(\log m)\) time.  
To read from \((i,j)\), compute \(k\) and search for \(k\); if found, return its value; otherwise return a default value (e.g., 0), which takes \(O(\log m)\) time.  
To delete at \((i,j)\), compute \(k\), search for \(k\), and if found, delete the node from the tree in \(O(\log m)\) time.  
Initialization is \(O(1)\) since the tree starts empty.  
Space at any time is \(O(t)\), where \(t\) is the number of stored (non-empty) entries, satisfying the required space bound.

Use a dynamic 2D balanced search structure keyed by coordinates.  
Treat each stored entry as a triple \((i,j,val)\) and maintain them in (for example) a balanced binary search tree (e.g., red–black tree or AVL) ordered lexicographically by \((i,j)\).  
To insert at \((i,j)\), perform a standard tree insertion with key \((i,j)\) and value \(val\), which takes \(O(\log n)\), and since there are at most \(m^2\) entries, this is \(O(\log m)\) in terms of \(m\).  
To read from \((i,j)\), search the tree for key \((i,j)\); this also takes \(O(\log m)\) time.  
To delete at \((i,j)\), perform a standard tree deletion by key \((i,j)\), again in \(O(\log m)\) time.  
Initialization needs only to create an empty tree, which is \(O(1)\) time and space.  
Space at any time is proportional to the number of stored entries, because each nonempty cell contributes one node in the tree.

Store only the nonempty entries of the matrix in a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red–black tree or AVL tree).  
Represent each key as an ordered pair \((i,j)\), and order keys lexicographically: \((i_1,j_1) < (i_2,j_2)\) if \(i_1<i_2\) or (\(i_1=i_2\) and \(j_1<j_2\)).  
Each node in the tree stores the key \((i,j)\) and the corresponding matrix value.  
To read \((i,j)\), search the tree for key \((i,j)\); if found, return its value, otherwise return 0 (or “empty”).  
To insert at \((i,j)\), perform a standard balanced-BST insert with key \((i,j)\); if the key already exists, just update its value.  
To delete at \((i,j)\), perform a standard balanced-BST delete of the key \((i,j)\) if it exists.  
All these operations on a balanced BST take \(O(\log k)\) time where \(k\) is the number of stored elements.  
Since \(k \le m^2\), \(O(\log k) = O(\log m)\), giving the required worst-case bounds.  
Initialization creates an empty tree, which is \(O(1)\) time and space.  
At any time, the space used is \(O(k)\), i.e., proportional to the number of stored (nonempty) entries.

Store only the nonempty entries in a balanced search tree keyed by their (i, j) positions.  
Use a single balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red-black tree or AVL tree) whose keys are pairs (i, j), ordered lexicographically: (i₁, j₁) < (i₂, j₂) if i₁ < i₂ or (i₁ = i₂ and j₁ < j₂).  
For insertion at (i, j), perform a tree search for key (i, j); if absent, insert (i, j) with its value in the tree, which costs O(log m) because there are at most m² possible positions and at most O(m²) nodes, but we only store existing entries so it’s O(log k) where k is current number of stored entries (≤ m²).  
For reading at (i, j), search the tree for key (i, j); if found, return its associated value; otherwise, return 0 (or “empty”); this search costs O(log k) ⊆ O(log m).  
For deletion at (i, j), search for key (i, j); if present, delete that node from the tree, which costs O(log k) ⊆ O(log m).  
Initialization is O(1) by creating an empty tree.  
Space used at any time is proportional to the number of stored (nonempty) entries, which is O(number of elements in the data structure).

Use a balanced binary search tree keyed by pairs \((i,j)\), storing only the nonempty entries of the matrix.  
Represent each key as a single integer, for example \(k = i \cdot m + j\), so that comparisons are done in \(O(1)\) time and the tree is ordered lexicographically by \((i,j)\).  
To insert at \((i,j)\), compute \(k\), then insert the key–value pair \((k, \text{value})\) into the balanced BST in \(O(\log m^2) = O(\log m)\) time (since there are at most \(m^2\) positions).  
To read from \((i,j)\), compute \(k\) and search for key \(k\); return its value if found, or a default (e.g. 0) if not present, again in \(O(\log m)\) time.  
To delete at \((i,j)\), compute \(k\) and delete the key \(k\) from the BST in \(O(\log m)\) time.  
Initialization is \(O(1)\) by starting with an empty BST and remembering \(m\).  
The data structure stores only entries actually inserted, so its space at any time is \(O(t)\), where \(t\) is the number of stored entries, which is \(O\) of the number of elements in use.

Use a balanced binary search tree (e.g., red–black tree or AVL tree) that stores only nonempty entries as nodes.  
Each node’s key is the pair \((i,j)\), ordered lexicographically: \((i_1,j_1) < (i_2,j_2)\) iff \(i_1 < i_2\) or \((i_1 = i_2 \text{ and } j_1 < j_2)\).  
The node’s value is the matrix entry at \((i,j)\).  
To read \((i,j)\), search the tree for key \((i,j)\); if found, return the stored value, otherwise return 0 (or “empty”).  
To insert at \((i,j)\), perform a standard tree insertion for key \((i,j)\), updating the value if the key already exists.  
To delete at \((i,j)\), perform a standard tree deletion for key \((i,j)\) if it exists.  
All these tree operations (search, insert, delete) take \(O(\log k)\) time where \(k\) is number of stored entries, and since \(k \le m^2\), this is \(O(\log m)\).  
Initialization is \(O(1)\) by creating an empty tree, and space is \(O(k)\), the number of stored (nonzero) entries.

Use a balanced binary search tree keyed by the element value (e.g., a Red-Black Tree or AVL Tree).  
1. **write(x)**:  
   - Search the tree for key `x` (O(log n)).  
   - If not found, insert a new node with key `x` into the tree using standard balanced-BST insertion and rebalancing (O(log n)).  

2. **contains(x)**:  
   - Perform a standard balanced-BST search for key `x`.  
   - Return true if a node with key `x` exists, otherwise false (O(log n)).  

3. **erase(x)**:  
   - Search the tree for key `x` (O(log n)).  
   - If found, delete that node using standard balanced-BST deletion and rebalancing (O(log n)).  

The tree nodes total exactly the number of elements stored, so space at any moment is linear in the number of elements.

Use a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red–black tree or AVL tree) keyed by the element value.  
Each node stores: the key (element), pointers to left/right children and parent, and any balancing metadata (e.g., color or height).  
`write(x)`: insert key `x` into the tree using the standard BST insertion procedure, followed by the tree’s balancing steps; this takes \(O(\log n)\) time because the tree height is \(O(\log n)\).  
If duplicates must be allowed, store with each key either a count or a secondary balanced structure; updating or inserting still costs \(O(\log n)\).  
`contains(x)`: search for key `x` following BST search rules: compare `x` with the current node’s key and go left or right accordingly until found or a null is reached; this is \(O(\log n)\).  
`erase(x)`: locate `x` via BST search, then delete the found node using the usual BST deletion algorithm (handling 0, 1, or 2 children) and perform the tree’s rebalancing; both search and rebalancing are \(O(\log n)\).  
Initialization just sets the root pointer to null, which is \(O(1)\) time and space.  
The tree stores exactly one node per element (plus constant metadata), so space at any moment is \(O(n)\).

Use a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red-black tree or AVL tree) keyed by the element value.  
Each node stores: the key (element), pointers to left and right children, a parent pointer (optional), and balancing metadata (color or height).  
For `write(x)`, perform a standard BST search for x; if found, do nothing (or update the node); if not found, insert a new node in the correct position and rebalance in O(log n).  
For `contains(x)`, perform a standard BST search comparing x with keys along a root-to-leaf path, returning true if a matching node is found, otherwise false, in O(log n).  
For `erase(x)`, search for x; if not found, do nothing; if found, remove the node using the BST deletion procedure (handle 0, 1, or 2 children) and then rebalance in O(log n).  
The tree is empty at initialization, so initialization time and space are O(1).  
At any moment, the tree stores one node per element, so total space is O(n), which is O(the number of elements in the data structure).

Use a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red–black tree or AVL tree) keyed by the element’s value.  
1. **write(x)**: Insert key `x` into the tree if it is not already present; this costs \(O(\log n)\) due to tree rebalancing.  
2. **contains(x)**: Search for key `x` in the tree using standard BST lookup, which descends at most the tree height, giving \(O(\log n)\).  
3. **erase(x)**: Delete key `x` from the tree if it exists, using the standard BST delete plus rebalancing, which is \(O(\log n)\).  
The tree nodes store only the element and the necessary balancing metadata (e.g., color or height), so each node is \(O(1)\) space.  
Initialization is \(O(1)\) by starting with an empty tree (no nodes allocated).  
At any time, the total space used is \(O(k)\), where \(k\) is the current number of stored elements, since there is exactly one node per element.

Use a balanced binary search tree keyed by element values (e.g., a red–black tree or AVL tree).  
Each node stores: the value, links to left/right child and parent, and the usual balancing metadata (e.g., height or color).  
`write(x)`: search for value `x` in the tree in `O(log n)`; if it does not exist, insert a new node for `x` and rebalance the tree, which also takes `O(log n)`.  
`contains(x)`: perform a standard BST search by comparing `x` with node keys along a root-to-leaf path; the height is `O(log n)`, so this takes `O(log n)`.  
`erase(x)`: search for node containing `x` in `O(log n)`; if found, remove it using the BST deletion procedure, then restore balance using rotations/recoloring, which is `O(log n)`.  
Initialization allocates an empty tree (just a null root), which is `O(1)` time and space.  
At any time, there is exactly one node per stored element, plus `O(1)` metadata per node, giving total space `O(n) = O(number of elements)`.

Use a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red–black tree or AVL tree) keyed by the element’s value.  
Each node stores a key (the element) and any needed metadata for balancing, using \(O(1)\) extra space per node.  
**write(x):** insert x as in a standard balanced BST insertion; if x already exists, you may either ignore or update its node.  
The tree rebalancing after insertion keeps the height \(O(\log n)\), so write runs in \(O(\log n)\).  
**contains(x):** search for x in the BST by following left/right child pointers according to key comparisons.  
The search path length is at most the tree height, so contains runs in \(O(\log n)\).  
**erase(x):** find x using the same \(O(\log n)\) search; if found, delete its node using standard balanced BST deletion.  
After deletion, perform the usual rebalancing rotations/updates, keeping height \(O(\log n)\), so erase runs in \(O(\log n)\).  
Initialization allocates an empty tree structure, which is \(O(1)\) time and space.  
At all times, the tree stores exactly one node per element, so space is \(O(\text{number of elements})\).

Use a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red-black or AVL tree) keyed by the element value.  
Each node stores a key (the element) and possibly satellite data; left and right child pointers and a parent pointer maintain the tree structure.  
Initialization is O(1): create an empty tree with a null root and no allocated nodes.  

**write(x):**  
Search the tree for key x in O(log n); if found, optionally update its value; if not found, insert a new node in the appropriate position, then rebalance/rotate as required by the balanced BST rules in O(log n).  

**contains(x):**  
Run a standard BST search from the root, going left or right based on comparisons, and return true if a node with key x is found, false otherwise; this takes O(log n).  

**erase(x):**  
Search for key x in O(log n); if present, delete the node using the BST delete procedure (handle 0, 1, or 2 children), then perform any rebalancing/rotations needed by the balanced BST in O(log n).  

At any time, the tree stores exactly one node per element, so total space is Θ(n), and each individual operation takes O(log n) time.

Use a balanced binary search tree augmented with a small buffer of pending updates, then periodically rebuild the tree to keep operations efficient.  
Store all keys in an array-based, balanced BST representation (e.g., a static balanced BST over a sorted array), plus a balanced “buffer tree” (like a red–black tree) holding recent inserts/deletes.  
Search(x): look up x in the main tree in O(log n) and, if not found, in the buffer tree in O(log n); total O(log n).  
Insert(x): insert into the buffer tree in O(log n); when the buffer size reaches a threshold (e.g., n/2), rebuild the entire main structure from the union of main + buffer in O(n), then clear the buffer, giving amortized O(log n) per insert.  
Delete(x): insert a delete marker into the buffer tree (or delete from buffer if present) in O(log n); when too many deletions accumulate (e.g., half the elements are marked), rebuild the main tree without deleted keys, giving amortized O(log n).  
The main tree is always a perfectly balanced BST over a sorted array, so its operations are O(log n) worst case.  
The buffer tree is a standard balanced BST (e.g., red–black or AVL) supporting O(log n) inserts, deletes, and searches.  
Rebuilds cost O(n) but are triggered only after Θ(n) updates, hence their cost spreads to O(1) amortized per update, preserving the O(log n) amortized bounds.

Use an ordinary balanced binary search tree (BST) (e.g., a red–black tree or AVL tree) keyed by x.  
The tree maintains the BST order invariant and keeps height O(log n) via local rotations and recoloring/rebalancing after updates.  
Insert(x) is done by a standard BST insert followed by O(1) amortized or O(log n) worst-case rotations/fixes, giving amortized O(log n) time.  
Search(x) follows the BST property down the tree, taking time proportional to the height, which is O(log n).  
Delete(x) first finds the node in O(log n), then uses the usual BST delete cases (0, 1, or 2 children), and finally performs O(1) amortized or O(log n) rebalancing work.  
Red–black trees guarantee height at most 2 log(n+1), ensuring all three operations run in O(log n) worst-case, and thus also amortized.  
Nodes store key, pointers to children and parent (optional), and a small constant extra field (e.g., color for red–black or balance factor for AVL).  
The structure dynamically adjusts as elements are inserted and removed, so no rebuilding or global restructuring is needed to keep the amortized bounds.

Use a *randomized treap* (tree + heap by random priority).  
Each node stores a key, a randomly chosen priority, and pointers to left/right children; keys obey the BST order, priorities obey the min-/max-heap order.  
Search(x) is just a standard BST search, following left/right depending on key comparisons, which takes expected O(log n) time because random priorities keep the tree height O(log n) in expectation.  
Insert(x) first performs a BST insert by key, then assigns a random priority to the new node and rotates it upward (using standard tree rotations) until the heap property is restored, costing expected O(log n).  
Delete(x) first searches for the node, then repeatedly rotates it downward (choosing the child with “better” priority to rotate with) until it becomes a leaf, and finally removes it, again costing expected O(log n).  
The randomization makes all operations have expected (amortized) logarithmic time without requiring strict rebalancing rules as in AVL or red–black trees.  
Space usage is linear in the number of keys, and no additional global structure besides the root pointer is needed.

Use a standard balanced binary search tree such as a red–black tree or AVL tree.  
Store all keys in nodes ordered by their key values with parent/left/right pointers and a balance/color bit.  
The tree maintains height Θ(log n) through local rotations and recolorings (or rebalancing operations) after updates.  
Insert(x): perform a standard BST insert in O(h) time, then rebalance using a constant number of rotations and recolorings, keeping h = O(log n).  
Search(x): descend the tree from the root comparing x with node keys, following left/right pointers; this takes O(h) = O(log n) time.  
Delete(x): perform standard BST delete (remove a leaf or swap with successor/predecessor) in O(h) and then rebalance via a bounded number of rotations and recolorings.  
Each rotation/recoloring/rebalancing step is O(1), and only O(log n) nodes on the search path are affected.  
Thus Insert, Search, and Delete all run in O(log n) worst-case time, which also implies O(log n) amortized time.

Use a standard balanced binary search tree such as a red–black tree or AVL tree.  
Store one key per node; maintain the usual BST invariant (left < node < right).  
Each node additionally stores a constant amount of metadata (e.g., color in red–black trees or height/balance factor in AVL trees).  
Insert(x): perform a standard BST insert in O(h), where h is tree height, then rebalance using rotations and local metadata updates; in a balanced BST, h = O(log n), so insertion is O(log n).  
Search(x): follow the BST property from the root, going left or right at each node until x is found or a null pointer is reached; this takes O(h) = O(log n).  
Delete(x): search for x, then delete it using the usual BST deletion cases (leaf, one child, two children with successor/predecessor swap), followed by rebalancing via rotations and metadata updates; this is also O(h) = O(log n).  
All three operations thus run in worst-case O(log n), which implies amortized O(log n) as required.

Use a standard balanced binary search tree (BST), such as a Red-Black Tree or AVL Tree, with keys stored in its nodes.  
Each node keeps a key, pointers to its children (and optionally parent), and any balancing metadata (color for Red-Black, height/balance factor for AVL).  
Insert(x) performs a standard BST insertion followed by a bounded number of local rebalancing steps (rotations and recolorings/height updates), giving worst-case and thus amortized time O(log n).  
Search(x) follows the usual BST search path from the root down, comparing x with node keys and moving left or right, taking time proportional to the tree height O(log n).  
Delete(x) first finds the node by Search(x), then removes it using standard BST delete cases (leaf, one child, or two children via successor/predecessor swap), followed by a bounded number of rebalancing operations, all in O(log n) worst-case.  
The balancing rules ensure the tree height remains O(log n) for n nodes, so all three operations run in O(log n) amortized time.  
To implement, define node structure, rotation functions (left/right), and the standard insert-fixup and delete-fixup algorithms from Red-Black or AVL trees.  
Memory usage is O(n), and the structure works for any totally ordered keys (using a comparator if needed).

Use a standard self-balancing binary search tree, such as a Red-Black Tree (RB-tree).  
Each node stores a key, color (red/black), and pointers to left child, right child, and parent.  
Insert(x) works by performing a standard BST insert in O(h) time (h is tree height), then running the RB “fixup” procedure with rotations and recolorings, which costs O(h); since RB-trees maintain h = O(log n), insertion is O(log n).  
Search(x) is a normal BST search following left/right pointers until x is found or a null is reached, which takes O(h) = O(log n).  
Delete(x) first performs a standard BST delete (replacing with successor if needed) in O(h), then runs the RB “delete fixup” to restore balance, also in O(h).  
The RB invariants (root black, no red-red parent-child, same number of black nodes on all root–leaf paths) ensure the height stays Θ(log n).  
Thus all three operations (Insert, Search, Delete) run in O(log n) worst-case time, which also satisfies the amortized O(log n) requirement.

Use two structures in parallel:

1. Maintain all keys in a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red–black tree) ordered by key; this supports searches and ordered traversals in O(log n) time.  
2. Also maintain a hash table mapping each key x directly to its corresponding node pointer in the tree; this lets you find the exact node for x in expected O(1) time.  
3. Insert(x): insert x into the balanced BST in O(log n), get the node pointer, and store (x → node) in the hash table in expected O(1); total amortized O(log n).  
4. Search(x): look up x in the hash table in expected O(1) to get its node pointer, or, if strictly amortized O(log n) is required under worst-case hashing assumptions, do a normal BST search in O(log n).  
5. Delete(x): find the node via the hash table in O(1), delete its entry from the hash table in O(1), and instead of doing a full BST delete, lazily mark the node as “deleted” in O(1).  
6. To keep the BST size under control, rebuild/clean up the tree (physically removing marked nodes) only when the number of marked-deleted nodes is, say, at least half the total nodes.  
7. That global rebuild takes O(n) but happens after Ω(n) deletions, so the amortized cost per delete remains O(1) while search and insert remain O(log n).

Use an *indirect* dictionary: maintain a hash table from keys to pointers into a dynamic array (or vector) that stores the actual keys.  
Insert(x): append x to the end of the array in O(1) amortized, and store (x → index) in a hash table in O(1) expected; overall O(1) amortized, which satisfies O(log n).  
Search(x): look up x in the hash table in O(1) expected time, again within O(log n).  
Delete(x): use the hash table to find x’s index i in O(1) expected time, swap the last element of the array into position i in O(1), update that element’s stored index in the hash table in O(1), then remove x from the hash table in O(1); overall O(1) amortized.  
Thus, all three operations meet the requested amortized time bounds.  
If worst-case guarantees are needed, use a dynamic perfect hash table or deamortized hashing to ensure O(1) worst-case operations instead of expected.

Use two balanced binary search trees (BSTs): a main BST over keys and an auxiliary BST over “deletion order.”  
Each node has fields: key, pointers (left/right/parent), a boolean deleted flag, and a pointer `dptr` to its node (if any) in the deletion-order BST.  
In the deletion-order BST, nodes are ordered by an increasing counter (timestamp) assigned when a key is deleted; each contains a pointer back to the main BST node.  

**Insert(x)**:  
1) Search in the main BST (O(log n)); if x exists and is marked deleted, unmark it and remove its deletion-order node if present (O(log n)).  
2) Otherwise, insert a new node for x into the main BST (O(log n)), with `deleted = false` and `dptr = null`.  

**Search(x)**:  
Search for x in the main BST in O(log n), and check the deleted flag; return “found” only if not deleted.  

**Delete(x)** (logical delete, O(1) amortized):  
1) Find x in the main BST in O(log n); if absent or already deleted, do nothing.  
2) Mark node as deleted, assign it the next timestamp, and insert a node into the deletion-order BST (amortized O(1) if we batch/periodically rebuild).  

**Rebuild (garbage collection)**:  
When the ratio of deleted to total nodes in the main BST exceeds a constant (e.g., 1/2), rebuild a new clean BST from all non-deleted keys (cost O(n)), and reset the auxiliary tree and counters; this cost amortizes to O(1) per delete and keeps all operations within the stated bounds.

Use a hash table to store elements and a doubly linked list to maintain them in a structure that supports O(1) deletion by handle, but wrap this inside a global rebuilding scheme to keep amortized Insert and Search at O(log n).  
Partition keys into buckets whose sizes are kept between (1/2)log n and 2 log n by periodically rebuilding when the total size doubles or halves, like in global rebuilding for dynamic arrays.  
Inside each bucket, store elements in a balanced BST (e.g., red–black tree) or sorted dynamic array so that a bucket’s operations are O(log log n), and the number of buckets is O(n / log n).  
Maintain a top-level static balanced search structure (e.g., a perfectly balanced search tree or sorted array) over bucket representatives, rebuilt from scratch when n changes by a constant factor, giving amortized O(1) rebuilding cost per update.  
To Insert(x), find the correct bucket using the top-level structure in O(log n), insert into the bucket’s BST/array, and if the bucket overflows, split it and trigger global rebuilds only when total size doubles.  
To Search(x), use the top-level structure to locate its bucket and then search in the bucket’s local BST/array, yielding total amortized O(log n).  
For Delete(x), use the hash table to get a direct pointer to x’s node within its bucket, remove it from the local BST/array and any linked list in O(1) expected time, and mark the bucket underfull to be fixed lazily.  
When a bucket becomes too small, merge it with a neighbor during a global rebuilding phase, spreading the cost over many operations so the amortized delete time remains O(1).  
Thus, careful bucketing plus global rebuilding and hashing for direct access yields Insert and Search in amortized O(log n) and Delete in amortized O(1).

Use two synchronized data structures:  
1) a dynamic array `A` storing all current keys in arbitrary order, and  
2) a balanced binary search tree `T` (e.g., red-black tree) mapping key `x` to its index in `A`.  

Insert(x): append `x` to `A` in O(1) amortized, let its index be `i`, and insert (x → i) into `T` in O(log n).  
Search(x): look up `x` in `T` in O(log n) time; if found, return the index and/or value from `A`.  
Delete(x): search `x` in `T` to get its index `i` (cost O(log n), but can be paid for by earlier inserts, giving amortized O(1) delete if operations are intermixed appropriately), then:  
• Let `j` be the index of the last element in `A`; move `A[j]` into position `i` (swap or overwrite) in O(1).  
• Update in `T` the stored index for the key that was at `j` to `i` in O(log n) (again accounted for amortized).  
• Remove the last element from `A` in O(1) and delete x’s entry from `T` in O(log n).  

By standard amortized analysis (e.g., charging future delete costs to prior inserts/searches and using a potential function over number of elements and tree height), this yields Insert and Search in amortized O(log n) and Delete in amortized O(1).

Use two structures together: a dynamic array (or doubly linked list) storing all keys, and a hash table mapping each key x to its current index (or node pointer) in that container.  
Insert(x): append x to the end of the array in O(1) amortized and store (x → index) in the hash table in expected O(1); overall O(1) amortized ⊆ O(log n).  
Search(x): look up x directly in the hash table in expected O(1) ⊆ O(log n).  
Delete(x):  
1) Use the hash table to find the index i (or node) of x in expected O(1).  
2) Swap the element at i with the last element in the array (or splice it out from a linked list) in O(1).  
3) Update the hash table entry for the moved element (if any) and delete x from the hash table, all in expected O(1).  
All three operations use expected O(1) time, which satisfies the required amortized bounds of O(log n) for Insert/Search and O(1) for Delete.

Use a standard binary min-heap plus an auxiliary hash table that maps each key to its current index in the heap array.  
Insert(x): append x to the end of the heap array, store (x → index) in the hash table, then “bubble up” to restore the heap property; this is O(log n) worst-case and amortized.  
Search(x): look up x in the hash table; if present, you get its index in O(1) expected time, and you can return “found” or its position, so this meets the O(log n) amortized bound.  
Delete(x):  
1) Use the hash table to get the index i of x in O(1) expected time.  
2) Swap the element at i with the last element in the heap array; update the moved element’s index in the hash table, then pop the last element (removing x) — all O(1).  
3) Now possibly restore the heap by one “bubble up” or “bubble down” step, but amortize this to O(1) by using lazy restructuring: only fully fix the heap shape on future Insert/Search operations (which already allow O(log n) amortized time).  
The key idea is that direct access to an element’s position via the hash table makes the logical removal O(1), while any necessary reheapification work is deferred and charged to later heap operations.

Use two synchronized structures: a balanced binary search tree (e.g., red–black tree) on keys, and a hash table mapping each key to its tree node.  
Insert(x): Insert x into the tree in O(log n), get a pointer to the created node, and store (x → node*) in the hash table in expected O(1) (overall amortized O(log n)).  
Search(x): Look up x in the hash table in expected O(1) and optionally verify/locate it in the tree via the stored node pointer if needed (amortized O(log n) upper bound holds).  
Delete(x): Use the hash table to find the pointer to x’s node in O(1), then perform a constant-time “lazy delete” in the tree (e.g., mark the node as deleted and optionally unlink it in O(1) with appropriate node representation).  
To maintain the amortized bounds, periodically rebuild or clean up the tree when the fraction of lazily deleted nodes becomes too large, redistributing that cost over many operations.  
The hash table guarantees O(1) expected time and O(1) worst-case actual work per single Delete call, since delete only touches a constant number of pointers/flags.  
Balancing work (tree rebalancing and periodic rebuilding) occurs during Insert and/or Search and accounts for the O(log n) amortized time there, while Delete remains O(1) amortized and worst case.

Use two structures in parallel:  
1) a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red–black tree) keyed by x, and  
2) an unsorted dynamic array (or singly linked list) storing all keys, plus a hash table mapping each key x to its position (index or node pointer) in that array/list.  

Insert(x): Insert x into the balanced BST in O(log n), append x to the end of the array in O(1) amortized, and store x → position in the hash table in O(1) expected.  
Search(x): Perform standard BST search in O(log n) amortized.  

Delete(x):  
• In the array: use the hash table to find x’s position in O(1), swap it with the last element, update the last element’s position in the hash table, and pop the last element, all in O(1) worst case.  
• In the BST: use lazy deletion by marking the node as deleted (or keeping a separate “deleted” flag) in O(1), and ignore these nodes in future searches / operations.  

When the number of logically deleted nodes becomes a constant fraction of the total, rebuild the BST from the array in O(n); charging this cost over the insertions/deletions gives amortized O(log n) for Insert/Search and preserves O(1) Delete.

Use a standard balanced binary search tree (e.g., red–black tree or AVL tree) as the main structure, and add one extra pointer “toDelete” that always refers to some node currently in the tree (if the tree is nonempty).  
Maintain that every node stores its key, parent, left/right children, and (optionally) color/balance info; standard insert and search work in \(O(\log n)\) as usual.  
For Delete(x), do not immediately delete the node with key \(x\); instead, just mark it as “logically deleted” (a boolean flag) in \(O(1)\) time.  
Then physically delete the node pointed to by “toDelete” using the usual balanced-BST deletion procedure, which is \(O(\log n)\).  
After that deletion finishes, move “toDelete” to its in-order successor (or any deterministic next node), possibly skipping nodes already marked deleted.  
Because each real \(O(\log n)\)-time tree deletion is charged to many preceding \(O(1)\) logical deletions, the amortized cost per Delete is \(O(1)\) while the actual per-call pointer/flag operations stay \(O(1)\) worst-case.  
Search ignores logically deleted nodes (treat them as absent), and still runs in \(O(\log n)\) due to the balanced tree height.  
Insert is a normal balanced-BST insertion in \(O(\log n)\), and you ensure newly inserted nodes are unmarked and link correctly into the structural order used to advance “toDelete.”

Use a standard balanced binary search tree (e.g., red–black tree or AVL tree) to maintain the keys in order and support `Search(x)` and `Insert(x)` in \(O(\log n)\) time.  
Augment each node with:
1. A pointer to its “twin” record in a separate data structure.
2. A unique ID or pointer back so the twin can identify its tree node.

Separately, maintain a hash table mapping each key \(x\) to its corresponding tree node; collisions are handled with linked lists or open addressing.  
Insert: compute the hash in expected \(O(1)\), create a hash record, insert it into the hash table, then insert the key into the balanced BST and cross-link the two records; total amortized \(O(\log n)\) (dominated by the tree).  
Search: use the BST to find \(x\) in \(O(\log n)\), or alternatively use the hash table to find it in expected \(O(1)\).  
Delete: use the hash table to get the tree node in expected \(O(1)\), then perform a *lazy deletion* in the tree by marking the node as deleted and unlinking it from the hash table in \(O(1)\).  
To keep the BST from growing too large with deleted nodes, rebuild the tree from the currently active keys whenever the number of deleted nodes exceeds, say, half the total size; this rebuild costs \(O(n)\) but occurs infrequently, yielding amortized \(O(1)\) delete time.  
The worst-case cost of an individual `Delete(x)` is \(O(1)\) because it only touches a constant number of pointers and flags; the occasional rebuild cost is spread over many prior deletions.

Use an indirect combination of a balanced search tree and a doubly linked list with lazy deletion.  
Store all keys in a balanced BST (e.g., red–black tree) supporting Insert and Search in \(O(\log n)\) worst-case; each node stores a pointer to a node in a doubly linked list.  
Maintain a doubly linked list containing exactly the “live” elements, in any order (e.g., insertion order).  
Also maintain a hash table from key \(x\) to its list node (and optionally to its BST node) so that, given \(x\), you can access its list node in \(O(1)\) expected time.  
To Insert(\(x\)), insert \(x\) into the BST in \(O(\log n)\), append a node with key \(x\) to the linked list in \(O(1)\), and store/update the hash entry for \(x\).  
To Search(\(x\)), look up \(x\) in the BST in \(O(\log n)\) and check that its list node is present (i.e., not marked deleted).  
To Delete(\(x\)), use the hash table to find the list node in \(O(1)\), remove that node from the doubly linked list in \(O(1)\), and mark the corresponding BST node as “deleted” (without physically removing it), also in \(O(1)\).  
Physical removal of logically deleted nodes from the BST is postponed: when their number exceeds, say, half the tree size, rebuild the BST from the current list in \(O(n)\) time, distributing this cost over the preceding operations for amortized \(O(\log n)\) for Insert/Search and \(O(1)\) for Delete.  
Because Delete only adjusts constant-size structures (list and flags/hash entries) per call, its worst-case and amortized time are both \(O(1)\), while the tree-based Search/Insert ensure \(O(\log n)\) amortized bounds.

Use two synchronized structures:  
1) A balanced binary search tree (e.g., red–black tree) storing all keys to support ordered search and logarithmic-time insertion.  
2) A hash table mapping each key to its corresponding node in the BST.  

Operations:  
- Insert(x): search in hash table (O(1)) to check for existence; if absent, insert into BST (O(log n)) and store (x → node) in hash table (O(1)), giving O(log n) amortized.  
- Search(x): look up x in the hash table in expected O(1) time (which is ≤ O(log n) as required); if found, you can access the BST node if needed.  
- Delete(x): look up x in hash table to get its BST node in O(1), then delete that node from the BST in O(1) by using a “lazy deletion” flag (mark node as deleted without rebalancing) and remove x from the hash table in O(1), giving O(1) worst-case and amortized.  
To prevent unbounded growth of “deleted” nodes, periodically rebuild the BST from all undeleted keys when the fraction of deleted nodes becomes large; this global rebuild costs O(n) but done rarely yields O(1) amortized deletion and O(log n) amortized search/insert.

Use two structures in parallel:

1. Keep all keys in any balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red–black tree) to support `Insert(x)` and `Search(x)` in \(O(\log n)\) worst-case time.  
2. Also maintain a dynamic array (or doubly-linked list) storing all keys, and a hash table mapping each key \(x\) to its index in the array (or pointer to its list node).  
3. On insert, insert into the BST in \(O(\log n)\), append \(x\) to the array in amortized \(O(1)\), and record its position in the hash table in amortized \(O(1)\).  
4. On search, query the BST, which takes \(O(\log n)\) time.  
5. On delete, use the hash table to find the element’s array index (or list node) in \(O(1)\), swap-with-last-and-pop in the array (updating the moved element’s index in the hash table) or remove a list node, all in \(O(1)\) worst-case.  
6. You may leave the deleted key in the BST as a “logically deleted” node (marked as deleted), so delete never touches the BST, keeping delete at \(O(1)\).  
7. The number of logically deleted nodes in the BST remains \(O(n)\) if, when it exceeds a constant fraction of the tree size, you rebuild the BST from the current array in \(O(n)\), which amortizes to \(O(1)\) per delete.  
8. Thus, insert/search cost is dominated by the BST (\(O(\log n)\)), while delete is done via the hash+array (or list) in \(O(1)\) worst-case and amortized.

Let \(B = \left\lceil \frac{2^N}{N^3} \right\rceil\); each array element is of the form \(a_i = q_i \cdot B\), with \(0 \le a_i \le 2^N\).  
Then \(0 \le q_i \le \left\lfloor \frac{2^N}{B} \right\rfloor \le N^3\), so each \(q_i\) fits in the range \([0, N^3]\).  
To sort the original array, it suffices to sort the integer quotients \(q_i\), since multiplication by the positive constant \(B\) preserves order.  
Use counting sort (or radix sort) on the \(q_i\) values:  
1. Initialize a count array \(C[0 \dots N^3]\) with zeros.  
2. For each element \(a_i\), compute \(q_i = a_i / B\) (integer division) and increment \(C[q_i]\).  
3. Compute prefix sums over \(C\) to get starting positions.  
4. Build the sorted array of \(q_i\) using \(C\), and then multiply each by \(B\) to recover the sorted \(a_i\).  
This algorithm runs in \(O(k + N^3)\) time and uses \(O(N^3)\) auxiliary space, which is efficient when \(N^3\) is not too large relative to \(k\).

Map each number to a smaller “compressed” key, sort by this key, then recover the original values:

1. Let \(M = \left\lceil \frac{2^N}{N^3} \right\rceil\); each array value is of the form \(x_i = M \cdot y_i\), where \(0 \le x_i \le 2^N\), so \(0 \le y_i \le \frac{2^N}{M} = O(N^3)\).
2. In \(O(k)\) time, transform the array to \(y_i = x_i / M\); all \(y_i\) are integers in \([0, O(N^3)]\).
3. Use counting sort (or radix sort) on the \(y_i\) values in \(O(k + N^3)\) time, which is polynomial in \(N\) and linear in \(k\).
4. In \(O(k)\) time, map back to the original values by computing \(x_i = M \cdot y_i\) in sorted order.
5. The overall algorithm is comparison-free, runs in \(O(k + N^3)\), and correctly sorts the original array.

Let \(M = \left\lceil \frac{2^N}{N^3} \right\rceil\).  
1. Replace each element \(a_i\) by the quotient \(b_i = a_i / M\); all \(b_i\) are integers in \([0, \lfloor 2^N / M \rfloor]\), and \(\lfloor 2^N / M \rfloor = O(N^3)\).  
2. Allocate a counting array `C[0..R]`, where \(R = \lfloor 2^N / M \rfloor = O(N^3)\), initialized to 0.  
3. For each \(b_i\), increment `C[b_i]`.  
4. Traverse `C` from 0 to \(R\); for each index \(j\), output the value \(j \cdot M\) exactly `C[j]` times into the sorted array.  
This is a counting-sort–style algorithm with time complexity \(O(k + N^3)\) and space \(O(N^3)\), which is efficient when \(k\) is large relative to \(N\).

Use a counting-sort–style algorithm exploiting the large common divisor:

1. Let \(d = \left\lceil \frac{2^N}{N^3} \right\rceil\). Every element \(a_i\) can be written as \(a_i = d \cdot b_i\), where \(0 \le a_i \le 2^N\), so \(0 \le b_i \le \left\lfloor \frac{2^N}{d} \right\rfloor = O(N^3)\).  
2. Create an array `count[0 .. M]`, where \(M = \left\lfloor \frac{2^N}{d} \right\rfloor\), initialized to 0.  
3. For each element \(a_i\) in the input, compute \(b_i = a_i / d\) (integer division, exact) and increment `count[b_i]`.  
4. Traverse `count` from 0 to \(M\); for each index \(j\), output the value \(d \cdot j\) repeated `count[j]` times into the result array.  
5. This sorts the array in \(O(k + N^3)\) time and \(O(N^3)\) extra space, which is efficient because the effective key range is only \(O(N^3)\), not \(2^N\).

Use the fact that every number is a multiple of \(m = \left\lceil \frac{2^N}{N^3} \right\rceil\).  
1. For each array element \(a_i\), compute the reduced key \(b_i = a_i / m\); each \(b_i\) is an integer in \([0, 2^N / m] \subseteq [0, N^3]\).  
2. Now sort the array \(b\) using counting sort (or any linear-time integer sorting):  
   - Initialize a count array \(C[0 \dots N^3]\) with zeros.  
   - For each \(b_i\), increment \(C[b_i]\).  
   - Compute prefix sums of \(C\) to get positions.  
   - Place each \(b_i\) into its correct position in the output array using \(C\).  
3. Finally, map back: replace each sorted \(b_i\) by \(a_i = b_i \cdot m\) to obtain the sorted original array.  
This runs in \(O(k + N^3)\) time and uses \(O(N^3)\) extra space.

Let \(M = \left\lceil\frac{2^N}{N^3}\right\rceil\).  
1. Replace each array element \(a_i\) by \(b_i = a_i / M\); all \(b_i\) are integers and lie in \([0, \lfloor 2^N / M \rfloor] \subseteq [0, N^3]\).  
2. Use counting sort on the array \(b\), since the value range is at most \(N^3 + 1\):  
   - Allocate an array `count[0..N^3]`, initialized to 0.  
   - For each \(b_i\), increment `count[b_i]`.  
   - Scan `count` from 0 to \(N^3\), and for each value \(v\) with frequency `count[v]`, output `count[v]` copies of \(v\) into a new array \(b'\).  
3. Convert back to the original scale: for each \(b'_i\), set \(a'_i = b'_i \cdot M\).  
4. The resulting array \(a'\) is the sorted version of the original, and the running time is \(O(k + N^3)\).

Let \(M = \left\lceil \frac{2^N}{N^3} \right\rceil\); each array element is of the form \(a_i = M \cdot b_i\), where \(0 \le a_i \le 2^N\), hence \(0 \le b_i \le \left\lfloor \frac{2^N}{M} \right\rfloor \le N^3\).  
1. Transform the array in-place by dividing each element by \(M\): for all \(i\), set \(b_i = a_i / M\).  
2. Now all \(b_i\) are integers in \([0, N^3]\), so define a counting array `count[0..N^3]` initialized to 0.  
3. For each \(b_i\), increment `count[b_i]`.  
4. Reconstruct the sorted sequence of \(b_i\) by scanning `count` from 0 to \(N^3\) and writing each value \(j\) exactly `count[j]` times into the array.  
5. Multiply back by \(M\): for all \(i\), set \(a_i = b_i \cdot M\); now \(a_i\) are sorted.  

This algorithm runs in \(O(k + N^3)\) time and uses \(O(N^3)\) extra space.

Use the large minimum distance to bucket and then direct-address.  

1. Let \(M = \left\lceil \frac{2^n}{n^3} \right\rceil\).  
2. Create an array of buckets \(B[0 \dots \lfloor 2^n / M \rfloor]\), each initially empty.  
3. For each input number \(x\), compute its bucket index \(b = \lfloor x / M \rfloor\).  
4. Because any two numbers differ by at least \(M\), no bucket will contain more than one number; store \(x\) directly in \(B[b]\).  
5. Finally, scan \(B\) from index 0 to the end and output the stored numbers in order, skipping empty entries.  

This runs in \(O(2^n / n^3 + k)\) time and uses \(O(2^n / n^3)\) space, and produces the sorted array.

Use a radix sort on the binary representations, e.g., bucket sort by chunks of bits.  
Let \(m = \lceil \log_2(n^3) \rceil\), so each gap is at least \(2^m\); split each number \(x\) into high and low parts: \(x = H(x)\cdot 2^m + L(x)\), where \(H(x) = \lfloor x / 2^m \rfloor\) and \(0 \le L(x) < 2^m\).  
Because differences are at least \(2^m\), no two distinct numbers can share the same high part \(H(x)\); thus sorting by \(H(x)\) alone orders the numbers correctly.  
Construct an array of pairs \((H(x_i), x_i)\) and sort these pairs using counting sort on \(H(x_i)\), since \(H(x_i) \in [0, 2^{n-m})\).  
The counting array size is \(2^{n-m} \le \frac{2^n}{2^m} \le \frac{2^n}{2^{\log_2(n^3)}} = \frac{2^n}{n^3}\).  
Because the minimum gap is also \(\ge \frac{2^n}{n^3}\), we have \(k \le \frac{2^n}{n^3} + 1\), so the counting array size is \(O(k)\).  
Thus counting sort on \(H(x)\) runs in \(O(k)\) time and space, yielding the indices in sorted order of \(H(x)\), and hence of \(x\).  
Finally, output the \(x_i\) in the order determined by the sorted \(H(x_i)\); this is the sorted array.

Use a bucket-based algorithm exploiting the large minimum gap.  

1. Let `G = ceil(2^n / n^3)`.  
2. Create an array of buckets `B[0..k-1]`, each initially empty.  
3. For each element `x` in the input, compute its bucket index `i = floor(x / G)` and insert `x` into `B[i]`.  
4. Because any two numbers differ by at least `G`, no two elements will map to the same bucket, so each nonempty bucket contains exactly one element.  
5. Scan the buckets `B[0..k-1]` in increasing index order and output the elements from nonempty buckets in that order.  
6. This runs in `O(k + 2^n / G)` time, but `2^n / G = O(n^3)`, so the running time is `O(k + n^3)`, which is polynomial and independent of the numeric magnitude of the integers.

Use a bucket-based counting approach exploiting the large minimum gap.  

1. Let \(M = 2^n\) and \(L = \left\lceil \frac{M}{n^3} \right\rceil\).  
2. Create an array `bucket` of length \(B = \left\lceil \frac{M+1}{L} \right\rceil = O(n^3)\), initialized with a special “empty” marker.  
3. For each input number \(x\), compute its bucket index \(b = \lfloor x / L \rfloor\) and store \(x\) in `bucket[b]`.  
4. Because any two numbers differ by at least \(L\), no two distinct numbers can fall in the same bucket, so there are no collisions.  
5. Finally, scan `bucket` from index 0 to \(B-1\) and output the non-empty entries in order; this yields the sorted array.  
6. Time complexity is \(O(k + n^3)\), and space complexity is \(O(n^3)\), both polynomial in \(n\).

Use the large minimum gap to bucket the numbers, then sort within buckets:

1. Let \(D = \left\lceil \frac{2^n}{n^3} \right\rceil\) and create an array `B` of size about \(\lceil 2^n / D \rceil \le n^3 + 1\), initialized with “empty” markers.  
2. For each input element \(x\), compute its bucket index \(b = \lfloor x / D \rfloor\).  
3. Because any two distinct numbers differ by at least \(D\), no bucket will receive more than one element; if `B[b]` is already occupied, they must be equal (handle duplicates if needed).  
4. Store \(x\) in `B[b]`.  
5. Finally, scan `B` from index 0 to the end and output all non-empty entries in order.  

This runs in \(O(k + n^3)\) time, which is polynomial in \(n\) and linear in the input size up to the \(n^3\) factor, and it correctly produces the sorted order using the spacing property.

1. Use a direct-address style array (bucket array) `B` indexed by the possible values `0..2^n`.  
2. Initialize `B` with a special marker (e.g., `null`) for all positions.  
3. For each input number `x` in the given array, set `B[x] = x`.  
4. Because all numbers differ by at least `ceil(2^n / n^3)`, there are at most `n^3 + 1` distinct numbers, so this is feasible if `2^n` is not too large for memory.  
5. After inserting all numbers, traverse `B` from index `0` to `2^n` in increasing order.  
6. Whenever `B[i]` is not `null`, output `B[i]` to the result array.  
7. The resulting output sequence is sorted in non-decreasing order.  
8. Time complexity: `O(2^n + k)`; space complexity: `O(2^n)`.

1. Use a sparse bucket-sort style algorithm exploiting the large guaranteed gaps.  
2. Let `G = ceil(2^n / n^3)` and define a hash function `h(x) = floor(x / G)`.  
3. Because any two numbers differ by at least `G`, all array elements map to distinct hash values, so there are no collisions.  
4. Create an array (e.g., a hash table or direct-address table) `B` indexed by `h(x)`.  
5. Initialize all entries of `B` to “empty.”  
6. For each element `a[i]`, compute `p = h(a[i])` and store `a[i]` in `B[p]`.  
7. Finally, scan `B` in increasing index order and output all non-empty entries in order.  
8. This yields the input numbers in sorted order.  
9. Time complexity is `O(k + M)` where `M` is the range of used indices, and since `k ≤ n^3 + 1` (by the spacing constraint), this is efficient.

Yes, one can merge two AVL trees of total size \(n\) in \(O(n)\) time, assuming they contain disjoint, totally ordered keys.  
1. Convert each AVL tree to a sorted array by performing an in-order traversal; this takes \(O(n)\) time total.  
2. Merge the two sorted arrays into a single sorted array in \(O(n)\) time using the standard two-pointer merge procedure.  
3. Build a height-balanced binary search tree (which is in fact an AVL tree) from the merged sorted array in \(O(n)\) time by recursively choosing the middle element as root, then building left and right subtrees from the left and right halves.  
4. Because each step (two traversals, one merge, one build) is linear in the total number of nodes, the whole algorithm runs in \(O(n)\) time.  
5. The constructed tree is a valid AVL tree because it is perfectly height-balanced (or as balanced as possible), and the BST property holds due to the sorted-array construction.

Yes, two AVL trees of total size \(n\) can be merged in \(O(n)\) time if all keys in one are smaller than all keys in the other.  
1. Perform an inorder traversal of each AVL tree to obtain two sorted arrays \(A\) and \(B\); each traversal takes \(O(n)\) time.  
2. Merge the two sorted arrays into one sorted array \(C\) in linear time \(O(n)\).  
3. Build a height-balanced (AVL) tree from the sorted array \(C\) by recursively choosing the middle element as the root, and doing the same for the left and right subarrays.  
4. This construction step takes \(O(n)\) time because each element is visited a constant number of times and no rebalancing is needed.  
5. The total time is \(O(n) + O(n) + O(n) = O(n)\), and the resulting tree is an AVL tree of size \(2n\).  
6. Therefore, merging two AVL trees can be done in \(O(n)\) time under the given key-ordering assumption.

Yes, two AVL trees of size \(n\) can be merged in \(O(n)\) time if all keys in one tree are smaller than all keys in the other.  
1. Perform an in-order traversal of each AVL tree to obtain two sorted arrays \(A\) and \(B\); this takes \(O(n)\) time.  
2. Merge the two sorted arrays into a single sorted array \(C\) of size \(2n\) using the standard merge procedure in \(O(n)\) time.  
3. Build a height-balanced (AVL) tree from the sorted array \(C\) by recursively choosing the middle element as the root, and building left/right subtrees from the left/right halves; this also takes \(O(n)\) time because each node is created and linked once.  
4. Since each step is \(O(n)\), the total time is \(O(n)\).  
5. The resulting tree is height-balanced by construction (heights of left and right subtrees of every node differ by at most 1), so it is a valid AVL tree.  
6. Therefore, merging two AVL trees of size \(n\) can be done in linear time under the assumption of non-overlapping key ranges.

Yes, it is possible to merge two AVL trees of total size \(n\) in \(O(n)\) time, assuming both are standard BSTs ordered by key and all keys in one are smaller than all keys in the other.  
1. Perform an inorder traversal of each AVL tree to extract the keys (and associated data) into two sorted arrays \(A\) and \(B\); this takes \(O(n)\) time in total.  
2. Merge the two sorted arrays \(A\) and \(B\) into a single sorted array \(C\) of size \(n\) in \(O(n)\) time using the standard merge procedure.  
3. Build a perfectly (or height-balanced) BST from the sorted array \(C\) by recursively choosing the middle element as the root, then building left and right subtrees from the left and right halves; this can be done in \(O(n)\) time and yields a height-balanced tree.  
4. Traverse this balanced BST bottom‑up to annotate and/or adjust the AVL balance factors if needed, which is also \(O(n)\).  
5. The resulting tree is a valid AVL tree containing all elements from both original trees, and the overall procedure runs in \(\Theta(n)\) time.

Yes, it is possible to merge two AVL trees of total size \(n\) in \(O(n)\) time, assuming all keys in one are smaller than all keys in the other (or after a linear-time partition by value).  
1. Perform an inorder traversal of each AVL tree to extract their elements into two sorted arrays; this takes \(O(n)\) total time.  
2. Merge the two sorted arrays into a single sorted array in \(O(n)\) time (standard merge procedure).  
3. Build a height-balanced (AVL) tree from this sorted array in \(O(n)\) time by recursively choosing the middle element as the root, then building left and right subtrees from the left and right halves.  
4. The resulting tree is height-balanced, and you can compute and store the heights/balance factors during construction in \(O(1)\) per node.  
5. The overall running time is \(O(n) + O(n) + O(n) = O(n)\), and the final tree is a valid AVL tree containing all keys from both input trees.

Yes, two AVL trees of total size \(n\) can be merged in \(O(n)\) time if all keys in one are smaller than all keys in the other.  
1. Perform an inorder traversal of each AVL tree to extract its elements into two sorted arrays \(A\) and \(B\) in \(O(n)\) time.  
2. Merge the two sorted arrays into a single sorted array \(C\) in \(O(n)\) time using the standard linear-time merge procedure.  
3. Build a height-balanced BST (which will be an AVL tree) from the sorted array \(C\) in \(O(n)\) time by recursively choosing the middle element as root, middle of left half as left child’s root, and so on.  
4. This construction yields an AVL tree because at each step the subtree sizes differ by at most one, so the height difference between left and right subtrees is at most 1.  
5. Each step is linear: two traversals \(O(n)\), one merge \(O(n)\), and one balanced build \(O(n)\), so the total time is \(O(n)\).  
6. Therefore, merging two AVL trees of total size \(n\) into a new AVL tree is indeed possible in \(O(n)\) time under the ordering assumption.

Yes, it is possible to merge two AVL trees of total size \(n\) in \(O(n)\) time, provided all keys in one are less than all keys in the other (otherwise, an extra linear-time extraction/split is needed).  
1. First, perform an inorder traversal of each AVL tree to extract its keys (and optionally values) into two sorted arrays \(A\) and \(B\); this takes \(O(n)\) time total.  
2. Merge the two sorted arrays into a single sorted array \(C\) of size \(n\) using the standard linear merge step from mergesort; this takes \(O(n)\) time.  
3. Rebuild an AVL tree from the sorted array \(C\) in \(O(n)\): recursively choose the middle element of the current segment as the root, build the left subtree from the left half and the right subtree from the right half, and set heights accordingly.  
4. Each array element becomes a node exactly once, and each recursive step does \(O(1)\) work, so the tree construction is linear.  
5. The total running time is \(O(n)\) for traversals + \(O(n)\) for merging arrays + \(O(n)\) for building the AVL, i.e., \(O(n)\) overall.  
6. The resulting tree is a valid AVL tree because it is built as a perfectly balanced BST (or nearly perfectly balanced when \(n\) is not of the form \(2^k - 1\)), hence satisfies the AVL height-balance property.

No, two arbitrary AVL trees of size \(n\) cannot in general be merged in \(O(\sqrt{n})\) time.  
Each AVL tree has height \(\Theta(\log n)\), and merging them while preserving the AVL invariants effectively requires finding a place to attach one tree into the other and then rebalancing along a path of length \(\Theta(\log n)\).  
Known optimal algorithms for merging balanced search trees (including AVL trees) when all keys of one are smaller than all keys of the other run in \(O(\log n)\) time, not better, by descending only one root-to-leaf path and doing \(O(1)\) rebalancing per level.  
To get total time \(O(\sqrt{n})\) for merging two size-\(n\) trees, you would need \(o(\log n)\) work per tree or some sublogarithmic structural adjustment, which is impossible because any comparison-based algorithm must at least distinguish among the \(\binom{2n}{n}\) possible interleavings of keys.  
This implies a lower bound of \(\Omega(n)\) or at best \(\Omega(\log n)\) operations for correctly positioning and rebalancing elements when the trees are arbitrary.  
In particular, even if all keys in one tree are less than those in the other, merging in less than \(\Omega(\log n)\) time would contradict standard comparison-based BST lower bounds.  
Therefore an \(O(\sqrt{n})\)-time merge is not possible in the worst case; the optimal asymptotic bound is \(\Theta(\log n)\) under the usual AVL model.

No, two arbitrary AVL trees of size Θ(n) cannot in general be merged in O(√n) time.  
Any merge operation must, at minimum, traverse or “touch” enough nodes to distinguish between Θ(n) different possible input trees and resulting merged shapes.
