However, in O(√n) time you can only visit O(√n) nodes, which is not enough information to determine the correct structure for all possible inputs of size n.  
More concretely, there exist inputs where the height of the resulting AVL tree is Θ(log n), and balancing rotations/insertions propagate from the “join point” up to the root, requiring work on Θ(log n) nodes.  
But even ignoring balance-fixing, merging two sorted sets of size n from comparison-based structures requires Ω(n) comparisons in the worst case to be correct for all inputs, by standard decision-tree arguments.  
Thus any algorithm that merges two arbitrary size-n AVL trees into a single correct AVL tree must take Ω(n) time in the worst case.  
Since Ω(n) ∉ O(√n), an O(√n) merge algorithm for all size-n AVL trees cannot exist.

No, two arbitrary AVL trees of size \(n\) each cannot be merged in worst-case time \(O(\sqrt{n})\).  
Any merge must at least *identify* or *process* enough nodes to build a valid AVL tree containing all \(2n\) keys in sorted order.  
In the worst case, the keys of the two trees are interleaved (e.g., all keys of \(T_1\) are the odd numbers, all keys of \(T_2\) are the even numbers), so no subtree of one tree can simply be reused as a whole in the final tree.  
A standard lower bound argument: to determine the relative order of all \(2n\) distinct keys from two interleaved sorted sets, one needs \(\Omega(n)\) comparisons (this is essentially merging two sorted sequences).  
Each comparison in a comparison-based model inspects at most one key from each tree and cannot “skip” large parts without knowing their ordering relative to the other tree’s keys.  
Thus any correct algorithm must perform \(\Omega(n)\) work in the worst case, regardless of how pointers are rearranged.  
Known optimal methods (e.g., extract all keys by inorder traversals and merge arrays, then rebuild an AVL tree) run in \(O(n)\) time and are asymptotically optimal.  
Therefore, an \(O(\sqrt{n})\)-time merge of two arbitrary size-\(n\) AVL trees is impossible in the standard comparison-based model.

No, in the worst case two AVL trees of size \(n\) cannot be merged in \(O(\sqrt{n})\) time.  
Any comparison-based algorithm that merges two sets of \(n\) distinct keys into a single sorted structure must spend \(\Omega(n)\) time in the worst case, since the output contains \(2n\) ordered elements and at least \(\Omega(n)\) comparisons are required to determine their relative order.  
More specifically, consider the case where the sets are disjoint and we must produce a valid AVL tree containing all \(2n\) keys: the sorted order of those \(2n\) keys is not known a priori and cannot be inferred without considering essentially all of them.  
If you attempt to merge by inserting all nodes of one tree into the other using standard AVL insert (each in \(O(\log n)\)), you obtain \(O(n \log n)\) time.  
Even the best known “join” or “split-and-join” methods on balanced BSTs work in \(O(m \log(\frac{n}{m} + 1))\), where \(m \le n\) is the smaller tree, which is \(\Omega(n)\) when \(m = n\).  
Thus any correct merge procedure for two size-\(n\) AVL trees requires \(\Omega(n)\) time, so \(O(\sqrt{n})\) is impossible.

No, in general two arbitrary AVL trees of size \(n\) cannot be merged in \(O(\sqrt{n})\) time in the worst case.  
Each AVL tree has height \(\Theta(\log n)\), and any comparison-based algorithm must at least “touch” (traverse or compare) enough nodes to determine the relative ordering of elements in both trees.  
If the key ranges are interleaved, one essentially has to perform an operation equivalent to merging two sorted lists of size \(n\), which has a known lower bound of \(\Omega(n)\) comparisons in the worst case.  
Even if you try to convert one tree to an array (inorder) and then insert into the other, the inorder traversal alone costs \(\Theta(n)\), and bulk insertion while maintaining AVL balance also costs at least \(\Omega(n)\).  
More formally, comparison-based set union (or merge) of two size-\(n\) sets drawn from a totally ordered universe has decision-tree lower bound \(\Omega(n)\), and AVL-tree operations are comparison-based.  
Since \(\Omega(n)\) is asymptotically larger than \(O(\sqrt{n})\), no algorithm can guarantee \(O(\sqrt{n})\) worst-case time for merging two size-\(n\) AVL trees while preserving correctness and balance.

No, two arbitrary AVL trees of size n each cannot be merged in O(√n) time in the worst case.  
Any comparison-based algorithm that merges two sets of n keys into one ordered set of size 2n must perform Ω(n) key comparisons in the worst case, by the standard lower bound for merging two sorted sequences.  
The in-order traversals of the two AVL trees give two sorted lists of size n, and any merge operation that preserves the BST order must be able to merge those two lists.  
Thus, even ignoring the structural/balancing work on the trees, the information-theoretic lower bound for merging the key sets is Ω(n).  
Therefore O(√n) time is asymptotically impossible for merging two arbitrary AVL trees of size n.

No, in general two AVL trees of size Θ(n) cannot be merged in O(√n) time.  
Any correct merge procedure must inspect or move Ω(n) keys in the worst case, because all n elements of one tree must be integrated into the other while preserving both the BST order and AVL height balance.  
If we treat the two AVL trees as sets A and B, their union must contain all elements of A ∪ B, so every element of at least one tree must be copied, relinked, or traversed to ensure correct placement and rebalancing.  
More formally, suppose such an O(√n) merge existed: then we could sort an arbitrary array of n distinct keys in o(n log n) time by first building n singleton AVL trees and repeatedly merging them in a tournament, contradicting the comparison‐sorting lower bound of Ω(n log n).  
Therefore, any comparison-based algorithm that merges two balanced search trees of size Θ(n) requires Ω(n) time in the worst case, so O(√n) is impossible.

Use an array plus a bucketed “amount → id-list” structure with a global max pointer.

1. Keep an array `A[1..3N]` where `A[i]` = current amount for id `i` (0 if absent).  
2. Maintain an array of buckets `B[0..M]`, where bucket `B[a]` stores a linked list of all ids with `amount = a` (M is a known upper bound on any amount).  
3. For each id, also store a pointer/index to its node in the list in `B[amount]` so we can move it in O(1).  
4. Keep an integer `currentMax` that always stores the maximum amount currently present (0 if structure is empty).  
5. `Add(i,x)`:
   - Let `old = A[i]`; if `old > 0`, remove `i` from bucket `B[old]` in O(1).  
   - Set `new = old + x`, `A[i] = new`, insert `i` into bucket `B[new]` in O(1).  
   - Update `currentMax = max(currentMax, new)` in O(1).  
6. `whoIsMax()`:
   - Return any id from bucket `B[currentMax]` in O(1) (bucket non-empty by definition).  

All operations use O(1) worst-case time, assuming a known upper bound on amounts and direct array access.

Use an array-based “bucketed max-counter” structure keyed by id and by amount.

Data structures:
- An array A[1..3N], where A[i] = current amount of id i (0 if not present).
- An array B[0..N], where B[a] is a doubly linked list of all ids with amount = a.
- An array pos[1..3N] of pointers (or indices) to the node for id i inside its list B[A[i]].
- An integer currentMax storing the maximum amount present.

Initialization: set all A[i] = 0, B[a] empty, pos[i] = null, currentMax = 0.

Add(i, x):  
1) Let old = A[i]; new = old + x; set A[i] = new.  
2) If old > 0, remove i from B[old] using pos[i]; insert i into B[new] and update pos[i].  
3) If old = 0, just insert i into B[new] and set pos[i].  
4) If new > currentMax, set currentMax = new. (All O(1).)

whoIsMax(): while currentMax > 0 and B[currentMax] is empty, decrement currentMax; then return any id from B[currentMax] (or “none” if currentMax = 0).

Use two synchronized hash‐based structures plus a global “current maximum amount” variable.

1. Store elements in an array A[1..3N], where A[i] holds the current amount for id = i (0 if absent).  
2. Maintain a second array/list L of all ids whose amount is equal to the current maximum amount M.  
3. Also maintain a counter cnt of how many ids are currently at amount M.  

Operations:  
• Add(i, x):  
  – If A[i] = 0, insert id i with amount x by setting A[i] = x.  
  – Else set A[i] = A[i] + x.  
  – If A[i] > M, set M = A[i], reset L to contain only i, and cnt = 1.  
  – Else if A[i] = M, append i to L and increment cnt.  
• whoIsMax():  
  – Return any id from L (e.g., L[1]); this is guaranteed to have amount M.  

All lookups and updates are array operations, so both Add and whoIsMax run in worst-case O(1) time.

Use **direct addressing plus a max counter**:

- Maintain an array `A[1..3N]` of integers, where `A[i]` stores the current `amount` for `id = i` (0 if absent).  
- Maintain an integer `currentMax` that always equals the maximum value in `A`.  
- `Add(i, x)`:  
  - Do `A[i] += x`.  
  - If `A[i] > currentMax`, set `currentMax = A[i]`.  
  - This is O(1) worst case.  
- `whoIsMax()`:  
  - Prestore, for each possible `amount` value `v`, a representative `id` with `amount = v`, or track one `id*` that currently attains `currentMax`; update `id*` only when `currentMax` increases.  
  - Then return `id*` in O(1) worst case.

Use two structures:  
1. A direct-access array `A[1..3N]` where each cell stores the current `amount` for id `i` (0 if absent).  
2. A bucket array `B[0..M]` where `B[v]` is a linked list (or dynamic array) of all ids with `amount = v`, and a variable `maxVal` tracking the maximum nonempty bucket index; `M` is an upper bound on any amount value.  

`Add(i, x)`:  
- Let `old = A[i]`; if `old > 0`, remove `i` from list `B[old]`.  
- Set `new = old + x`, store `A[i] = new`, and insert `i` into `B[new]`.  
- If `new > maxVal`, set `maxVal = new`.  

`whoIsMax()`:  
- While `maxVal > 0` and `B[maxVal]` is empty, decrement `maxVal`.  
- Return any `id` from `B[maxVal]`.  

All operations use only array indexing and constant-time list insert/delete, so `Add` and `whoIsMax` run in worst-case O(1) time, given a known bound on `amount`.

Use two arrays plus a running maximum pointer:

1. Maintain an array `A[1..3N]` where `A[i]` stores the current `amount` for `id = i` (0 if absent).  
2. Maintain an array `B[0..M]` of buckets, where bucket `B[v]` is a doubly linked list of all ids with `amount = v` (here `M` is a known upper bound on any `amount`).  
3. Maintain an array `pos[1..3N]` that stores, for each `id`, a pointer (or index) to its node in the bucket list `B[A[i]]`.  
4. Maintain an integer `curMax` storing the current maximum amount such that `B[curMax]` is nonempty (0 if the structure is empty).  
5. `Add(i, x)`:  
   - Let `old = A[i]`; if `old > 0`, delete `i` from bucket `B[old]` using `pos[i]`.  
   - Set `A[i] += x`, insert `i` into bucket `B[A[i]]`, and update `pos[i]` accordingly.  
   - If `A[i] > curMax`, set `curMax = A[i]` (all in O(1)).  
6. `whoIsMax()`: return any `id` from the head of bucket `B[curMax]` in O(1).

Use two arrays and an amortized-reset “epoch” trick.

1. Maintain:
   - `amount[1..3N]`: current amount for each `id` (0 if absent).
   - `present[1..3N]`: boolean flag if the element with that `id` is currently in the structure.
   - `curMaxId`: any id with maximal `amount`; its `amount[curMaxId]` is the current max value.
   - `globalMax`: the current maximum amount.

2. `Add(i, x)` in O(1):
   - If `present[i]` is false, set `present[i] = true` and `amount[i] = x`.
   - Else set `amount[i] += x`.
   - If `amount[i] > globalMax`, set `globalMax = amount[i]` and `curMaxId = i`.

3. `whoIsMax()` in O(1):
   - Just return `curMaxId`.

4. To ensure worst‑case O(1) even under long sequences, bound `amount[i]` by periodically “renormalizing” when `globalMax` exceeds some threshold (e.g., `O(N)`):  
   - Scan all ids once, subtract a fixed offset from every `amount[i]`, recompute `curMaxId` and `globalMax`, and reset the offset.  
   - Amortize this scan by doing O(1) work from it during each future Add call (standard incremental rebuild trick), keeping every single operation worst‑case O(1) while N is the capacity bound.

Use a 3-part structure combining direct indexing with a tournament tree over buckets:

1. Partition the ID range [1,3n] into n buckets, each bucket containing 3 consecutive IDs; store the current `amount` for each ID in a direct array `A[1..3n]`.  
2. For each bucket `b`, maintain `bucketMax[b] = (bestId, bestAmount)` for the 3 IDs in that bucket; this can be recomputed in O(1) time from `A`.  
3. Build a complete binary tree (segment-tree–like) over the n buckets, where each internal node stores the maximum `(bestId, bestAmount)` of its two children; this tree has size O(n), and the root always stores the global maximum, so `whoIsMax()` is O(1).  
4. For `Add(i,x)`: update `A[i] += x`, recompute `bucketMax` for bucket `b = ceil(i/3)` in O(1), but *do not* immediately update the tree; instead, mark bucket `b` as “dirty” in a bitset.  
5. Maintain a potential function as the number of dirty buckets; when a threshold (e.g., ≥ n/2) is reached, rebuild the entire tree bottom‑up from the current `bucketMax` values in O(n) time and clear all dirty bits.  
6. Because each `Add` causes at most one bucket to become dirty, and each rebuild “pays for” O(n) dirtiness, the amortized cost per `Add` is O(1).  
7. For `Reduce(i,y)`: update `A[i] -= y`, recompute `bucketMax` for bucket `b`, and then *immediately* update the tree along the path from bucket `b` to the root (O(log n)) to restore correctness for decreases.  
8. The tree update on `Reduce` is O(log n) worst-case, so amortized O(log n); correctness of `whoIsMax()` is preserved because only decreases are propagated immediately, and increases are delayed but globally corrected during rebuilds.

Use an array for direct access by id, plus one global max-heap for amounts, and perform Reduce lazily with periodic rebuilding.

1. Maintain:
   - An array `A[1..3N]` where `A[i]` stores the current `amount` for id `i` (0 if not present).
   - A max-heap `H` of pairs `(amount, id)`.
   - A counter `m` for the number of successful `Reduce` operations since last rebuild.

2. `Add(i, x)` (amortized O(1)):
   - If `A[i] = 0`, treat as insertion: set `A[i] = x` and insert `(x, i)` into `H`.
   - Else: set `A[i] += x` and insert the new pair `(A[i], i)` into `H`.
   - We allow old heap entries for `i` to become stale; they are ignored later.

3. `whoIsMax()` (worst-case O(1)):
   - While the heap’s top `(val, id)` has `val ≠ A[id]` (stale), pop it.
   - The top is then consistent: return its `id`.

4. `Reduce(i, y)` (amortized O(log n)):
   - Set `A[i] -= y` (can become smaller but assume problem keeps it nonnegative or we delete when it hits 0).
   - Insert updated `(A[i], i)` into `H`.
   - Increment `m`; when `m = N`, rebuild: clear `H` and insert `(A[i], i)` only for ids where `A[i] > 0`, then set `m = 0`.
   - Each element participates in rebuild O(1) times, so rebuild cost spreads to O(1) per operation, and the heap operations in `Reduce` give the amortized O(log n).

Use an array-based bucketing structure over possible `amount` values together with an index by `id`.

1. Maintain:
   - An array `A[1..3N]` where `A[i]` stores the current `amount` for `id = i` (0 if absent).
   - A doubly linked list of *nonempty amount-buckets*, each bucket representing a distinct amount value.
   - For each `amount` value that currently appears, a bucket node containing a linked list of all ids with that `amount`.
   - A hash/array `bucketPtr[amount]` pointing to the corresponding bucket node (or null).
   - A pointer `maxBucket` to the bucket with largest amount (by value).

2. `whoIsMax()` is `O(1)`: return any `id` from `maxBucket`.

3. `Add(i,x)`:
   - Let `old = A[i]` (0 means new element). Set `new = old + x`, update `A[i]`.
   - If `old > 0`, remove `i` from bucket `old`; if bucket `old` becomes empty, delete that bucket and clear `bucketPtr[old]`.
   - Insert `i` into bucket `new`, creating the bucket if needed, updating `bucketPtr[new]`.
   - If `new` is greater than current max amount, move `maxBucket` to `new`.
   - Each insertion/deletion into bucket lists is `O(1)`, and each `A[i]` increases total amount at most `O(#Adds)`, giving amortized `O(1)` (since bucket creation/deletion across all operations is bounded).

4. `Reduce(i,y)`:
   - Let `old = A[i]`, set `new = old - y`, update `A[i]`.
   - Remove `i` from bucket `old`; if bucket `old` becomes empty, delete it and (if it was `maxBucket`) move `maxBucket` to the previous nonempty bucket in the ordered list of buckets.
   - If `new > 0`, insert `i` into bucket `new`, creating bucket if needed and updating `bucketPtr[new]`.
   - Since each `Reduce` only walks a constant number of bucket nodes and bucket operations are `O(1)`, but we may need to search at most `O(log N)` distinct amounts over many reduces (using a balanced search tree or ordered list of active amounts), this yields amortized `O(log N)` time for `Reduce`.

Use two arrays plus a bucketed max-structure on the range of possible IDs.

1. Maintain an array `A[1..3N]`, where `A[i]` holds the current `amount` for `id = i` (0 means “not present”).  
2. Maintain a doubly linked list of IDs for each possible amount “bucket”; let `B[0..M]` be an array of bucket heads, where `M` is an upper bound on any amount. Each bucket `B[a]` stores all IDs whose `amount = a`.  
3. Also store an array `pos[1..3N]` of pointers into the lists so we can remove/move an `id` from its current bucket in O(1).  
4. Maintain a variable `maxAmount` storing the largest `a` such that `B[a]` is nonempty; `whoIsMax()` returns any ID from bucket `B[maxAmount]` in O(1).  
5. `Add(i,x)`:  
   - If `A[i] = 0`, insert `i` into bucket `B[x]`, set `A[i] = x`, update `maxAmount = max(maxAmount, x)`.  
   - Else, remove `i` from `B[A[i]]`, set `A[i] += x`, insert `i` into `B[A[i]]`, and update `maxAmount`.  
   This is O(1) worst case.  
6. `Reduce(i,y)`:  
   - Remove `i` from bucket `B[A[i]]`, set `A[i] -= y`, and if `A[i] > 0` insert `i` into `B[A[i]]` else delete it.  
   - If the old bucket was `maxAmount` and is now empty, decrease `maxAmount` stepwise until reaching a nonempty bucket.  
   Since `maxAmount` can only move down at most O(N) times over all operations and never goes above the maximum amount ever created, the total cost of all such decreases is O(N), giving amortized O(log N) time if the maximum amount is polynomially bounded in N (or O(1) amortized if it’s O(N)).

Use two structures together:

1. **Array for direct access (size 3n)**  
   Maintain an array `A[1..3n]`, where each entry stores a pointer (or index) to a node in a max-heap, or `null` if the id is absent.  
   This gives O(1) access to the heap node for a given id.

2. **Bucketed max-heap (or binary max-heap + lazy updates)**  
   Store all existing elements `(id, amount)` in a max-heap keyed by `amount`; the root always gives `whoIsMax()` in O(1).  
   Also keep the “real” `amount[i]` in an array so that heap keys may temporarily be stale; periodically fix them.

3. **Add(i, x) in amortized O(1)**  
   Access `A[i]` in O(1); if null, create a node `(id=i, amount=x)` with heap key = `amount[i] = x`, insert in the heap, and store its pointer in `A[i]`.  
   If not null, just set `amount[i] += x` in the array and *do not* immediately update the heap key (lazy increase).  
   Each node’s key can only lag behind by a bounded factor; total cost of eventual heap-key fixes can be amortized to O(1) per Add.

4. **whoIsMax() in O(1) worst-case**  
   While the heap’s root key is smaller than its real `amount[id]`, update the key and re-heapify (pop/push) until the root’s key matches its real amount.  
   Since each heap adjustment is charged to earlier lazy Add/Reduce operations, the amortized overhead stays O(1), and each `whoIsMax()` call itself does a constant expected amount of work.

5. **Reduce(i, y) in amortized O(log n)**  
   Access `A[i]` in O(1); decrease `amount[i]` by `y` in the array.  
   If the decrease is large enough to invalidate the heap order (current heap key > new `amount[i]` by some threshold), update the heap key for that node and perform a standard decrease-key operation in O(log n).  
   By charging these re-heaps to the reductions, the amortized cost of Reduce becomes O(log n).

Use three components:

1. **An array of positions:**  
   `pos[id]` in `[0..3N]` stores the index of the element with that `id` in a heap array (or 0 / -1 if absent).  
   This gives O(1) access to the element’s position by `id`.

2. **A max-heap (by amount):**  
   Keep all present elements in a binary max-heap `H[1..k]`, each node storing `(id, amount)`.  
   Then `whoIsMax()` is just `return H[1].id` in O(1) time.

3. **Lazy-decrease with rebuild:**  
   - `Add(i,x)`:  
     • If `pos[i] = 0`, create new node `(i, x)`; insert at end of `H` and set `pos[i]=k`. Do **not** heapify up; just append.  
     • If `pos[i] ≠ 0`, update its `amount += x` in-place; again do **not** heapify.  
     Thus each Add is O(1) amortized.
   - `Reduce(i,y)`:  
     • Find index `p = pos[i]`; set `amount[i] -= y`.  
     • Perform a standard heapify-down (or up/down as needed) from position `p` in the max-heap, taking O(log n).  
   - **Periodically rebuild the heap** every Θ(n) total updates (e.g., when the number of “dirty” nodes or size deviates by a factor of 2):  
     • Re-heapify the entire array of current elements in O(n) time using Floyd’s build-heap.  
     • Reset all `pos[id]` accordingly.  
     Amortizing this O(n) rebuild over Θ(n) operations gives O(1) extra per operation.

This structure gives: Add in amortized O(1), Reduce in amortized O(log n), and whoIsMax in worst-case O(1).

Use an array of buckets indexed by possible amounts together with a direct index from ids to their current bucket and amount.

1. Maintain:
   - An array `A[1..3N]` storing for each `id` its current `amount` (or 0 if absent).
   - Buckets `B[0..M]` where `B[v]` is a doubly linked list of all ids with `amount = v`.  
   - For each `id`, a pointer (or index) into its node in the list of `B[A[id]]`.
   - A variable `maxAmount` storing the current maximum amount with a nonempty bucket.

2. `whoIsMax()`:
   - Return any id from `B[maxAmount]`; this is O(1).

3. `Add(i, x)` (amortized O(1)):
   - Let `old = A[i]` (0 if new), set `new = old + x`, update `A[i] = new`.
   - If `old > 0`, remove `i` from bucket `B[old]` using its stored pointer in O(1).
   - Insert `i` into the head of `B[new]` in O(1).
   - Update `maxAmount = max(maxAmount, new)` in O(1).

4. `Reduce(i, y)` (amortized O(log n)):
   - Let `old = A[i]`, `new = old − y`, update `A[i] = new`.
   - Remove `i` from bucket `B[old]` in O(1).
   - If `new > 0`, insert `i` into `B[new]` in O(1); if `new = 0`, the element is effectively deleted.
   - If `old = maxAmount` and `B[old]` is now empty, scan downward to find the next nonempty bucket; this total downward scanning over all operations is O(N), so amortized O(1) per `Reduce`.
   - To ensure amortized O(log n) for `Reduce` even if amounts can grow large, rebuild (renormalize) the bucket index structure when `maxAmount` exceeds a threshold (e.g., compress/scale amounts or rebuild in O(N)), which is done rarely enough that its cost spreads to O(log n) amortized per `Reduce`.

If all three operations were supported in O(1) expected amortized time, we could solve a known strictly harder problem (dynamic maximum with arbitrary key changes) in O(1), contradicting lower bounds from the cell-probe model.  
Consider an input sequence where ids are fixed in advance, but the amounts change adversarially as a function of past randomness and answers.  
The core difficulty is that Reduce(i, y) can decrease the current maximum arbitrarily, forcing the data structure to “know” where the new maximum is without scanning many elements or having additional structure updated in more than O(1) time.  
Any auxiliary information (like maintaining candidates for the maximum) must be updated in response to every Add/Reduce, but an adversary can force these updates to propagate to Ω(log n) or more elements unless the structure has very large redundancy.  
Formally, in the cell-probe model with word size Θ(log n), dynamic predecessor / rank / selection / max-with-updates all have known Ω(log n / log log n) (or similar) lower bounds even with randomization and amortization.  
Our problem generalizes dynamic maximum with arbitrary increases/decreases, which in turn can encode dynamic prefix sums and dynamic order statistics, inheriting these lower bounds.  
Randomization does not help: Yao’s minimax principle converts any randomized O(1) expected-time scheme into a deterministic scheme that is O(1) on average over a hard distribution, contradicting those deterministic lower bounds.  
Thus, at least one of Add, Reduce, or whoIsMax must take super-constant amortized time in the worst case, so an O(1)-amortized implementation of all three operations is impossible.

Such a structure would give an \(O(1)\)-amortized randomized algorithm for the dynamic prefix maximum (or dynamic order statistics) problem, which is known to require superconstant time per operation in the cell-probe model.  
Use the id range \([1,3n]\) to encode array indices and simulate updates and queries on an array \(A[1..n]\):  
represent each prefix maximum query \(\max_{1\le j\le k} A[j]\) as \(\mathrm{whoIsMax}()\) on IDs whose cumulative amounts encode the array values, and represent array updates \(A[i] \leftarrow A[i] + \Delta\) via \(\mathrm{Add}(i,\Delta)\) / \(\mathrm{Reduce}(i,\Delta)\).  
If all three operations were \(O(1)\) amortized (even randomized), this would contradict lower bounds from dynamic data-structure complexity (cell-probe) that show any structure supporting both updates and queries of such “max / order” information must sometimes spend \(\Omega(\log n / \log\log n)\) time per operation.  
Equivalently, a fully \(O(1)\)-time implementation would allow constant-time maintenance of a dynamic maximum under arbitrary point increments/decrements using only \(O(N)\) space, which would imply a constant-time solution to problems known to have superconstant lower bounds under standard word-RAM / cell-probe assumptions.  
Thus, even allowing randomization and amortization, no such data structure can exist.

If all three operations ran in O(1) amortized time, we could solve an information-theoretically larger problem with too few bits of memory access.  
Consider an initially empty structure and perform N operations of the form Add(i,1) where each i ∈ [1,3N] and all ids used are distinct; this sequence encodes a subset S ⊆ [1,3N] of size N.  
There are \(\binom{3N}{N} \approx 2^{H(1/3)\cdot 3N}\) possible such subsets, requiring Θ(N) *constant* bits per operation to distinguish, but any O(1)-time (even amortized, even randomized) word-RAM algorithm can only read/write O(1)·w bits per operation, where w = Θ(log N).  
Now repeatedly call whoIsMax() followed by Reduce(i,1) on the returned id until the structure is empty; this sequence reveals exactly S.  
Thus the algorithm must, in expectation, “encode” an element of a set of size \(\binom{3N}{N}\) using only O(N log N) total bit probes, which is asymptotically fewer than the information contained in all possible S.  
Yao’s minimax principle then implies that some input distribution forces Ω(log N) expected time per operation for any randomized algorithm.  
Equivalently, the problem implicitly stores an unordered subset of size N from [1,3N] with constant-time update and max-queries, which exceeds the entropy that can be handled with constant-time access to a word-RAM of polynomial size.  
Therefore, at least one of Add, whoIsMax, or Reduce must take ω(1) amortized expected time; an O(1)-amortized implementation of all three (even randomized) is impossible.

Such a data structure would imply an \(O(1)\)-time algorithm (amortized, even randomized) for the dynamic prefix-sum/partial-sum problem, which is known to be impossible in the cell-probe (or RAM) model.  
Map each id \(i \in [1,3n]\) to a position in an array and interpret amount[i] as the prefix sum at that position; then Add and Reduce simulate point updates, and whoIsMax solves a global query about the updated array.  
By standard reductions (e.g., from dynamic prefix sums, partial sums, or dynamic set-disjointness) one can encode sufficiently many bits of information in the sequence of updates that answering queries in worst-case or amortized \(O(1)\) time would contradict known lower bounds on cell-probe complexity.  
In particular, dynamic prefix sums have a lower bound of \(\Omega(\log n)\) per operation (even randomized, amortized) when memory cells have \(O(\log n)\) bits.  
If our structure gave \(O(1)\) amortized time for Add, Reduce, and whoIsMax, we could implement such a dynamic prefix-sum structure with \(O(1)\) amortized time per operation.  
This would violate those lower bounds, so such an implementation cannot exist.

If all three operations had O(1) amortized expected time, we could violate known lower bounds for fundamental problems.  
Consider only Add and whoIsMax: this is exactly the “dictionary with predecessor/successor-like query” or “dynamic order statistics” problem restricted to the maximum; in the cell-probe model with word size Θ(log N), any dynamic structure that both updates and queries order information in O(1) expected time would imply O(1) expected time for comparison-based selection and sorting.  
Using such a structure on N distinct keys, we could sort in expected O(N) time, contradicting the Ω(N log N) lower bound for comparison-based sorting.  
Randomization does not help here: the Ω(N log N) bound holds for randomized algorithms as well (Yao’s minimax principle).  
More formally, to keep track of the maximum under arbitrary inserts and arbitrary reductions of arbitrary items, the structure must maintain enough information about the relative order of amounts that, in the worst case, encodes a permutation of N items and must be “learned” via comparisons or probes.  
The information-theoretic lower bound on the number of comparisons/probes needed to distinguish N! permutations is Ω(N log N), which is incompatible with O(1) per operation over Θ(N) operations.  
Thus at least one of Add, Reduce, or whoIsMax must cost ω(1) amortized expected time (on a RAM/cell-probe model with word size Θ(log N)), so implementing all of them in O(1) amortized time is impossible.

If all three operations had O(1) amortized time (even randomized), we could solve a known harder problem in O(1) amortized time, contradicting standard lower bounds.  
Consider using the structure to maintain the maximum of a dynamic array \(A[1..3N]\) under point increments/decrements: store an element with `id = i` and `amount = A[i]`, and never delete.  
Then `Add(i,x)` emulates \(A[i] \mathrel{+}= x\), `Reduce(i,y)` emulates \(A[i] \mathrel{-}= y\), and `whoIsMax()` returns an index achieving \(\max A[i]\).  
So an O(1) implementation would give O(1)-time update and query for dynamic range-maximum, which is impossible in the cell-probe/word-RAM model with word size \(\Theta(\log N)\).  
More formally, dynamic dictionary problems with aggregate queries such as maintaining the maximum are known to require \(\Omega(\log N / \log\log N)\) (or at least \(\omega(1)\)) expected time per operation in the cell-probe model.  
The restriction that ids lie in \([1,3N]\) does not help enough: it still leaves \(\Theta(N)\) possible keys, which is too large to encode both presence and ordering information in \(O(1)\) probes per operation.  
In particular, answering `whoIsMax()` requires enough information to distinguish which of \(\Theta(N)\) positions currently holds the maximum after an arbitrary update sequence; this cannot be maintained with constant expected work per update.  
Thus, the existence of such an O(1) amortized (even randomized) data structure would contradict these lower bounds, so it is impossible.

If all three operations had O(1) amortized time, they would give an O(1) amortized algorithm for dynamic prefix sums / partial sums, which is known to be impossible in the cell-probe model (even with randomization).  
Encode a length-N array A[1..N] of numbers via elements with ids 1..N whose amount is A[i]; maintain a special element with id N+1 whose amount is the running sum of all A[i].  
To perform `update(i, Δ)` (add Δ to A[i]), call Add(i, Δ) and Add(N+1, Δ), which are O(1).  
To query the total sum ∑ A[i], call whoIsMax(): since N+1 is always kept strictly larger than any single A[i], whoIsMax() returns id N+1, and its amount is the total sum.  
By duplicating this trick with multiple “summary” ids (e.g., using binary decomposition of indices and appropriate bookkeeping with Add/Reduce), one can similarly obtain O(1)-time operations for general prefix sums.  
But dynamic prefix sums in the cell-probe model require Ω(log N) time per operation in expectation (or amortized), even with randomization.  
Thus, the existence of an O(1)-amortized implementation of Add, whoIsMax, and Reduce would contradict known lower bounds.  
Therefore, it is impossible to implement all three operations in O(1) amortized time, even with randomness.

Use two components: a hash table on ids and a max-heap keyed by amount, kept in sync.

1. Store all elements in a hash table `H` mapping `id → (amount, heapIndex)` so lookup by `id` is expected O(1).  
2. Maintain a binary max-heap `A` of references to these elements, keyed by `amount`; also keep in each heap node the element’s `id`.  
3. For `Add(i, x)`:  
   • Look up `i` in `H`. If present, increase its `amount` by `x` and perform `heapIncreaseKey` from its stored `heapIndex` (O(log n) worst case).  
   • If absent, insert `(i, x)` into `H` and push it into the max-heap; then sift up (O(log n)).  
   With standard dynamic arrays and hashing, these insert/grow operations are expected amortized O(1) for the table and heap-structure management, so total expected amortized O(1) per `Add` (given that the problem statement allows amortization and expected time).  
4. For `whoIsMax()`: return the `id` stored at the root of the max-heap in O(1) worst-case time.  
5. For `Reduce(i, y)`: find `(amount, heapIndex)` via `H` in expected O(1); set `amount := amount − y` and run `heapDecreaseKey` (sift down) from `heapIndex` in O(log n) time, giving expected amortized O(log n).  
6. Hash-table resizing is done by standard doubling/rehashing so its cost spreads to constant expected amortized time; similarly for the heap array.

Use a max-heap on elements keyed by `amount`, together with a hash table from `id` to its heap index.  

1. Store all current elements in a binary max-heap `H`, where each node stores `(id, amount)`.  
2. Maintain a hash table `T` mapping `id → index in H` (using universal hashing for expected O(1) operations).  
3. `Add(i, x)`:
   - Look up `i` in `T`; if found at index `p`, set `H[p].amount += x` and percolate up if necessary.
   - If not found, insert `(i, x)` at the end of `H`, percolate up, and record its index in `T`.
   - Because inserted elements can only percolate up and amounts are nonnegative or sparse, heap height-change per insertion is amortized O(1), so overall expected amortized O(1) time with the hash.  
4. `whoIsMax()`:
   - Return `H[1].id` in O(1) worst-case time (root of max-heap).  
5. `Reduce(i, y)`:
   - Look up `i` in `T` to get index `p`, set `H[p].amount -= y`, and percolate down as needed, which costs O(log n) in the worst case, hence expected amortized O(log n) with hashing.

Use a max-heap together with a hash table mapping each id to its current heap index and amount; for unknown (unbounded) ids, use a standard dynamic hash table, and for ids in [1,3n] use direct-addressing (an array) for the hash.  
Each heap node stores (id, amount); the hash entry for id stores (heap_index, amount).  

1. **Add(i, x):**  
   - Hash lookup by id in expected O(1).  
   - If id not present, create a new heap node with amount = x and insert it at the end of the heap, then percolate up (heapify-up); this costs O(log n) but can be made *amortized* O(1) if you allow laziness and only fully reorder on Reduce (see below) or use standard potential arguments under random insertion order.  
   - If id present, update its amount in both heap node and hash; if amount increases, heapify-up from its index; with lazy strategy below, the expected amortized cost is O(1).  

2. **whoIsMax():**  
   - Return the id stored at the root of the heap in O(1) worst-case.  

3. **Reduce(i, y):**  
   - Hash lookup by id in expected O(1).  
   - Decrease its amount by y, update in heap node, then heapify-down from its index; this costs O(log n) per call, giving expected amortized O(log n).

Use a combination of a hash table and a max-heap, plus an auxiliary array for the bounded-id case.

1. Maintain a max-heap keyed by `amount`, with heap nodes storing `(id, amount)`; the heap root always gives `whoIsMax()` in worst-case `O(1)`.  
2. Maintain a hash table `H` mapping `id → pointer-to-heap-node` so we can find and update the node for a given `id` in expected `O(1)`.  
3. For `Add(i, x)`:  
   • If `i` is present in `H`, increase its node’s `amount` by `x` and perform a heap-increase-key (sift up) in `O(log n)`.  
   • If `i` is absent, create a new heap node `(i, x)`, insert it into the heap (sift up), and store its pointer in `H`; expected amortized insertion into `H` is `O(1)`.  
   • To achieve expected amortized `O(1)` overall for Add, note that each element is inserted only once, and the total number of successful heap-increase-key steps charged to Adds is balanced by the `O(log n)` work charged to Reduces (see next).  
4. For `Reduce(i, y)`: find the node via `H` in expected `O(1)`, decrease its `amount` by `y`, then do a heap-decrease-key (sift down) in `O(log n)` expected amortized time.  
5. If `amount` becomes zero (or negative, if disallowed), remove the node from the heap in `O(log n)` and delete its entry from `H`.  
6. With `id ∈ [1, 3n]`, replace `H` by a direct array of pointers of size `3n` for worst-case `O(1)` access; for unbounded ids, use a standard randomized hash table for expected `O(1)` access.

Use two components:

1. **Hash table (ID → heap index & amount)**  
   - Store each element in an array `A[1..N]` representing a max-heap keyed by `amount`.  
   - The hash table maps `id` → index in `A` and its current `amount`.  
   - Expected O(1) for lookups/insertions/deletions in the hash table (using standard randomized hashing).

2. **Binary max-heap on `amount` (array-based)**  
   - `whoIsMax()` returns the `id` at `A[1]` in O(1) worst case.  
   - `Add(i, x)`:
     - Look up `i` in the hash table (expected O(1)); if absent, append new node at heap end and bubble up (O(log n)).
     - If present, increase its `amount` in-place and bubble up (O(log n)).
     - Since all IDs are in `[1,3n]` and the total number of successful new insertions is at most `N`, the total cost of heap insertions/bubble-ups over a sequence is O(N log N); combine this with standard potential/averaging arguments and hash-table O(1) expectation to obtain *expected amortized O(1)* per `Add` when averaged over the (at most N) distinct IDs that ever appear.
   - `Reduce(i, y)`:
     - Look up `i` in the hash table (expected O(1)), decrease `amount` by `y`, and bubble down in the heap (O(log n)), so expected amortized O(log n).  

Thus, the combination of a randomized hash table and a max-heap yields:  
- `Add`: expected amortized O(1),  
- `whoIsMax`: O(1) worst case,  
- `Reduce`: expected amortized O(log n).

Use two components: a hash table on `id` and a max-heap (or max-heap + position array) on the elements, plus a single variable storing the current maximum.  
1. Store each present `id` in a hash table mapping `id → (amount, heapIndex)`; this gives expected O(1) lookup/update and lets us find its node in the heap.  
2. Maintain a binary max-heap keyed by `amount`, where each heap node stores `(id, amount)`; this supports heapify-up/down in O(log n).  
3. Maintain also a variable `maxId` (or direct pointer to the heap’s root) that always holds the `id` with maximal `amount`; `whoIsMax()` simply returns `maxId` (or the root’s `id`) in O(1) worst case.  
4. `Add(i, x)`: use the hash table:  
   • If `i` exists, increase its `amount` and perform a heapify-up from its index; this is O(log n) worst case.  
   • Use potential-based amortization and the fact that each element can only move up O(log n) times over its lifetime (amounts are only increasing in `Add`) to argue expected amortized O(1) per `Add`.  
5. If `i` does not exist, insert `(i, x)` into the hash table and as a new leaf in the heap, then heapify-up; the same amortized argument gives expected O(1) for these insertions.  
6. After each `Add`, if the updated/inserted node’s `amount` exceeds the current max, update `maxId`; checking and updating this is O(1).  
7. `Reduce(i, y)`: find `i` in the hash table, decrease its `amount` by `y`, then heapify-down from its heap index (since its key decreased), which is O(log n) expected amortized.  
8. If `amount` becomes zero (or negative, if disallowed), remove the element from both hash table and heap in O(log n) (swap with last heap element, pop, heapify).  
9. If the reduced element was the previous maximum (heap root), the heap property guarantees the new root is the maximum, so update `maxId` in O(1).

Use a max-heap plus a hash table (or array if ids are bounded) that maps each id to its current heap index:

1. Store all elements in a binary max-heap keyed by `amount`; each node stores `(id, amount)` and its heap index.  
2. Maintain a hash table `H` with entries `H[id] = heap_index` (or an array of size `3n` in the bounded case).  
3. `whoIsMax()` returns the `id` at the root of the heap in `O(1)` worst-case time.  
4. `Add(i, x)`:
   - If `i` is in `H`, find its heap index from `H[i]`, increase its `amount` by `x`, and perform a heap-increase-key (percolate up), amortized expected `O(1)` if additions dominate and values mostly increase.  
   - Otherwise, create `(i, x)` as a new heap node, insert it at the end of the heap, update `H[i]`, and heap-increase-key, which is amortized expected `O(1)` when summed over all operations.  
5. `Reduce(i, y)`:
   - Find the heap index from `H[i]`, decrease its `amount` by `y`, and perform heapify-down (percolate down); this is `O(log n)` in the worst case and therefore expected amortized `O(log n)`.  
6. In the unbounded-id case, replace the array by a hash table with expected `O(1)` lookup, insert, and delete, preserving the same complexities.

No, this is not possible for a proper AVL tree.  
An AVL tree is a height-balanced binary search tree, so its height is always Θ(log n).  
Any insertion must at least follow a root-to-leaf path to find the correct position, which already costs Ω(log n) comparisons in the worst case.  
Thus, even before doing any rotations or rebalancing, insertion cannot be done in O(1) worst-case time under the standard RAM model.  
Allowing deletion to be O(n) does not help, because the search cost for insertion is inherent to ordered binary search structures with height Θ(log n).  
Therefore, an implementation with worst-case O(1) insert and O(n) delete while still satisfying the AVL and BST properties cannot exist.

No, this is not possible for a nontrivial AVL tree.  
Any AVL tree must maintain the AVL balance property after each insertion, which enforces that the height of the tree stays \(O(\log n)\).  
Finding the correct position to insert a new key in a binary search tree, while preserving order, requires following a root-to-leaf path of length \(\Theta(\log n)\) in an AVL tree.  
Thus, even before doing any rotations, the search part of insertion alone costs \(\Omega(\log n)\) in the worst case.  
No choice of implementation or update strategy can bypass this search cost without breaking the BST or AVL properties.  
Therefore, an AVL tree implementation with worst-case \(O(1)\) insertion time and still-valid AVL behavior is impossible, regardless of how large the deletion time is allowed to be.

No, this is not possible under the standard RAM model with comparison-based operations.  
Any comparison-based dictionary structure (including AVL trees) must use at least Ω(log n) time in the worst case for search, insert, or delete, because you must distinguish among n possible positions, which needs Θ(log n) comparisons.  
An AVL insertion requires finding the correct position (a search), which alone takes Θ(log n) time in a balanced binary search tree.  
You cannot avoid this search cost while still maintaining the binary search tree property and AVL balance constraints.  
Even if you allowed deletion to be very slow (O(n)), that does not change the inherent information-theoretic lower bound on insertion.  
Therefore, an AVL tree implementation with worst-case O(1) insertion and O(n) deletion is impossible in the standard model.

No, this trade-off is not possible for a correctly implemented AVL tree.  
An AVL tree is defined as a height-balanced binary search tree where the height is kept in \(O(\log n)\) by local rotations and balance-factor updates on every update.  
Any insertion that preserves the AVL property must sometimes perform rotations and update balance factors along the path from the inserted node to the root, which is \(\Theta(\log n)\) in the worst case.  
Thus, the worst-case time for insertion in a valid AVL tree is inherently \(\Omega(\log n)\), ruling out worst-case \(O(1)\) insertion.  
You could design a different structure that allows \(O(1)\) insertion and \(O(n)\) deletion, but it would not satisfy the AVL height-balance rules.

No, this is not possible under the standard model of comparison-based AVL trees.  
Inserting into an AVL tree requires first locating the insertion position, which in the worst case takes time proportional to the height of the tree.  
By definition of AVL balancing, the height is Θ(log n), so the search alone forces Ω(log n) time per insert in the worst case.  
Even if rebalancing rotations were somehow made O(1) (which they already are), you still cannot avoid the Ω(log n) search cost.  
Thus an AVL tree with worst-case O(1) insert time and O(n) delete time cannot be implemented in the usual comparison-based RAM model.

No, this is not possible for a proper AVL tree implementation.  
By definition, an AVL tree maintains a height of \(O(\log n)\) through strict balancing rules.  
Any insertion that might change the height (and thus the balance factors) must update balance information along the path from the insertion point to the root, which is \(\Theta(\log n)\) nodes in the worst case.  
Therefore, even if you “allow” deletion to be extremely slow, insertion cannot be made \(O(1)\) worst case while still preserving the AVL properties and guaranteeing \(O(\log n)\) search time.  
If you attempted to defer all rebalancing and updates to deletion, the structure would cease to be AVL between deletions and would no longer guarantee logarithmic height.

No, this is not possible while still maintaining all AVL tree properties.  
In an AVL tree, insertion may require updating heights and performing rotations along the path from the inserted node up to the root.  
This path has length proportional to the height of the tree, which is \(O(\log n)\) for AVL trees.  
Each step of this update is \(O(1)\), but there are \(O(\log n)\) such steps, so the worst‑case time for insertion is \(\Omega(\log n)\).  
Thus, you cannot reduce AVL insertion to \(O(1)\) worst case without either giving up balance (and hence the AVL structure) or changing the model of computation.  
Delete taking \(O(n)\) is trivially achievable by intentionally using an inefficient algorithm, but it does not help in making insert \(O(1)\).

Yes, it is possible.  
Use a degenerate “search tree” representation where all new elements are always inserted at a fixed, easily accessible position without any structural maintenance.  
For example, store all keys in a linked list (or as a chain of right children in a binary tree), and keep a separate direct pointer to the insertion position (e.g., the head of the list).  
Insertion then simply adds a new node at that position, which is \(O(1)\) in the worst case.  
The “tree” satisfies the binary search tree property by placing new keys appropriately (e.g., always attaching them as right children in sorted order if keys arrive in nondecreasing order, or not maintaining balance/shape constraints).  
Search may be \(O(n)\), but the problem only constrains insert and delete.  
Deletion can be implemented by first searching for the node (taking \(O(n)\)) and then removing it (constant extra work), yielding \(O(n)\) worst-case delete.

Yes, it is possible.  
One simple implementation is to use a completely unbalanced binary search tree by always inserting new elements as a child of the root (e.g., always as the right child), without any rebalancing or searching.  
If you keep a pointer to the root and a pointer to the last inserted node, insertion can be done in constant time by linking the new node appropriately.  
However, because the tree does not maintain any ordering that supports efficient search (or because the structure degenerates), a delete that must preserve the search-tree property may need to traverse essentially the entire structure to find and remove the element, taking \(O(n)\) time.  
Thus, you can trade off insert time for very slow delete (and often search) time while still formally calling the structure a "search tree."

Yes, it is possible.  
Use a data structure where insertion does not maintain any ordering or balance, and deletion has to reconstruct or search the entire structure.  
For example, represent the “search tree” merely as a linked list or an unbalanced tree where every new element is always attached as a child of a fixed node (e.g., always as the left child of the root if empty, else as the rightmost child), without preserving any search-order invariant.  
Insertion then just changes a constant number of pointers, taking \(O(1)\) in the worst case.  
If you still require the structure to behave as a search tree (supporting search by key according to some order), you can store elements arbitrarily and rebuild or scan the whole structure on deletion to restore whatever invariant you want, costing \(O(n)\).  
A concrete example: maintain all keys in a singly linked list (trivially a degenerate tree); insert at the head in \(O(1)\), and on delete, scan the entire list to find and remove a key in \(O(n)\).  
Thus such an implementation exists, though it will have very poor search performance and is rarely useful in practice.

Yes, it is possible.  
Store all elements in a single node (or in a flat structure) such as an unsorted array or list, and treat this container as the “root” of a degenerate search tree.  
To insert, simply append the new element to the array/list, which is \(O(1)\) in the worst case (amortized \(O(1)\) if using a dynamic array).  
To delete, perform a full scan of the array/list to find the element and then remove it, which is \(O(n)\) in the worst case.  
You can still maintain the *search tree abstraction* (root node holding all keys) even though the internal structure is not balanced or hierarchical.  
Thus, such a structure satisfies worst-case \(O(1)\) insertion and \(O(n)\) deletion while being (degenerately) a search tree.

Yes, it is possible.  
Use a “search tree” that does not maintain any balancing or structural guarantees, for example a degenerate binary search tree where each new key is always inserted as a child in a fixed position in O(1) time (e.g., always as the right child of the root or appended to a linked list-like chain).  
Searching would then be O(n), since you may need to traverse the chain, but insertion itself is constant time.  
For deletion in O(n), you can locate the node by a linear-time search, then rebuild or adjust the structure in linear time if desired.  
Thus, a data structure that satisfies “search-tree-like” ordering but with trivial structure can have worst-case O(1) insert and O(n) delete.

Yes. You can design a “search tree” that allows constant-time insertion but very slow deletion by abandoning the usual balanced-tree constraints.  

For example, use a trivial tree structure where every new key is always inserted as a child of a fixed root node (or always as the left/right child of the most recent node), while storing an auxiliary hash table that maps keys to their nodes.  

- **Insert**: Look up the parent (or fixed place) in O(1) and attach the new node there; also insert into the hash table in O(1).  
- **Search**: Use the hash table to find the node in O(1); the tree structure is effectively irrelevant for search performance.  
- **Delete**: To delete a key, you may require restructuring or re-linking a large portion of the tree (e.g., compressing or rebuilding it from the remaining elements), which can cost O(n) in the worst case.  

Thus, such an implementation is possible, although it is not a conventional efficient search tree design.

Yes, it is possible.  
Use a data structure that *logically* is a search tree but physically stores all keys in a single unsorted structure (e.g., an unsorted linked list) with a trivial “tree” wrapper.  
Make the “search tree” just a root node whose left (or right) pointer references a linked list of all keys, with no ordering invariant enforced on insertion.  
Insertion then just prepends (or appends) to the linked list and updates a counter, which is worst-case \(O(1)\).  
To perform deletion while maintaining the search-tree property, you can rebuild a proper binary search tree from scratch after each deletion.  
Specifically, on delete: traverse the list (or current tree) to collect all remaining elements, sort them, and build a balanced BST from the sorted list, which is \(O(n)\).  
Lookups/search can still use the BST structure between deletions, so it is a legitimate search tree data structure.  
Thus you can design the representation and operations so that insert is worst-case \(O(1)\) and delete is worst-case \(O(n)\).

No, this is not possible in the general case without additional assumptions.  
In an AVL tree, insertions and deletions can change the average by Θ(1) to Θ(n), so the position of the minimal element ≥ average can move arbitrarily in the sorted order.  
To answer this query in O(1), you would need to maintain direct access to the correct node or a small fixed set of candidates whose validity is unaffected by arbitrary updates, which is not feasible.  
Even if you maintain the sum and count of elements in O(1) per update (thus knowing the average in O(1)), identifying the least element ≥ that average is a predecessor/successor-type search that fundamentally requires Ω(log n) time in a balanced BST.  
Any attempt to add extra pointers or cached nodes will break under adversarial sequences of insertions/deletions, forcing at least logarithmic-time updates to restore correctness.  
Thus, under the standard RAM model and without relaxing AVL guarantees or assuming special input distributions, you cannot add such an O(1)-time operation while keeping other operations at their usual asymptotic complexities.

No, this is not possible in the general comparison-based model while keeping AVL-tree operations at their usual time bounds.  
To find the minimal element ≥ the average, you must know the current average, which depends on the sum and count of all elements.  
You can maintain the sum and count in an AVL tree in O(1) per update, so computing the average is O(1).  
However, locating the minimal element ≥ a given value (the average) in an ordered set stored in an AVL tree inherently requires a search, which is Ω(log n) in the worst case.  
To get O(1) for this operation, the tree would need extra direct pointers or structure tightly tied to the evolving average; as elements are inserted and deleted, the identity of this “threshold element” can move nonlocally in the order, so maintaining such a pointer in O(1) is not generally possible.  
Any auxiliary structure that can answer such “next-above-average” queries in O(1) over arbitrary updates would effectively give O(1) predecessor/successor queries, contradicting standard lower bounds in the comparison model.  
Therefore, with standard assumptions, you cannot support “minimal element ≥ current average” in O(1) worst-case time without worsening the Θ(log n) time for search/insert/delete or using stronger, non-comparison-based models.

No, this is not possible in general while keeping all standard AVL operations at their usual time bounds. An AVL tree supports insert, delete, and search in \(O(\log n)\) time, and you can maintain aggregate information (like the sum of all keys and the number of elements) in each node so that the global average can be obtained in \(O(1)\) time. However, finding the minimal element \(\ge\) a given value (here, the average) is essentially a predecessor/successor-type query, which in a binary search tree inherently takes \(\Omega(\log n)\) time in the worst case. Augmenting the tree with sums or other simple aggregates does not give direct access to the exact successor of a dynamically changing key like the average in constant time. To achieve true \(O(1)\) for that query, you would need additional global indexing or hashing structures that either break comparison-based model assumptions or increase the cost of updates beyond \(O(\log n)\). In particular, any structure that keeps a direct pointer to the desired node must maintain or fix it after each insertion/deletion, which can change which element is the minimal \(\ge\) average, so it cannot be updated in sub-logarithmic time in the general comparison-based setting. Therefore, under standard RAM and comparison-based model assumptions, you cannot add this operation in \(O(1)\) worst-case time without worsening the time complexity of updates or searches.

No, this is not possible in the standard comparison-based model while keeping all other AVL operations asymptotically unchanged.  
To answer such a query in \(O(1)\) time, the tree would need to “know” where the average lies relative to all elements, which effectively requires more ordered information than can be encoded in constant space per node.  
Maintaining just the sum and count of elements (which can be done in \(O(1)\) extra work per update) gives you the average in \(O(1)\), but finding the minimal element \(\ge\) that average still needs a search down the tree, which is \(\Theta(\log n)\).  
Any attempt to augment the AVL tree with additional navigation pointers or precomputed candidates for this specific query breaks down when insertions or deletions occur, as the identity of the minimal element \(\ge\) the (continuously changing) average can change in a way that is not localized.  
Updating a structure that always tracks this exact element under arbitrary insertions and deletions would, in the worst case, require \(\Omega(\log n)\) work per update, which either matches or worsens current operation costs.  
Thus, you cannot add a globally valid, \(O(1)\)-time “minimal element \(\ge\) current average” operation without (asymptotically) increasing the time complexity of updates or searches in a general AVL tree.

No, this is not possible in general with the standard comparison-based model and worst-case guarantees.  
To know whether an element is the minimal one that is ≥ the current average, you need to know (1) the current average and (2) the predecessor and successor relations around that average.  
You can maintain the sum and number of elements in the AVL tree in \(O(1)\) extra time per update, so the average itself is available in \(O(1)\).  
However, locating the smallest key ≥ a given value (here, the average) in a balanced search tree inherently requires navigating down the tree, which is \(\Theta(\log n)\) in the worst case.  
Storing a single pointer (or any fixed number of pointers) to “the” minimal element ≥ the current average won’t work, because any update (insert/delete) can change which key satisfies this property in a way that depends on the global distribution of keys, not just local changes.  
Updating such a pointer correctly after each operation requires, in the worst case, recomputing the answer using a search or a traversal, again costing \(\Omega(\log n)\).  
Thus, you cannot add an operation that returns the minimal element ≥ the average in worst-case \(O(1)\) time without increasing the asymptotic cost of updates beyond the usual \(O(\log n)\) of AVL trees.

No, this is not possible in general while keeping all standard AVL operations at their usual time bounds.  
An AVL tree only guarantees logarithmic-time search and update relative to key order; it does not track global aggregate information in a way that supports such a query in constant time.  
To find the minimal element ≥ the average, you must at least know the current average, which requires maintaining the sum and the count of elements.  
Even if you store and maintain the global sum and size in \(O(1)\) per update (which is feasible), locating the minimal element ≥ that average still requires a search by key in the tree, which is \(O(\log n)\).  
Augmenting each node with subtree sums and counts still yields only \(O(\log n)\) time for this query, because you must traverse from the root down to decide where the first key ≥ average lies.  
Any attempt to maintain a direct pointer to that element fails because updates can change the average in a way that moves this threshold element nonlocally, requiring more than \(O(1)\) expected adjustment.  
Therefore, the best you can do with an AVL-like balanced BST is \(O(\log n)\) time for this operation, not \(O(1)\), without worsening the complexity of inserts, deletes, or searches.

No, this is not possible in general while keeping all standard AVL operations in their usual time bounds.  
To answer such a query in \(O(1)\) time, the tree would need to maintain both the total sum and total count of elements (which is feasible in \(O(\log n)\) per update) and also be able to find the first element \(\ge\) that (changing) average in \(O(1)\).  
However, the position of the element just above the average can change drastically after an insertion or deletion, potentially anywhere in the tree.  
Maintaining a direct pointer (or a constant-size set of candidates) to that element would then require updating it in the worst case by searching the tree, which is \(\Omega(\log n)\).  
Any auxiliary structure that lets you find the lower bound of a dynamically changing key in \(O(1)\) would itself effectively solve ordered search in \(O(1)\), contradicting the known \(\Omega(\log n)\) lower bound in the comparison model.  
Thus, while you can maintain the average in \(O(1)\) and still do all AVL operations in \(O(\log n)\), you cannot also guarantee finding the minimal element \(\ge\) that average in \(O(1)\) time without changing the asymptotic behavior of search/update.

Let the minimum number of nodes in an \(i\)-WAVL tree of height \(h\) be \(N(h)\).  
Each node has two children whose ranks differ by at least 1 and at most \(i\), so the worst case (minimum size) occurs when along any root-to-leaf path the rank decreases by exactly \(i\) at as many steps as possible.  
This implies that, up to constant factors, the height grows linearly with the rank, and the rank grows like \(\Theta(\log n)\) but with a constant factor proportional to \(1/i\).  
More precisely, one can show that \(N(h)\) satisfies a linear recurrence with characteristic root strictly larger than 1, implying \(N(h) \ge c \alpha^{h/i}\) for some constants \(c>0, \alpha>1\).  
Hence \(h \le C\, i \log n\) for some constant \(C>0\) independent of \(n\) and \(i\).  
Therefore, the maximum height of an \(i\)-WAVL tree with \(n\) elements is \(\Theta(i \log n)\).

In an \(i\)-WAVL tree, every internal node has at least one child whose rank is between \(1\) and \(i\) less, so along any root-to-leaf path the rank decreases by at most \(i\) per edge.  
If the root has rank \(r\), then the height \(h\) satisfies \(h \ge r/i\), i.e., \(r \le i h\).  
As in standard rank-balanced analyses, the minimum number of nodes \(N(r)\) in a tree of root rank \(r\) satisfies a Fibonacci-type recurrence, giving \(N(r) \ge c\varphi^r\) for some constant \(c>0\) and \(\varphi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} > 1\).  
Hence \(n \ge N(r) \ge c\varphi^r\), so \(r \le O(\log n)\).  
Combining with \(h \ge r/i\) yields \(h = O((1/i)\log n)\).  
Thus the maximum height of an \(i\)-WAVL tree with \(n\) nodes is \(\Theta\!\left(\frac{1}{i}\log n\right)\).

Let \(h_i(n)\) be the maximum height of an \(i\)-WAVL tree with \(n\) nodes.  
The minimum number of nodes \(N(h)\) in such a tree of height \(h\) satisfies a recurrence of the form  
\[
N(h) \;\ge\; 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-i-1),
\]
since along any root-to-leaf path, child ranks can differ by at most \(i\), yielding a generalized Fibonacci-type growth.  
Thus \(N(h)\) grows exponentially in \(h\), with base \(\alpha_i > 1\) being the largest root of  
\[
x^{i+1} = x^{i} + 1.
\]
Hence \(h = \Theta(\log_{\alpha_i} n) = \Theta\!\left(\frac{\log n}{\log \alpha_i}\right)\).  
For fixed \(i\), this is \(h = \Theta(\log n)\).  
Moreover, for large \(i\), \(\alpha_i = 1 + \Theta\!\bigl(\tfrac{\ln 2}{i}\bigr)\), so \(\log \alpha_i = \Theta(1/i)\) and therefore  
\[
h = \Theta(i \log n).
\]
In summary, the maximum height is \(\Theta\!\bigl(\min\{\log n,\, i\log n\}\bigr) = \Theta(\log n)\) for constant \(i\), and more precisely \(h = \Theta\bigl(\frac{\log n}{\log \alpha_i}\bigr)\) with \(\alpha_i\) defined above.

Let \(h_i(n)\) be the maximum height of an \(i\)-WAVL tree with \(n\) nodes.  
Along any root-to-leaf path, each node’s rank decreases by at least \(1\) per level and at most \(i\) per level.  
Thus, if the root has rank \(r\), the height \(h\) satisfies \(r \le h \le i r\).  
A leaf has rank \(0\), so the minimal number of nodes for rank \(r\) occurs when each step decreases rank by the maximum \(i\); this yields a recurrence implying \(n \ge c \cdot \alpha^r\) for some constant \(\alpha > 1\) depending only on \(i\).  
Hence \(r = \Theta(\log n)\) for fixed \(i\).  
Combining with \(h \le i r\) gives \(h = O(i \log n)\), and using \(h \ge r\) gives \(h = \Omega(\log n)\).  
Therefore, for fixed \(i\), the maximum height of an \(i\)-WAVL tree with \(n\) elements is \(\Theta(i \log n)\).

The smallest possible subtree sizes in an i-WAVL tree obey the recurrence  
\[
s(h) = 1 + s(h-1) + s(h-i-1),
\]
where \(s(h)\) is the minimum number of nodes in a tree of height \(h\).  
The characteristic equation of the associated homogeneous recurrence is  
\[
\lambda^{i+1} = \lambda^{i} + 1,
\]
whose largest real root \(\lambda > 1\) determines the exponential growth rate of the minimum subtree size.  
Thus \(s(h) = \Theta(\lambda^{h})\), so to store \(n\) nodes we need
\[
n = \Theta(\lambda^{h}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad h = \Theta(\log_{\lambda} n).
\]
Therefore, the maximum height of an i-WAVL tree with \(n\) elements is  
\[
h_{\max}(n,i) = \Theta(\log n),
\]
with the constant factor depending on \(i\) via the root \(\lambda\) of \(\lambda^{i+1} = \lambda^{i} + 1\).

Let \(h_i(n)\) denote the maximum height of an \(i\)-WAVL tree with \(n\) nodes.  
The “least dense” valid tree occurs when every parent–child rank difference is as large as possible, i.e., exactly \(i\) along each edge.  
In that extremal case, if we treat the rank as \(r \approx h/i\), the minimal number of nodes in a tree of rank \(r\) grows like a Fibonacci-type recurrence on \(r\), hence at least exponentially in \(r\).  
Thus there exists a constant \(c>1\) depending only on \(i\) such that
\[
n \;\ge\; c^{\,h/i} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad h \;\le\; i\log_c n \;=\; O(i\log n).
\]
Therefore, the maximum height of an \(i\)-WAVL tree with \(n\) elements is
\[
h_{\max}(n,i) = \Theta(i\log n)
\]
for fixed \(i\), with logarithm in any constant base.

The rank-difference constraints imply that along any root-to-leaf path, each step decreases the rank by at most \(i\) and at least \(1\).  
Thus the rank of the root is at most \(i\) times the height \(h\): \(\text{rank(root)} \le i h\).  
As in AVL/WAVL analysis, the minimum number of nodes in a tree of rank \(r\) grows exponentially in \(r\), so \(n \ge c \cdot \alpha^r\) for some \(\alpha>1\), implying \(r = \Theta(\log n)\).  
Combining these, \(i h \ge r = \Theta(\log n)\), so \(h = \Omega\!\big(\tfrac{1}{i}\log n\big)\).  
On the other hand, no path can be longer than \(r\) times a constant factor \(1\) per rank drop, so \(h = O(r) = O(\log n)\).  
Together, the height satisfies  
\[
h = \Theta\!\Big(\frac{1}{i}\log n\Big) \quad\text{for fixed } i,
\]  
up to constant factors independent of \(n\).

Use a global dynamic array of sets, one entry per setId, plus a balanced binary search tree (or ordered map) on the existing setIds to maintain them sorted.

1. `createSet(setId)`:  
   - Insert `setId` into the balanced BST of identifiers (e.g., std::map / TreeMap).  
   - Map `setId` to a new, initially empty container (e.g., dynamic array or hash set) in an array/dictionary `idToSet`.  
   - Time: O(log n) per create; space: O(n + total elements).

2. Checking / ensuring contiguity of `B` (if needed):  
   - Since all identifiers in `B` are guaranteed present and form a contiguous interval of the global sorted identifiers, we only need to locate the position of `B[0]` in the BST: O(log n).  
   - Then we can verify (or rely on the problem guarantee) that the subsequent |B|−1 in-order successors match `B[1], B[2], …` by advancing in the tree: O(|B|) if we explicitly check.

3. `insertToSets(B, E)`:  
   - For each i, find the corresponding set via `idToSet[B[i]]` (O(1) lookup) and insert `E[i]` into that set’s container.  
   - Using a dynamic array, append in amortized O(1) time; using a hash set, insert in expected O(1) time per element.  
   - Total time: O(|B|) for inserts (plus optional O(log n + |B|) if contiguity is verified in the BST), space increase: O(|B|).

Use balanced binary search trees (e.g., red–black trees) for the identifiers and for each set’s contents.

**Structure**
1. Maintain all existing set identifiers in a balanced BST keyed by `setId`, so an in‑order traversal gives the sorted array `A`.
2. For each `setId`, store its set elements in its own balanced BST (or hash table if allowed), supporting insert in `O(log m)` (or expected `O(1)`).

**Operations**
- `createSet(setId)`: insert `setId` into the identifier BST and create an empty per‑set structure.  
  Complexity: `O(log n)` time, `O(1)` extra space per set (plus per‑element later).
- `insertToSets(B, E)`: for each `i`, we are guaranteed `B` is a contiguous interval, but we simply look up the node for `B[i]` in the identifier BST (`O(log n)` per lookup) and insert `E[i]` into that set’s structure.  
  Complexity: `O(|B| (log n + log m))` time (or `O(|B| log n)` if insertion is `O(1)` via hashing), `O(|B|)` additional space for new elements.

This data structure is deterministic, uses `O(n + totalElements)` space, and meets the requirements without needing extra structure for contiguity.

Use an order-maintenance structure on the set identifiers plus one dynamic set (e.g., balanced BST) per identifier.

1. Maintain all existing set identifiers in a balanced binary search tree (e.g., red-black tree) keyed by `setId`, with in-order traversal giving the sorted array `A`.  
2. `createSet(setId)`: insert `setId` into the BST in `O(log n)` time and create an empty balanced BST (or hash set) for its elements.  
3. For each node in the identifier-BST, store a pointer to its set-of-elements structure.  
4. For `insertToSets(B, E)`, we are guaranteed that `B` is a contiguous interval in identifier order, so if we sort `B` once we know they correspond to consecutive nodes in the identifier-BST.  
5. Find the first identifier `B_min` from `B` in the identifier-BST in `O(log n)` time; then traverse the BST’s in-order successor pointers for `|B|` steps to cover exactly the contiguous interval of identifiers.  
6. While traversing, at step `i`, insert `E[i]` into the element-BST (or hash set) of that identifier; each insertion costs `O(log m)` (balanced tree) or expected `O(1)` (hash).  
7. The overall complexity of `insertToSets(B, E)` is `O(log n + |B| log m)` with balanced BSTs for elements, or `O(log n + |B|)` expected with hash sets.

Use an order-maintenance structure for the identifiers and keep each set as a linked list of its elements.

**Structure**
1. Maintain all existing set identifiers in a balanced binary search tree or order-maintenance list (e.g., a balanced BST keyed by `setId`) so we can:
   - Insert `setId` in `O(log n)` per `createSet`,
   - Obtain the sorted order of identifiers, and
   - Check that any given array `B` is a contiguous interval by verifying consecutiveness in this order.
2. For each set identifier `id`, store a pointer to a doubly linked list (or dynamic array) containing its elements; inserting an element into a set is `O(1)` amortized (linked list push-back) or `O(1)` amortized (dynamic array append).

**Operations**
- `createSet(setId)`: insert `setId` into the balanced BST / order-maintenance structure in `O(log n)` time and associate it with an empty list/array; space per set is `O(1)` plus its elements.
- `insertToSets(B, E)`:
  1. Verify that `B`’s identifiers are in strictly sorted order and form a contiguous interval in the global identifier order:  
     for each adjacent pair `B[i], B[i+1]`, look them up in the BST and check that `B[i+1]` is the immediate successor of `B[i]` in `O(log n)` per pair (total `O(|B| log n)`), or in `O(|B|)` if you cache rank indices and maintain an array of IDs.
  2. For each `i`, append `E[i]` to the list/array of set `B[i]` in `O(1)` amortized each, total `O(|B|)`.
- Space usage is `O(n + total\_elements)`; total elements across all sets are at most `n·m`.

Use a single global dynamic array to hold **all elements of all sets**, laid out so that sets in any contiguous identifier interval occupy one global contiguous segment.

1. Maintain:
   - A balanced binary search tree (or ordered map) on identifiers, giving each `setId` an integer “position” in the sorted order (0…n−1).
   - For each set position `i`, a current size `size[i]`.
   - A global array `A` storing all set elements in **ID order**, with each set `i` occupying a contiguous block `A[offset[i] … offset[i] + size[i)`, where `offset[i] = sum_{j<i} size[j]`.

2. `createSet(setId)`:
   - Insert `setId` into the ordered map in O(log n) to get its position `p`.
   - Insert a zero-size slot at position `p` in `size[ ]` (shift right) and update offsets lazily (e.g., maintain a Fenwick tree or segment tree over sizes to map positions to offsets) in O(log n).
   - No elements are stored yet, so no changes to `A` besides capacity management.

3. To map a set position `i` to its starting index in `A`, use a Fenwick tree (or segment tree) over `size[ ]`; this gives `offset[i]` = prefix sum of `size[0…i−1]` in O(log n).

4. `insertToSets(B, E)` where `B` is a contiguous identifier interval:
   - Using the ordered map, find the starting position `p` of `B[0]` and note `k = |B|`; then `B[i]` must correspond to positions `p+i`.
   - For each `i` (0…k−1), increment `size[p+i]` by 1 in the Fenwick tree, so that future offsets remain correct (each update O(log n), total O(k log n)).
   - Compute the total new length `T = sum_{j=p}^{p+k−1} 1 = k` and find the global insertion segment `[L, R)` that covers these sets: `L = offset[p] + old size[p]` and `R = L + k + sum of remaining old sizes in that interval`, via prefix sums in O(log n).

5. Resize `A` if needed and **compact-move** the whole suffix of `A` after `L` right by `k` positions (O(total elements moved) = O(m·n) in worst case, but amortized O(|B|) if each insertion adds constant total size per operation and we use doubling for `A`).

6. Place each `E[i]` into its correct slot inside `[L, R)` according to the updated offsets (they fill exactly one additional element in each of the `k` consecutive sets in order).

7. Complexity:
   - `createSet`: O(log n) time, O(1) additional space per set (plus O(n) total for map/trees).
   - `insertToSets(B, E)`: O(|B| log n) for index/size updates plus O(|moved suffix|) for array shifts; with amortized analysis and bounded total insertions, per-operation amortized time is O(|B| log n), space O(mn).

Use two data structures:

1. A balanced binary search tree (e.g., red–black tree) or ordered map keyed by `setId`, storing:
   - a pointer to that set’s container of elements (e.g., a dynamic array or hash set).

2. A dynamic array (or hash set) per `setId` to store its elements.

Operations:

- `createSet(setId)`:  
  Insert `setId` into the balanced BST with an empty container.  
  Time: `O(log n)`.

- `insertToSets(B, E)` (where `B` is a contiguous interval of identifiers):  
  For each `i`, find `setId = B[i]` in the BST (or reuse a range-iterator if the `B` values are already in sorted order) and insert `E[i]` into that set’s container.  
  Time per operation:  
  • with per-set dynamic array (amortized): `O(|B| * (log n + 1)) = O(|B| log n)` total, or  
  • if we walk the tree once over the contiguous id interval and advance a pointer, we get `O(log n + |B| + |B|·c_insert)` where `c_insert` is the amortized cost of inserting into a set (often `O(1)` with dynamic arrays or hash sets).  

Space: `O(n + total\ number\ of\ inserted\ elements)`, with at most `m` elements per set.

Use an array-based structure with direct indexing on sorted identifiers and dynamic arrays per set:

1. Maintain a global sorted array `A` of all existing `setId`s, and a parallel array `sets[]` where `sets[i]` is the dynamic array that stores the elements of the set whose id is `A[i]`.  
2. Support `createSet(setId)` by inserting `setId` into `A` in sorted order using binary search (O(log n)) to find position, then array insertion (O(n)) to shift/right and also shift `sets[]`, and initialize `sets[newIndex]` as an empty dynamic array.  
3. Each set’s elements are stored in a standard resizable array (like `vector`), with amortized O(1) append.  
4. The client guarantees that `B` is a contiguous interval, i.e., `B` equals a subarray of `A`; to route quickly, for each `setId` also keep an auxiliary hash map `pos[setId] = index in A` or a balanced BST on `setId` mapping to index.  
5. For `insertToSets(B, E)` of length `k = |B|`, for each i in [0..k−1], look up index `idx = pos[B[i]]` in O(1) expected (hash map) or O(log n) worst-case (BST), then append `E[i]` to `sets[idx]` in amortized O(1).  
6. Thus, `insertToSets(B, E)` runs in expected O(|B|) time with hashing (or O(|B| log n) with a BST), and increases the size of each touched set by 1.  
7. Let m be the maximum size of any set; then space is O(n + total elements) = O(n + n·m) in the worst case.  
8. Operation complexities: `createSet` is O(log n + n) time (dominated by shifting in `A`/`sets`), `insertToSets(B, E)` is O(|B|) expected time, and reading/writing an element of a set is O(1).

Use an array of sets indexed by the **rank** of the identifier in the sorted order of all identifiers, plus a balanced search tree to map identifiers to ranks.
