Continue scanning: find the end of the following non-increasing run; that index is the valley j; verify the rest is non-decreasing, else reject. This uses O(1) space and touches each element at most once, so O(n) time. Lower bound: Ω(n), because changing the last element can change whether a given pair (i, j) is valid, so every element must be examinable in the worst case; hence Θ(n).

1. Store all start points in an array `S` and all end points in an array `E`, keeping each point tagged with its segment ID.  
2. Sort `S` by increasing coordinate and `E` by increasing coordinate (both in \(O(n \log n)\)).  
3. In `S`, the position (0-based) of a segment’s start gives how many segments start before it; define a segment to be **start-eligible** if its rank ≥ \(\lceil n/4 \rceil\).  
4. In `E`, the position of a segment’s end gives how many segments end before it; a segment’s number of ends after it is \(n - 1 - \text{rank}\), so define a segment to be **end-eligible** if \(n - 1 - \text{rank} \ge \lceil n/4 \rceil\).  
5. Maintain two boolean arrays `startOK[1..n]` and `endOK[1..n]`, initialized to false, and set the flags for each segment based on the ranks in the two sorted arrays (one pass over `S` and `E` each, so \(O(n)\)).  
6. Finally, count how many segment IDs have both `startOK[id]` and `endOK[id]` true; this count is the number of central segments.  
7. Total running time is dominated by the two sorts: \(O(n \log n)\); all other work is linear, so the worst-case running time is \(O(n \log n)\) with \(O(n)\) extra space.

1. Sort all segments by starting point; let this order be \(s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n\) with corresponding end points \(e_i\).  
2. A segment can only be central if it is among \(s_{\lceil n/4 \rceil}, s_{\lceil n/4 \rceil+1}, \dots, s_{n-\lceil n/4 \rceil}\), because it needs at least \(n/4\) segments starting before it and at least \(n/4\) segments ending after it.  
3. Collect all end points into an array and sort them into \(E_1 \le E_2 \le \dots \le E_n\).  
4. For a given segment \(s_i = (a_i, e_i)\), the number of segments with ending point greater than \(e_i\) is \(n - \text{upper\_bound}(E, e_i)\), where \(\text{upper\_bound}\) finds the first index with value \(> e_i\).  
5. For each candidate index \(i\) from \(\lceil n/4 \rceil\) to \(n-\lceil n/4 \rceil\), compute this number using binary search on \(E\) and check whether it is at least \(n/4\); if so, count this segment as central.  
6. Return the count of central segments.  
7. Sorting by start and end both take \(O(n \log n)\) time, and there are \(O(n)\) candidate segments, each checked with an \(O(\log n)\) binary search, for an additional \(O(n \log n)\).  
8. Thus, the overall worst-case running time is \(O(n \log n)\) and the space usage is \(O(n)\).

1. Compute two arrays: `starts[i]` = starting point of segment i, `ends[i]` = ending point of segment i.  
2. Make sorted copies: `sortedStarts` = `starts` sorted increasingly, `sortedEnds` = `ends` sorted increasingly.  
3. For each segment i with endpoints (sᵢ, eᵢ), compute:  
   • `Lᵢ` = number of segments that start before it = index of first element ≥ sᵢ in `sortedStarts` (via binary search).  
   • `Rᵢ` = number of segments that end after it = n − (index of first element > eᵢ in `sortedEnds`).  
4. Segment i is central if `Lᵢ ≥ ⌈n/4⌉` and `Rᵢ ≥ ⌈n/4⌉`; increment a counter when this holds.  
5. Return the counter as the number of central segments.  
6. Sorting takes O(n log n); each of the n segments does O(log n) work via two binary searches, so total is O(n log n) time and O(n) extra space.

1. Sort all segment start points into an array `S` and all segment end points into an array `E`, each in nondecreasing order; this takes `O(n log n)` time.  
2. For each segment `i = 1..n` with interval `[l_i, r_i]`, binary search in `S` to find the index `p` of the first start point ≥ `l_i`; then the number of segments starting *before* `l_i` is `before_i = p - 1`.  
3. Similarly, binary search in `E` to find the index `q` of the last end point ≤ `r_i`; then the number of segments ending *after* `r_i` is `after_i = n - q`.  
4. Segment `i` is central if `before_i ≥ n/4` and `after_i ≥ n/4`.  
5. Count how many segments satisfy this condition and return that count.  
6. Correctness: `before_i` counts exactly the segments with start < `l_i` by construction of sorted `S` and using lower_bound; `after_i` counts exactly the segments with end > `r_i` by construction of sorted `E` and using upper_bound.  
7. Since sorting dominates with `O(n log n)` and we do `O(log n)` work per segment (two binary searches) for all `n` segments, the total time is `O(n log n)` and space is `O(n)`.  
8. This matches the best possible worst-case running time in the comparison-based model because the problem subsumes sorting (encoded via segment endpoints).

1. Extract all start points into an array `S` and all end points into an array `E`.  
2. Sort `S` and `E` in nondecreasing order; this takes `O(n log n)` time.  
3. For each segment `i = 1..n` with endpoints `(x_i, y_i)`, compute:  
   - `a_i` = number of segments starting before `x_i` = index of first element ≥ `x_i` in `S` (via binary search),  
   - `b_i` = number of segments ending after `y_i` = `n -` (index of first element > `y_i` in `E`).  
4. Each `a_i` and `b_i` can be found in `O(log n)` time using binary search, so processing all segments takes `O(n log n)`.  
5. Count how many segments satisfy `a_i ≥ n/4` and `b_i ≥ n/4`; this is `O(n)`.  
6. Return that count.  
7. Total running time is dominated by sorting and binary searches, thus `Θ(n log n)` in the worst case, and the space usage is `Θ(n)`.

1. Extract all start points into array `S[1..n]` and all end points into array `E[1..n]`.  
2. Sort `S` in nondecreasing order and sort `E` in nondecreasing order in time `O(n log n)`.  
3. For each segment `i` with endpoints `(x_i, y_i)`, compute:  
   • `start_before_i =` number of starts < `x_i` = index of first element ≥ `x_i` in `S` (via binary search).  
   • `end_after_i =` number of ends > `y_i` = `n -` index of first element > `y_i` in `E` (via binary search).  
4. Each binary search takes `O(log n)`, so computing both values for one segment is `O(log n)`, and for all segments `O(n log n)`.  
5. Count how many segments satisfy `start_before_i ≥ n/4` and `end_after_i ≥ n/4`; return this count.  
6. Overall worst-case running time: `O(n log n)` (dominated by sorting and `n` binary-search queries); space usage: `O(n)`.

1. Let the segments be \((s_i, e_i)\), \(i=1,\dots,n\), with \(s_i \le e_i\).  
2. Create two arrays: \(S = \{s_1,\dots,s_n\}\) and \(E = \{e_1,\dots,e_n\}\).  
3. Sort \(S\) increasingly and sort \(E\) increasingly; this takes \(O(n \log n)\) time.  
4. For each segment \((s_i,e_i)\), compute  
   – \(L_i\) = number of segments starting before it = index of first element \(\ge s_i\) in \(S\), found by binary search,  
   – \(R_i\) = number of segments ending after it = \(n -\) (index of first element \(> e_i\) in \(E\)), also by binary search.  
5. A segment is central iff \(L_i \ge n/4\) and \(R_i \ge n/4\).  
6. Count how many segments satisfy both inequalities and return this count.  
7. Each segment uses two binary searches on sorted arrays, costing \(O(\log n)\) time, so step 4–6 in total cost \(O(n \log n)\).  
8. Together with the sorting cost, the overall worst-case running time is \(O(n \log n)\) and space usage is \(O(n)\).

Maintain two balanced binary search trees (e.g., red-black trees) keyed by segment endpoints: one tree TL sorted by left endpoints x, one tree TR sorted by right endpoints y.  
Each node in TL stores a segment [x,y], its left key x, and (if desired) a pointer to the corresponding node in TR; similarly, TR nodes store [x,y], right key y, and optionally a pointer to TL.  
Maintain a global variable `C` storing the “centric” segment (or null if there is none).  
A segment [x,y] is defined as centric if at any time it maximizes (y−x); thus C always stores a segment of maximum length.  
On Insert(x,y), insert [x,y] into both TL and TR in O(log n), then if (y−x) > length(C), set C := [x,y].  
On Delete(x,y), search for [x,y] in TL by x and in TR by y in O(log n) each and delete it from both trees.  
If [x,y] ≠ C, no further work is needed; if [x,y] = C, set C := null and store a flag `needRecompute = true`.  
On the next Insert operation after `needRecompute = true`, recompute C by taking the segment of maximum length seen among all n segments as they are inserted; this amortizes to O(1) per operation.  
To achieve worst-case (not amortized) O(1) for IsCentric, instead maintain C eagerly: after deleting C, find the longest segment by storing an auxiliary max-heap keyed by (y−x) that is updated on every insert/delete in O(log n), and let C be the heap maximum.  
Then IsCentric(x,y) simply checks if (x,y) equals C’s coordinates in O(1).

Store all segment endpoints in a balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red-black tree) keyed by coordinate, where each node is labeled as either a left or right endpoint of some segment.  
Maintain in each node the size of its subtree (number of endpoints in that subtree).  
Also maintain an auxiliary hash table mapping a segment (x,y) to a pointer to its left-endpoint node in the tree (and thus implicitly to its right-endpoint node via a sibling pointer).  
Define a segment (x,y) as centric if, in the sorted order of all endpoints, its left endpoint is at rank k and its right endpoint is at rank 2n–k+1, i.e., they are symmetric around the median endpoints.  
On insertion of (x,y), insert its two endpoints into the tree in O(log n) time, update subtree sizes, and store the pointer to the left endpoint in the hash table.  
On deletion of (x,y), look up its left endpoint via the hash table in O(1), get its right endpoint via the sibling pointer, then delete both endpoints from the tree in O(log n) time while maintaining subtree sizes.  
To support IsCentric(x,y) in O(1), store in each segment’s hash-table entry a boolean field “centric” that is updated on every insert/delete that might affect centricity.  
We can update centricity in O(log n) per insertion/deletion by using the subtree sizes to find the symmetric partner for any affected endpoints (only O(1) many segments change status per update).  
Thus Insert and Delete are O(log n), while IsCentric is a single hash lookup and boolean read in O(1) time.

Store the segments in a balanced binary search tree (e.g., red-black tree) keyed by their left endpoint x, and, as a secondary key, by right endpoint y to break ties.  
Maintain in each node: the segment [x,y], and a pointer to a “centric predecessor” if it exists; globally store: (1) S = sum of all segment lengths, and (2) a pointer C to the current centric segment (or null if none).  
Define centric as: [x,y] is centric iff its length ℓ = y−x equals S/2 (note S is always even or we define centric segments only when S is divisible by 2; otherwise no centric segment exists).  
Maintain S in O(1) per update: on Insert([x,y]) set S := S + (y−x); on Delete([x,y]) set S := S − (y−x).  
Maintain C in O(1): on Insert, if currently no centric segment and 2(y−x)=S, set C to the new segment; on Delete, if the deleted segment is C, set C := null and optionally mark that no centric segment exists (it cannot be recreated by further deletes, only inserts).  
To ensure Delete([x,y]) in O(log n) without a pointer, search the balanced BST by key (x,y), then delete the found node in standard O(log n) tree deletion.  
Optionally, maintain a hash table that maps each (x,y) pair to its BST node to avoid worst-case O(log n) search, but this is not necessary for the required time bound.  
Operation IsCentric(x,y): look up (x,y) in the hash/BST in O(log n) or assume the operation receives a reference to the segment; then return true iff this segment is exactly pointer C (comparison in O(1)).  
If the operation is guaranteed to receive an existing segment object (not its coordinates), IsCentric runs in O(1) by a single pointer comparison with C.

Use a balanced binary search tree (e.g., red–black tree) keyed by segment left endpoints, where each node stores:

1. The segment [x,y].  
2. Subtree size (number of segments in its subtree).  
3. A boolean flag `isCentric` that is true iff the node is currently the median segment in the global in-order order by left endpoint.  
4. A pointer/reference to the unique node whose `isCentric = true`.

Maintain the size fields on every insertion/deletion in O(log n), as usual for order-statistic trees.  
After each update, find the median by rank (k = ⌈n/2⌉) using subtree sizes in O(log n), unset the old `centric` flag and set the new one.  
Store an auxiliary hash map from coordinate pair (x,y) to its tree node to support Delete(x,y) in O(log n) without having a direct pointer.  
IsCentric(x,y) just looks up (x,y) in the hash map and returns that node’s `isCentric` flag in O(1).

Use two synchronized balanced binary search trees (e.g., red–black trees): one ordered by left endpoint and one ordered by right endpoint.  
At every node of the “left-tree,” store, in addition to (L,R), the maximum right endpoint in its subtree; similarly, in the “right-tree,” store, in addition to (L,R), the minimum left endpoint in its subtree.  
Keep two global values: `R_min` = minimum of all right endpoints, and `L_max` = maximum of all left endpoints (these can be kept as the extremal keys of the right-tree and left-tree, respectively).  
A segment (x,y) is centric iff `x = L_max` and `y = R_min`; thus, IsCentric(x,y) can be answered in O(1) by comparing x,y with these two global values.  
Insert(x,y): insert (x,y) into both trees in O(log n), update subtree-augmented values, and update `L_max` and `R_min` if x or y becomes new extremal values.  
Delete(x,y): delete (x,y) from both trees by searching for the node with key x in the left-tree and key y in the right-tree in O(log n); update subtree-augmented values as usual.  
If the deleted segment had x = L_max or y = R_min, recompute the new extremal left or right endpoint from the corresponding tree in O(log n).  
The balanced trees ensure all updates and deletions run in worst-case O(log n), while checking centricity uses only the maintained global extremal endpoints and runs in O(1).

Store all segments in a global balanced binary search tree (e.g., a red-black tree) keyed by their length, and inside each node keep a secondary balanced tree of all segments having that length, keyed by (x,y).  
Maintain two pointers/fields in the global structure: `maxLen` and `secondMaxLen`, the largest and second-largest segment lengths currently present, updated in O(1) per insertion/deletion using subtree-size or count fields in the length-tree nodes.  
`Insert(x,y)` computes `L = y − x`, searches for `L` in the length-tree in O(log n), inserting if needed and then inserting (x,y) into the secondary tree in O(log n); update `maxLen` and `secondMaxLen` using a few comparisons and counts in O(1).  
`Delete(x,y)` computes `L = y − x`, finds node for `L` in O(log n), deletes (x,y) from its secondary tree in O(log n), and if that tree becomes empty deletes the length-node in O(log n); then update `maxLen` and `secondMaxLen` in O(1) by looking at neighboring length-nodes and stored counts.  
To support O(1) `IsCentric`, store with each segment a boolean field `isCentric` that is recomputed only for segments on the two currently longest-length levels whenever `maxLen` or `secondMaxLen` changes; since only O(1) length-nodes are affected per update, you can adjust these flags in O(1) amortized worst case by maintaining counts and identities of segments at those lengths.  
Define a segment to be centric exactly when its length is equal to `maxLen` (or equal to both `maxLen` and `secondMaxLen`, depending on the specific formal definition you adopt); then `IsCentric(x,y)` simply returns the stored `isCentric` flag of that segment in O(1).  
Since deletion is by coordinates, use the (x,y) key in the secondary tree to retrieve the segment record in O(log n) and access/update its `isCentric` flag when global maxima change.  
All structure navigation and updates are over balanced trees, giving O(log n) worst-case for `Insert` and `Delete`, while `IsCentric` is table-lookup of a precomputed flag, hence O(1).

Store segments in a balanced binary search tree (e.g., red-black tree) keyed by their center `c = (x + y)/2`, and for equal centers break ties by left endpoint.  
Augment each node with the segment’s endpoints `(x, y)` and a hash-map index from the pair `(x, y)` to its corresponding tree node to support deletion by coordinates.  
Maintain two global variables: `Lmax` = maximum right endpoint among all segments, and `Rmin` = minimum left endpoint among all segments, updating them on each insert/delete in `O(log n)` via additional balanced trees or heaps for left- and right-endpoints.  
Define a segment `[x, y]` to be centric if its center `c` satisfies `c = (Lmax + Rmin)/2`.  
To support `IsCentric(x,y)` in `O(1)`, compute `c = (x + y)/2` and compare with the maintained global value `C = (Lmax + Rmin)/2`.  
Insert(x,y): compute c, insert into the center-ordered tree and both endpoint structures, and update `Lmax`, `Rmin`, and `C` in `O(log n)`.  
Delete(x,y): use the hash-map to find the tree node in `O(1)`, delete it from the center-tree and both endpoint structures in `O(log n)`, then recompute/update `Lmax`, `Rmin`, and `C` in `O(log n)`.  
The search, insertion, and deletion in balanced trees guarantee worst-case `O(log n)` for updates, while the centricity check uses only stored globals and basic arithmetic for `O(1)` time.

Use a randomized treap keyed by segment length, augmented with a hash map from segment endpoints to the treap node.  

1. Store each segment s = (x, y) as a node with key = |x − y|, a random heap priority, and usual treap pointers (left, right).  
2. Maintain at each node only its subtree size (or no augmentation at all); the minimum-length segment is always the segment at the global treap root since keys are lengths.  
3. Keep a hash table H that maps the pair (x, y) to the corresponding treap node pointer.  
4. Insert(x, y): compute ℓ = |x − y|, create a new node, insert it into the treap by BST rules on key ℓ with heap rotations in expected O(log n), and store (x, y) → node in H.  
5. Delete(x, y): look up node = H[(x, y)] in expected O(1), delete that node from the treap in expected O(log n) using standard treap deletion (merge children appropriately), and remove (x, y) from H.  
6. Find-Min(): return the segment at the treap root in O(1).  
7. To build in linear time from n segments, first assign them priorities as a random permutation and sort them by length in linear expected time using randomized bucket or radix-sort (assuming lengths fit in word-size); then build the treap in linear time using the standard cartesian-tree-from-sorted-keys algorithm.

Store all segments in a randomized balanced binary search tree (e.g., treap) keyed by their length, and augment each node with subtree-size and subtree-min-length information.  
Because all segment lengths are distinct, length is a strict total order and each segment maps to a unique key.  
Each node stores: segment endpoints (x,y), its key = length = y−x, random priority, and the minimum length of any segment in its subtree (needed only for correctness checks; the root already gives the global min).  
Find-Min is then just returning the segment stored at the root, which is constant time.  
Insert(x,y): compute key = y−x, and insert a node with that key into the treap in expected O(log n); rotations preserve the heap property on priorities, so the root always has the smallest key with high probability.  
Delete(x,y): compute key = y−x and delete the node with that key from the treap in expected O(log n) using standard treap deletion by rotations.  
To build in linear expected time from n given segments, assign all priorities independently at random, sort segments by length in linear time using bucket/radix sort over a real-to-integer encoding (or assume word-RAM and bounded precision), then construct the treap in a single bottom-up pass using the known O(n) build algorithm for treaps from keys sorted by BST-order.  
This yields expected O(log n) time for Insert and Delete, O(1) for Find-Min, and O(n) expected preprocessing.

Store all segments in a treap (randomized balanced BST) keyed by length, with each node augmented by the minimum segment length in its subtree.  
Maintain also a hash table mapping each geometric segment (x,y) to its treap node (or to its key/length), so Delete can find the node in O(1) expected time.  
Insert(x,y): compute its length ℓ, insert a new node with key ℓ into the treap in expected O(log n), update subtree-min fields on the search path, and add (x,y)→node to the hash table.  
Delete(x,y): use the hash table to find its node/key in O(1) expected time, delete that node from the treap in expected O(log n), update subtree-min on the path, and remove (x,y) from the hash table.  
Find-Min(): keep a pointer to the treap node with global minimum key, updating it during Insert/Delete (using subtree-min fields), so this operation is O(1).  
All segment lengths are distinct, so keys are unique and comparisons are total.  
To build in linear time, first sort segments by length using counting/rank-based methods or assume pre-given order by length; then create treap nodes in that order with preassigned random priorities and build the treap via a single stack-based pass (like Cartesian tree construction), which is O(n); then fill the hash table in O(n).  
With these components, Insert and Delete run in expected O(log n), Find-Min runs in O(1), and construction is O(n).

Use a randomized balanced binary search tree (treap) keyed by segment length, augmented with subtree-size and subtree-min-length information.

1. Represent each segment by its endpoints and its (unique) length; the treap key is this length, so the minimum-length segment is always at the root if we store the heap priority to favor smaller lengths.  
2. To support `Find-Min()` in O(1), keep a direct pointer to the current root (whose key is the smallest length), or equivalently define the treap heap-order so that the smallest key has highest priority and is always at the root.  
3. To support `Insert(x,y)` in expected O(log n), compute the length ℓ = |y−x|, create a node with key ℓ, and perform a standard treap insertion (BST insert by key, then heap rotations by a random priority) that maintains the heap property with smallest key at root.  
4. To support `Delete(x,y)` in expected O(log n), compute ℓ, search the treap for key ℓ using BST search, and perform standard treap deletion (rotate node down until it becomes a leaf, then remove).  
5. The assumption of distinct segment lengths guarantees that each key ℓ is unique, so search and deletion by length are unambiguous.  
6. Expected O(log n) for insert/delete follows from standard treap analysis.  
7. For linear-time construction, assign random priorities to the n segments, sort them by priority (or simply take priorities in input order), and build the treap in linear time using the standard “Cartesian tree from sorted keys” method on the lengths.  
8. This yields a dynamic structure with expected O(log n) insert/delete, O(1) `Find-Min()`, and O(n) build time.

Use a hash table plus a randomized heap structure (e.g., a treap or skiplist ordered by segment length) with elements stored by unique ID.  

1. Assign each segment \((x,y)\) a unique ID (e.g., an incrementing integer or via global counter) and store it in a hash table keyed by the pair \((x,y)\) mapping to that ID and to the node pointer in the heap.  
2. Maintain a (randomized) heap-ordered search structure (treap/skiplist) keyed by segment length \(|y-x|\); store in each node: length, \((x,y)\), ID, and standard balancing metadata.  
3. Find-Min() is just returning the root’s segment, which is \(O(1)\).  
4. Insert\((x,y)\): compute length and ID, insert into hash table in expected \(O(1)\), then insert node into treap/skiplist by key = length in expected \(O(\log n)\).  
5. Delete\((x,y)\): use the hash table to look up the node pointer in expected \(O(1)\), then delete that node from the treap/skiplist in expected \(O(\log n)\).  
6. Distinct lengths guarantee unique keys, making the search structure simple.  
7. For linear-time construction: sort is not needed—build a *randomized* heap-like structure directly by inserting all segments with prechosen random priorities arranged in heap order (e.g., build a treap bottom-up using preassigned random priorities and linear-time Cartesian-tree construction by lengths).  
8. Construct the hash table in \(O(n)\) by inserting each of the \(n\) segments once, and build the treap/Cartesian tree in \(O(n)\) given all lengths and random priorities.

Use a randomized treap keyed by segment length, with each node storing one segment and its length as the key and a random priority.  
Because all segment lengths are distinct, this gives a valid BST over lengths and a heap over priorities simultaneously.  
Augment each node with a pointer to the node containing the segment of minimum length in its subtree (or simply keep the global root’s minimum as an external pointer), enabling O(1) Find-Min as the root’s (or global) min pointer.  
Insert(x,y): compute length ℓ = y−x, perform standard treap insertion by key ℓ in expected O(log n) time, and update subtree-min pointers on the insertion path in O(log n).  
Delete(x,y): compute ℓ = y−x, search for key ℓ in O(log n), then perform standard treap deletion/rotations and update subtree-min pointers along the affected path in O(log n).  
Find-Min(): return the segment stored in the node pointed to by the global minimum pointer in O(1).  
For linear-time construction, first compute all lengths and use a linear-time randomized heap-building process over all nodes’ priorities (e.g., build a Cartesian tree / treap from keys sorted by length, where the sorting order is already given or can be obtained in linear time if keys are from a bounded universe or word-RAM model with linear-time integer sort).  
Then do a single bottom-up pass to initialize subtree-min pointers in total O(n) time.

Use a randomized skip list keyed by segment length.  
Store each segment as a node whose key is its length |y−x|, and in the node also store (x,y).  
Maintain a single pointer to the minimum-length node (the first node in the bottom-level list) to support Find-Min() in O(1) time.  
Insert(x,y): compute ℓ = |y−x|, create a skip-list node with key ℓ, insert it by standard randomized skip-list insertion in expected O(log n), and update the min pointer if needed.  
Delete(x,y): compute ℓ = |y−x|, search in the skip list for key ℓ in expected O(log n), verifying that the stored segment is (x,y), then remove that node by standard skip-list deletion; if it was the min node, set min to its successor in the bottom list (or null if none).  
Distinct lengths guarantee that each length key ℓ corresponds to at most one segment, so searching by length suffices.  
To build in linear time, first sort the segments by length using counting-sort or radix-sort on a suitable discretization/bit-representation of lengths so sorting is O(n), then create the bottom-level list in increasing-length order and assign node heights using coin flips while linking higher levels in one left-to-right sweep, which overall is O(n) expected.  
The expected height and total number of forward pointers in a skip list is O(n), so both space usage and construction remain linear.
